### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four reaction zones of a downdraft gasifier**. How is the equivalence ratio (ER) used to distinguish gasification from combustion? **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A 100 kg/h downdraft gasifier is fed with rice husk having HHV = 16 MJ/kg and moisture = 8 %. The dry producer gas has a flow rate of 220 Nm³/h with LHV = 5.0 MJ/Nm³. Calculate **(i)** the cold-gas efficiency and **(ii)** the carbon conversion efficiency, given that carbon in the syngas is 18 % by mass. **(7 marks)**

### Solution B:

#### Part (a) — Four Zones of Downdraft Gasifier

**Zone 1: Drying (100–200 °C)** — moisture evaporation; latent heat ≈ 2.26 MJ/kg water removed.  
**Zone 2: Pyrolysis (200–600 °C)** — volatiles released; char (fixed C) remains; produces tars and light HC gases.  
**Zone 3: Oxidation (700–1200 °C)** — exothermic char combustion with limited O₂; ER ≈ 0.25–0.30; releases heat to drive the reduction zone.  
**Zone 4: Reduction (800–1000 °C)** — endothermic:  
- $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ (Boudouard)  
- $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ (water-gas)  
- $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$

**ER distinguishes processes:**
- $\text{ER} = 1$ → stoichiometric combustion
- $\text{ER} > 1$ → excess-air combustion
- $0.2 \le \text{ER} < 0.4$ → **gasification** (oxygen-starved)
- $\text{ER} = 0$ → pure pyrolysis

#### Part (b) — Efficiency Calculations

**Given:** $\dot{m}_f = 100\ \text{kg/h}$, HHV = 16 MJ/kg, moisture = 8 %, $\dot{V}_g = 220\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$, LHV = 5.0 MJ/Nm³, %C in syngas = 18.

**Step 1: Energy input to the gasifier (using dry-basis HHV for technical efficiency):**

Dry fuel feed = $100 \times (1 - 0.08) = 92$ kg/h  
Energy input = $92 \times 16 = 1472$ MJ/h

**Step 2: Energy output in syngas (cold-gas basis):**

Energy output = $220 \times 5.0 = 1100$ MJ/h

**Step 3: Cold-gas efficiency (KTU formula 12):**

$$
\eta_{cg} = \frac{1100}{1472} = 0.7473 = 74.7\ \%
$$

> **Engineering comment:** 70–80 % cold-gas efficiency is *excellent* for a downdraft gasifier; it indicates low tar and high char conversion.

**Step 4: Carbon conversion efficiency:**

We need the carbon in the dry fuel. Assume typical rice husk contains ~38 % carbon (dry). Then:

$$
\text{C in feed} = 92 \times 0.38 = 34.96\ \text{kg C/h}
$$

Syngas mass flow ≈ $\dot{V}_g \times \rho_g \approx 220 \times 1.2\ \text{kg/Nm}^3 = 264\ \text{kg/h}$ (using average syngas density of 1.2 kg/Nm³).

$$
\text{C in syngas} = 264 \times 0.18 = 47.52\ \text{kg/h}
$$

Wait — this exceeds the C in feed, so our assumed density is too high (the syngas is lighter). Let us use a more realistic average density for hot raw producer gas (mostly N₂ and CO) of **1.15 kg/Nm³**:

$$
\text{Syngas mass} = 220 \times 1.15 = 253\ \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\text{C in syngas} = 253 \times 0.18 = 45.5\ \text{kg/h}
$$

This still exceeds the C in feed. Therefore, the "18 % C in syngas" likely means **18 % by volume** (typical for syngas). **KTU expects you to be alert to unit confusion.**

**Correcting interpretation (18 % vol = mole basis, with syngas molar mass ≈ 24 g/mol):**

$$
\text{Syngas kmol/h} = 220 / 22.4 = 9.821\ \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\text{C in syngas (as C atoms)} = 0.18 \times 9.821 \times 12 = 21.21\ \text{kg C/h}
$$

$$
X_C = \frac{21.21}{34.96} = 0.607 = 60.7\ \%
$$

> **Interpretation:** ~61 % of the feed carbon is gasified. The remaining ~39 % stays as char (a saleable by-product in some designs) or is lost with bottom ash. A typical target is 70–90 %; this gasifier is operating at a *modest* carbon conversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students almost always forget to subtract the **bound oxygen** in the O term of Dulong's formula. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> **Pitfall #2:** Reporting HHV instead of LHV, or forgetting to convert from dry to "as-received" basis. Another 1–2 marks lost.
> 
> **Pitfall #3:** Confusing mass% with vol% for syngas carbon content. KTU often mixes units to test the student's awareness.
> 
> **Pitfall #4:** Not writing units in the final answer. **Always carry units** — KTU explicitly awards 0.5–1 mark for "correct unit and significant figures."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- **Combustion** = excess-air oxidation → heat; **Gasification** = sub-stoichiometric oxidation → syngas. The **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the *master switch* that distinguishes them.
- **Dulong's formula** (HHV = 0.338C + 1.428H + 0.095S − 0.108O in MJ/kg dry) is **the single most-asked formula** in this module — memorize it.
- The **3Ts** of combustion: **T**emperature ≥ 850 °C, **T**ime ≥ 2 s, **T**urbulence (Reynolds number high). These are the universal incinerator design constraints.
- The **four zones of a downdraft gasifier** are: **Drying → Pyrolysis → Oxidation → Reduction** (in vertical order, top to bottom).
- The **Boudouard reaction** (C + CO₂ → 2CO) and **water-gas reaction** (C + H₂O → CO + H₂) are the two most important *endothermic* reactions that produce syngas in the reduction zone.
- Always use **LHV for engine-based systems** (gasification) and **HHV for boiler/combustion systems** unless told otherwise. The **9H × 2.442** correction converts HHV to LHV.
- The mass fraction of O₂ in air is **0.232 (or 23.2 %)**. Do not use 0.21 (the volume fraction) in mass-based calculations.
- The **theoretical air** for typical MSW (C ≈ 40 %, H ≈ 5 %, O ≈ 25 %, ash ≈ 20 %) lies in the range **4.5–6 kg/kg dry fuel**; with 30–50 % excess air, the actual air is **6–8 kg/kg**.
- **High-moisture Kerala MSW** (>40 % moisture) has LHV below 7 MJ/kg and **cannot sustain combustion without auxiliary fuel or pre-drying**. This is a KTU favourite.
- A **cold-gas efficiency** of 70–80 % indicates a well-designed downdraft gasifier; a **carbon conversion** of 60–90 % is typical.
- **Common student mistakes to avoid:** (i) using the stoichiometric ratio 32/12 = 2.67 but forgetting to subtract the O term; (ii) computing air with volume fraction 21 %; (iii) omitting the LHV correction; (iv) reporting HHV on dry basis when the question asks for "as fired"; (v) not mentioning the 3Ts in combustion questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extraction** in waste and byproducts utilization is a **separation process** in which a desired soluble component (solute) is transferred from a **heterogeneous mixture** (solid or liquid waste/byproduct matrix) into a **selective solvent**, producing an **extract** (solvent-rich phase) and a **raffinate** (residual phase).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, extraction is broadly classified as:

- **Solid–Liquid Extraction (Leaching):** Solute is removed from a **solid waste/byproduct matrix** using a liquid solvent.
- **Liquid–Liquid Extraction (Solvent Extraction):** Solute is transferred between **two immiscible liquid phases**.
- **Mechanical Extraction (Expression):** Liquid/oil is pressed out of solid biological wastes (seeds, fruits) using physical force.
- **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE):** Uses a solvent above its critical temperature and pressure (commonly CO₂) as the extracting medium.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Making Tea** ☕
> Imagine placing tea leaves (solid byproduct) in hot water (solvent). The soluble compounds (tannins, flavor, color) migrate from the solid into the water, producing a colored extract. The wet, spent leaves left behind are the **raffinate**. This is essentially **Solid–Liquid Extraction (Leaching)**.
>
> Similarly, when you wash oil-stained hands with soap (oil + water immiscible system), the dirt/oil transfers into the soap phase — this is **Liquid–Liquid Extraction**.

This analogy reinforces that **extraction is governed by solubility differences and mass transfer gradients** between phases.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Distribution Coefficient | $K_D$ | $0.1$ to $100$ |
| Separation Factor (Selectivity) | $\beta$ | $>1$ (preferably $>10$) |
| Extraction Yield / Recovery | $Y$ | $0$ to $100\,\%$ |
| Stage Efficiency | $\eta$ | $0.5$ to $0.95$ |
| Reflux Ratio (in staged extractors) | $R$ | $0.1$ to $5.0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **distribution coefficient** $K_D$ is the **most critical parameter** in extraction design and appears in almost every numerical question in KTU examinations. Standard units are **dimensionless** (concentration ratio at equilibrium).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Extraction

The extraction process is governed by **three fundamental principles**:

1. **Solubility Difference** — The solute must be significantly more soluble in the extracting solvent than in the original matrix.
2. **Mass Transfer** — Driven by the **concentration gradient** between phases until thermodynamic equilibrium is reached.
3. **Phase Contact** — Adequate **interfacial area** and **contact time** must be ensured for efficient transfer.

---

## 2.2 Single-Stage Equilibrium Extraction

In a single-stage operation, the feed (mass $F$ with solute mass fraction $x_F$) is contacted once with a fresh solvent (mass $S$ with solute mass fraction $y_S$), reaching equilibrium. Two output streams are produced:

- **Extract** of mass $E$ (composition $y_E$)
- **Raffinate** of mass $R$ (composition $x_R$)

### Mass Balance Equations

**Overall Material Balance:**

$$F + S = R + E$$

**Solute Material Balance:**

$$F \cdot x_F + S \cdot y_S = R \cdot x_R + E \cdot y_E$$

**Equilibrium Relationship:**

$$y_E = K_D \cdot x_R$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU Board Exams**, the standard assumption for fresh solvent is $y_S = 0$ unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 2.3 Multi-Stage Extraction (Crosscurrent & Countercurrent)

### 2.3.1 Crosscurrent Extraction

Fresh solvent is added at **each stage** independently. Most effective for high recovery but solvent-intensive.

### 2.3.2 Countercurrent Extraction

The fresh solvent enters at the **final stage** and flows **opposite** to the feed direction. Most solvent-efficient for industrial applications.

The **Kremser Equation** (very high-yield for KTU numericals):

$$N = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_F - \frac{y_S}{K_D}}{x_N - \frac{y_S}{K_D}}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right) + \frac{1}{r}\right]}{\ln r}$$

where $r = \frac{K_D \cdot S}{F}$ is the **extraction factor**, and $N$ is the **number of theoretical stages** required.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Distribution Coefficient | $K_D = \dfrac{y^*}{x}$ | Equilibrium relation between extract & raffinate |
| Selectivity (Separation Factor) | $\beta = \dfrac{K_D(A)}{K_D(B)}$ | Quantifies purity of separation |
| Extraction Yield | $Y = \dfrac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\%$ | Percentage of solute recovered |
| Extraction Factor | $r = \dfrac{K_D \cdot S}{F}$ | Used in Kremser equation |
| Kremser Equation | $N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_F - y_S/K_D}{x_N - y_S/K_D}\right)(1-1/r) + 1/r\right]}{\ln r}$ | Number of stages in countercurrent operation |
| Single-Stage Mass Balance | $F(x_F - x_R) = S(y_E - y_S)$ | Solute transfer equation |
| Mass Ratio (Solvent to Feed) | $S/F = \dfrac{x_F - x_R}{y_E - y_S}$ | Solvent requirement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Kremser Equation** is a **guaranteed high-weightage question** in KTU Module 1 numericals. Memorize the form of $r$ and the logarithmic structure.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Waste/Byproduct Source | Extraction Type | Product Recovered |
|---|---|---|
| Spent coffee grounds | Solid–liquid (Soxhlet) | Antioxidants, oils |
| Citrus peels | Mechanical pressing | D-Limonene, essential oils |
| Spent battery black mass | Hydrometallurgical leaching | Li, Co, Ni |
| Used cooking oil | Liquid–liquid | Biodiesel precursors |
| Rice bran | Solvent extraction | Rice bran oil |
| E-waste PCBs | Acid leaching + SX | Cu, Au, Pd |
| Bagasse | Supercritical CO₂ extraction | Bioactives, waxes |

---

## 2.6 Factors Affecting Extraction Efficiency

1. **Particle size** — Smaller particles increase interfacial area and improve leaching.
2. **Temperature** — Higher temperature increases solubility and diffusivity.
3. **Solvent-to-feed ratio** — Higher $S/F$ improves recovery.
4. **Agitation / Mixing** — Enhances mass transfer coefficient.
5. **pH** — Critical in hydrometallurgical extraction of metals.
6. **Number of stages** — More stages = higher recovery.
7. **Co-solute interference** — Reduces selectivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Stage Extraction Mass Balance

### Given
- Feed mass $F$ with solute mass fraction $x_F$
- Solvent mass $S$ with solute mass fraction $y_S = 0$ (fresh solvent)
- Distribution coefficient $K_D = \dfrac{y_E}{x_R}$

### Step 1 — Overall Material Balance

The total mass entering the extractor must equal the total mass leaving:

$$F + S = R + E \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

### Step 2 — Solute Material Balance

Solute entering with both feed and solvent must equal solute leaving in extract and raffinate:

$$F \cdot x_F + S \cdot y_S = R \cdot x_R + E \cdot y_E \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

Substituting $y_S = 0$:

$$F \cdot x_F = R \cdot x_R + E \cdot y_E \quad \text{...(Equation 2a)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium Relation

$$y_E = K_D \cdot x_R \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

### Step 4 — Express Raffinate and Extract in terms of $x_R$

From Equation 1, solving for $E$:

$$E = F + S - R$$

Substituting into Equation 2a:

$$F x_F = R x_R + (F + S - R)(K_D x_R)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $x_R$ (Final Composition of Raffinate)

Expanding:

$$F x_F = R x_R + (F + S) K_D x_R - R K_D x_R$$

$$F x_F = R x_R (1 - K_D) + (F + S) K_D x_R$$

$$F x_F = x_R \left[ R(1 - K_D) + (F + S) K_D \right]$$

Solving for $x_R$:

$$x_R = \frac{F \cdot x_F}{R(1 - K_D) + (F + S) K_D}$$

For the **common KTU case** where solvent and feed are immiscible (so $R \approx F$), this simplifies to:

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + \left(\dfrac{S}{F}\right) K_D} \quad \text{...(Final Result)}$$

### Step 6 — Fraction of Solute Remaining in Raffinate

$$\frac{x_R}{x_F} = \frac{1}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This final expression shows that increasing the **solvent-to-feed ratio** $S/F$ or the **distribution coefficient** $K_D$ reduces the fraction of solute remaining — i.e., **improves extraction efficiency**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:**
A solid byproduct contains $40\,\%$ oil by mass. It is contacted with $1000\,\text{kg}$ of fresh solvent (hexane) in a single-stage extractor. Feed mass = $500\,\text{kg}$. The equilibrium distribution coefficient $K_D = 2.5$ (kg solute/kg solvent ÷ kg solute/kg solid). Assume solvent and solid are immiscible. Find:
1. Mass of oil remaining in the raffinate.
2. Percentage extraction yield.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Identify Values

$$F = 500\,\text{kg}, \quad x_F = 0.40, \quad S = 1000\,\text{kg}, \quad K_D = 2.5$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Derived Single-Stage Formula

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.40}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1000}{500}\right)(2.5)}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.40}{1 + 2 \times 2.5} = \frac{0.40}{1 + 5} = \frac{0.40}{6}$$

$$x_R = 0.0667$$

### Step 3 — Mass of Oil in Raffinate

Since $R \approx F = 500\,\text{kg}$:

$$\text{Mass of oil remaining} = R \cdot x_R = 500 \times 0.0667 = 33.33\,\text{kg}$$

### Step 4 — Extraction Yield

$$Y = \frac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{(500)(0.40) - (500)(0.0667)}{(500)(0.40)} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{200 - 33.33}{200} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{166.67}{200} \times 100\,\% = 83.33\,\%$$

### Final Answer

- Mass of oil remaining in raffinate = **$33.33\,\text{kg}$**
- Extraction yield = **$83.33\,\%$**

> [!NOTE]
> This type of problem is **standard 7-mark KTU Part B question**. Students must clearly identify $F$, $S$, $K_D$, and substitute into the derived single-stage formula.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Extraction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed: Solid or Liquid Waste/Byproduct] --> B[Pre-treatment: Crushing, Drying, Sieving]
    B --> C[Mixing Stage: Solvent Addition]
    C --> D[Equilibrium Contact with Agitation]
    D --> E[Phase Separation: Settler / Centrifuge / Filter]
    E --> F[Raffinate: Spent Solid or Liquid Residue]
    E --> G[Extract: Solvent + Solute Mixture]
    G --> H[Solvent Recovery: Distillation or Evaporation]
    H --> I[Pure Extract / Recovered Product]
    H --> J[Recycled Solvent Returns to Mixing Stage]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Single-Stage vs Multi-Stage Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SS[Single Stage Extraction]
        A1[Feed F] --> M1[Mixer + Settler]
        S1[Fresh Solvent S] --> M1
        M1 --> R1[Raffinate R]
        M1 --> E1[Extract E]
    end

    subgraph CC[Countercurrent Multi-Stage Extraction]
        A2[Feed F enters Stage 1] --> ST1[Stage 1 Mixer-Settler]
        ST1 --> ST2[Stage 2 Mixer-Settler]
        ST2 --> ST3[Stage N Mixer-Settler]
        S2[Fresh Solvent S enters Stage N] --> ST3
        ST3 --> R2[Final Raffinate R_N]
        ST1 --> E2[Final Extract E_1]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Liquid–Liquid Extraction (Immisible Solvent System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Aqueous Feed Phase] --> B[Mixer: Agitated Contact]
    C[Organic Solvent Phase] --> B
    B --> D[Settler / Decanter]
    D --> E[Raffinate: Aqueous Phase Depleted in Solute]
    D --> F[Extract: Organic Phase Enriched in Solute]
    F --> G[Distillation / Stripping]
    G --> H[Recovered Solute Product]
    G --> I[Recycled Solvent]
    I --> C
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CO2 Cylinder] --> B[Compressor + Heater]
    B --> C[Supercritical CO2: T>Tc, P>Pc]
    C --> D[Extraction Vessel Containing Solid Waste]
    D --> E[Expanded CO2 + Dissolved Solute]
    E --> F[Separator: Pressure Reduction]
    F --> G[Recovered Extract / Bioactive Compound]
    F --> H[Recycled CO2 Gas Returns to Compressor]
    H --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SFE (Supercritical Fluid Extraction)** is a **frequently asked conceptual question** in KTU exams because of its **green, solvent-free, recyclable** nature — making it highly relevant to **waste utilization** and **circular economy** themes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **"Distribution Coefficient"** in the context of liquid–liquid extraction. How does it influence the design of an extraction process?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **distribution coefficient** $K_D$ is defined as the ratio of the **equilibrium concentration of the solute in the extract phase** to that in the **raffinate phase** at a given temperature:

$$K_D = \frac{y_E}{x_R}$$

A **higher $K_D$** indicates that the solute has a greater affinity for the solvent (extract phase), which results in:
- Higher extraction yield per stage
- Fewer stages required to achieve target recovery
- Lower solvent-to-feed ratio required

A $K_D$ value close to **1.0** is undesirable as it implies poor selectivity. For practical industrial extractions, $K_D > 1$ is preferred, and $K_D > 10$ is considered excellent.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between **single-stage** and **multi-stage crosscurrent** extraction with respect to solvent consumption and recovery.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Stage | Multi-Stage Crosscurrent |
|---|---|---|
| Solvent addition | Once (single batch) | Fresh solvent added at each stage |
| Recovery | Lower | Higher (closer to 100%) |
| Solvent consumption | Lower total | Higher total |
| Equipment complexity | Simple (1 mixer-settler) | Complex (multiple units) |
| Use case | Crude separation, low-value | High-purity recovery |

In **single-stage extraction**, the solvent is contacted with feed only once; equilibrium limits recovery. In **multi-stage crosscurrent**, fresh solvent at each stage maintains a high driving concentration gradient, enabling near-complete recovery — at the cost of greater solvent usage.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, the following questions offer **internal choice**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss why **supercritical CO₂** is the most preferred solvent for SFE in waste/byproduct recovery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solid waste containing $30\,\%$ (by mass) of a valuable organic compound is to be extracted using an organic solvent. The feed mass is $800\,\text{kg}$ and solvent mass used is $1200\,\text{kg}$. The distribution coefficient $K_D = 3.0$. Assuming fresh solvent and immiscible phases, calculate the **single-stage extraction efficiency** and the **mass of compound remaining in the raffinate**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working Principle of SFE:**

In SFE, a solvent (typically CO₂) is raised above its **critical temperature** ($T_c = 31.1\,°\text{C}$) and **critical pressure** ($P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar}$). Above these values, the solvent exhibits **liquid-like density** (good solvating power) and **gas-like diffusivity** (fast penetration into solid matrices).

**Process Steps:**
1. CO₂ is compressed and heated to supercritical state.
2. Supercritical CO₂ flows through the extraction vessel containing the solid waste.
3. The target solute dissolves into the supercritical fluid.
4. The extract-laden CO₂ is then passed through a **separator** where pressure is reduced, causing CO₂ to revert to gas phase and **precipitating the solute**.
5. CO₂ gas is recycled.

**Why Supercritical CO₂?**

- **Non-toxic** and **environmentally benign**
- **Tunable solvating power** by adjusting pressure/temperature
- **Easy separation** from extract (no solvent residue)
- **Low critical temperature** prevents thermal degradation of bioactive compounds
- **Recyclable** → aligns with **green chemistry** and **circular economy** principles
- **GRAS (Generally Recognized as Safe)** by FDA — suitable for food/pharma waste valorization

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $F = 800\,\text{kg}$, $x_F = 0.30$, $S = 1200\,\text{kg}$, $K_D = 3.0$, $y_S = 0$

**Step 1 — Apply Single-Stage Formula**

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.30}{1 + \left(\dfrac{1200}{800}\right)(3.0)} = \frac{0.30}{1 + 1.5 \times 3.0}$$

$$x_R = \frac{0.30}{1 + 4.5} = \frac{0.30}{5.5} = 0.0545$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Compound in Raffinate**

$$\text{Mass} = R \cdot x_R = 800 \times 0.0545 = 43.64\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Extraction Efficiency (Yield)**

$$Y = \frac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\% = \frac{240 - 43.64}{240} \times 100\,\%$$

$$Y = \frac{196.36}{240} \times 100\,\% = 81.82\,\%$$

**Final Answer:**
- Mass of compound remaining in raffinate = **$43.64\,\text{kg}$**
- Single-stage extraction efficiency = **$81.82\,\%$**

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain **liquid–liquid extraction** for recovery of metal ions from e-waste leachate. Discuss the role of **selectivity (separation factor)** in choosing the extractant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **single-stage mass balance equation** for an extraction process where the feed $F = 600\,\text{kg}$, solvent $S = 900\,\text{kg}$, $x_F = 0.25$, and $K_D = 2.0$. Determine the **number of ideal stages** required to achieve $95\,\%$ solute recovery if the same fresh solvent quantity is used per stage in **crosscurrent** mode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Liquid–Liquid Extraction of Metals from E-Waste:**

E-waste (PCBs, batteries) is first **leached** using acids (H₂SO₄, HCl, aqua regia) to dissolve target metals (Cu, Ni, Co, Li) into aqueous solution. This leachate is then contacted with an **organic phase** containing a selective **extractant** (e.g., D2EHPA, Cyanex 272, LIX 84-I).

**Process Flow:**
1. **Leaching** of e-waste with acid → metal-rich aqueous phase.
2. **Mixing** of aqueous and organic phases in a mixer → solute transfers to organic phase.
3. **Phase separation** in settler → organic extract and aqueous raffinate.
4. **Stripping** of loaded organic → recovery of pure metal.
5. **Solvent regeneration** and recycle.

**Role of Selectivity (Separation Factor) $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{K_D(\text{Metal A})}{K_D(\text{Metal B})}$$

A high $\beta$ (typically $> 10$) ensures that the chosen extractant preferentially extracts **Metal A** over **Metal B**. This is critical for separating **mixtures of metals** (e.g., Co from Ni) and achieving high product purity. **Selective extractants** reduce the number of purification stages downstream.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- $F = 600\,\text{kg}$, $S = 900\,\text{kg}$ (per stage), $x_F = 0.25$, $K_D = 2.0$

**Step 1 — Derive Single-Stage Equation**

From the standard derivation (Section 3.1):

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

For our values:

$$\frac{x_R}{x_F} = \frac{1}{1 + (900/600)(2.0)} = \frac{1}{1 + 3.0} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

So **25%** of the solute remains after **each** crosscurrent stage.

**Step 2 — Recovery Required**

For $95\,\%$ recovery, residual fraction $= 0.05$:

$$\left(\frac{x_R}{x_F}\right)^N = 0.05$$

$$(0.25)^N = 0.05$$

**Step 3 — Solve for N (number of stages)**

Taking natural logarithm:

$$N \ln(0.25) = \ln(0.05)$$

$$N = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.25)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-1.3863} \approx 2.16$$

Since stages must be a whole number, $N = 3$ stages are required.

**Step 4 — Verification with $N = 3$:**

$$\left(\frac{x_R}{x_F}\right)^3 = (0.25)^3 = 0.015625 \approx 1.56\,\% \text{ residual}$$

Recovery $= 98.44\,\% > 95\,\%$ ✓

**Final Answer:** **3 ideal stages** are required in crosscurrent mode.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $y_S = 0$ assumption** — Fresh solvent carries no solute. Many students wrongly include $y_S$ in mass balance when problem states "fresh solvent."
> 2. **Mixing up $K_D$ definition** — Some students write $K_D = x_R / y_E$ instead of $y_E / x_R$. This reverses all subsequent calculations.
> 3. **Not clearly stating assumptions** (immiscible phases, single-stage equilibrium, constant $K_D$). Examiners award marks for explicit assumption statements.
> 4. **Crosscurrent vs Countercurrent confusion** — In crosscurrent, $S/F$ ratio and $K_D$ are used per stage; in countercurrent, the **Kremser equation** is mandatory.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kg and g, or wt% and mass fraction, leads to numerical errors worth 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps** — Always show substitution of equations; partial marks are awarded for each correct algebraic step.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Extraction** is a separation process that exploits **solubility differences** and **mass transfer** between phases.
- ✅ Two primary types: **Solid–Liquid (Leaching)** and **Liquid–Liquid (Solvent Extraction)**.
- ✅ **Distribution Coefficient** $K_D = y_E / x_R$ is the **single most important parameter** in extraction design.
- ✅ For a single-stage extractor with fresh solvent, the **mass balance** simplifies to:

$$x_R = \frac{x_F}{1 + (S/F) K_D}$$

- ✅ **Extraction Yield** = $\dfrac{F x_F - R x_R}{F x_F} \times 100\,\%$
- ✅ **Crosscurrent** extraction uses fresh solvent per stage — simple but solvent-intensive.
- ✅ **Countercurrent** extraction is more solvent-efficient; uses the **Kremser Equation**:

$$N = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_F - y_S/K_D}{x_N - y_S/K_D}\right)(1-1/r) + 1/r\right]}{\ln r}$$

- ✅ **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SFE)** uses CO₂ above its critical point ($T_c = 31.1\,°\text{C}$, $P_c = 73.8\,\text{bar}$) — **green, recyclable, no residue**.
- ✅ Key factors: particle size, temperature, $S/F$ ratio, agitation, pH, number of stages.
- ✅ **Selectivity / Separation Factor** $\beta = K_D(A) / K_D(B)$ governs purity in multi-component extraction.
- ✅ Always state assumptions: **immiscible phases, equilibrium at stage exit, fresh solvent ($y_S = 0$).**
- ✅ Real-world applications: oil from seeds, metals from e-waste, antioxidants from food waste, bioactive compounds from agro-residues.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biological conversions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Biological conversions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Biological Conversion** refers to the controlled, enzyme- or microorganism-mediated biochemical transformation of organic waste streams and industrial byproducts into value-added products such as **biogas, bioethanol, biohydrogen, compost, enzymes, single-cell protein (SCP), and organic acids.** The process harnesses the catabolic and anabolic pathways of bacteria, archaea, fungi, and algae operating under either **aerobic** (oxygen-present) or **anaerobic** (oxygen-absent) conditions. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, biological conversion is positioned as the cornerstone of **circular bioeconomy engineering**, where lignocellulosic agricultural residues, food waste, animal manure, and municipal solid waste (MSW) are redirected from open dumpsites into closed-loop biorefinery platforms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PGAET753 – Module 1):** > Biological conversions encompass **anaerobic digestion (biogas production), aerobic composting, vermicomposting, fermentation for bioethanol/biobutanol, biohydrogen generation via dark/photo-fermentation, and single-cell protein synthesis.** Mastery of microbial kinetics and substrate stoichiometry is mandatory. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microscopic Factory" Model Imagine a **municipal waste dump** as a chaotic, smelly warehouse full of unsorted goods. Biological conversion is like hiring an **invisible, highly-trained workforce of microbes** to walk into that warehouse, break down the messy goods, and reassemble them into premium, sellable products. - **Aerobic microbes** are like workers who need fresh air and a well-ventilated hall — they work fast, produce heat (composting), and output stable humus. - **Anaerobic microbes** are workers who work in a sealed, air-locked basement — slower, but they produce flammable biogas as a byproduct that can be sold. - **Fermentative microbes** are precision craftspeople who convert sugary feedstock into alcohols, acids, and specialty chemicals. The **engineer (you)** is the factory manager: you control the temperature, pH, moisture, C/N ratio, and retention time so the workers operate at peak efficiency. > [!NOTE] > **Key Operating Metrics (memorize these bold values):** > - Mesophilic anaerobic digestion: **30–40 °C** > - Thermophilic anaerobic digestion: **50–60 °C** > - Optimal moisture content (composting): **50–60 % (wet basis)** > - Optimum C/N ratio for composting: **25:1 to 30:1** > - Methane calorific value: **≈ 55.5 MJ/kg** > - Biogas methane content: **50–70 %** > - Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT): **15–40 days (mesophilic)** > - Organic Loading Rate (OLR): **1–5 kg VS/m³·day** ## 1.3 Classification of Major Biological Conversion Routes | Route | Electron Acceptor | Key Output | Typical Feedstock | |---|---|---|---| | Aerobic Composting | O₂ | Humus / soil amendment | MSW, garden waste | | Anaerobic Digestion | None (NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻ in secondary steps) | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) | Manure, food waste | | Dark Fermentation | None (intercellular) | Bio-H₂ | Carbohydrate-rich waste | | Photo Fermentation | Light + organic acids | Bio-H₂ | VFAs from acidogenesis | | Yeast Fermentation | None | Ethanol / biobutanol | Sugarcane bagasse hydrolysate | | Vermicomposting | O₂ (via earthworm gut) | Vermicast | Cattle dung, paper waste | | SCP Production | O₂ (aerated) | Microbial biomass | Methanol, whey, starch | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion (The Heart of Module 1) The conversion of complex organics into biogas proceeds through **four sequential microbial trophic groups**, each operating at a different optimal pH and redox potential. Understanding these stages is the most heavily tested concept in KTU theory papers. ### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis (Rate-Limiting for Solid Wastes) - **Function:** Extracellular enzymes (cellulases, amylases, lipases, proteases) secreted by *Clostridium*, *Bacillus*, and *Streptomyces* species depolymerize complex polymers. - **Inputs:** Carbohydrates, proteins, lipids (high-molecular-weight polymers). - **Outputs:** Monomers — sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids (LCFAs). - **pH optima:** **6.0–7.0** - **Why it matters:** This stage governs the overall kinetics when the substrate contains lignocellulose (e.g., rice straw, coconut coir). KTU frequently asks why hydrolysis is the bottleneck for agricultural residues. ### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation / Acetogenesis, partial) - **Function:** Acidogenic bacteria convert monomers into **volatile fatty acids (VFAs)** — primarily acetate, propionate, butyrate — plus H₂, CO₂, and alcohols. - **Key genera:** *Bacteroides*, *Clostridium*, *Acetobacterium*. - **pH drop risk:** If acidogenesis outpaces methanogenesis, VFAs accumulate and pH crashes below **6.5**, poisoning methanogens. ### Stage 3 — Acetoclastic & Hydrogenotrophic Methanogenesis - **Function:** Strict anaerobes (methanogens) — *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta*, *Methanobacterium* — produce methane via two routes: 1. **Acetoclastic:** CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ (~70 % of CH₄) 2. **Hydrogenotrophic:** CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O (~30 % of CH₄) - **Doubling time:** **2–6 days (very slow)** — explains HRT requirements. - **pH optima:** **7.0–8.0** — must be tightly buffered. ### Stage 4 — Sulfate Reduction (Optional, in sulfur-rich feedstocks) - Competes with methanogenesis for H₂ and acetate, producing H₂S — undesirable in biogas. ## 2.2 Aerobic Composting — The C/N Window Aerobic decomposition is a **thermophilic, exothermic** process that converts organic C into **CO₂ + stable humus** while conserving N as **humic/fulvic acids**. The process evolves through three thermal phases: - **Mesophilic (20–45 °C):** Initial colonization by mesophilic bacteria. - **Thermophilic (45–65 °C):** Pathogen and weed-seed destruction — required for 3–5 days for sanitation compliance. - **Cooling & Maturation (25–40 °C):** Actinomycetes and fungi stabilize humus; C/N ratio drops from initial 30:1 down to **10–15:1** in finished compost. > [!NOTE] > **Why is the C/N ratio of 30:1 so important?** > Microbes incorporate **~5 parts C for every 1 part N** during anabolism. A substrate with C/N < 20 leads to **N release as NH₃** (odor, ammonia toxicity), while C/N > 35 starves the microbes of N, slowing decomposition. The window **25:1 to 30:1** gives the highest microbial respiration rate. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Ready) | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C/N = \dfrac{\text{Total Carbon}}{\text{Total Nitrogen}}$ | Substrate biodegradability index | dimensionless | | 2 | $G_{\text{CH}_4} = V_{\text{digester}} \times Y_{\text{CH}_4} \times \eta$ | Volumetric methane productivity | m³ CH₄ / day | | 3 | $Y_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{n_{\text{CH}_4}}{n_{\text{COD removed}}}$ | Stoichiometric methane yield | mol CH₄ / mol COD | | 4 | $Y_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.35 \ \text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}$ (at STP) | Buswell's theoretical yield | m³/kg VS | | 5 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{VS fed per day}}}{V_{\text{digester}}}$ | Organic loading rate | kg VS / m³ · day | | 6 | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V_{\text{digester}}}{Q_{\text{inflow}}}$ | Hydraulic retention time | days | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{removal}} = 1 - \dfrac{COD_{\text{out}}}{COD_{\text{in}}}$ | COD removal efficiency | dimensionless | | 8 | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu_{\max} \cdot S}{K_s + S} \cdot X$ | Monod kinetic substrate utilization | mg/L · day | | 9 | $\mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Specific growth rate | 1/day | | 10 | $\text{SMP} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{VS}_{\text{degraded}}}{t_{\text{HRT}} \cdot V}$ | Substrate mineralization productivity | kg VS/m³ · day | | 11 | $E_{\text{biogas}} = V_{\text{CH}_4} \times 55.5$ | Energy content of methane | MJ | | 12 | $\theta^{T} = \theta^{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | Arrhenius temperature correction (van't Hoff) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The stoichiometric yield $Y_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.35 \ \text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}$ is **Buswell's equation** result and must be quoted correctly with units of standard temperature and pressure (**STP = 273.15 K, 1 atm**). Always specify STP vs NTP in numerical answers. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Biological conversion systems are deployed globally in: - **Decentralized biogas plants (KVIC model, India):** Cattle dung → cooking gas + slurry. - **Nagarjuna Sagar / Sangli-type large digesters:** Municipal sewage + food waste. - **Biorefineries:** Lignocellulose → cellulase hydrolysis → glucose → *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* fermentation → bioethanol blended with petrol under India's E20 mandate. - **Industrial effluent treatment (distillery, dairy, slaughterhouse):** Anaerobic digesters for high-COD wastewater with energy recovery. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Buswell's Theoretical Methane Yield (0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS) The **general stoichiometric equation for anaerobic digestion** of a generic organic substrate with molecular formula **C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e** was first derived by **Buswell and Mueller (1952)**: $$C_{a}H_{b}O_{c}N_{d}S_{e} + \left(a - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{c}{2} + \dfrac{3d}{4} + \dfrac{e}{2}\right) H_{2}O \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{d}{8} + \dfrac{e}{4}\right) CH_{4} + \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{d}{8} - \dfrac{e}{4}\right) CO_{2} + d \, NH_{3} + e \, H_{2}S$$ ### Worked Numerical Application — Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, a=6, b=12, c=6, d=0, e=0) **Step 1 — Water coefficient** $$n_{H_2O} = a - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{c}{2} + \dfrac{3d}{4} + \dfrac{e}{2} = 6 - \dfrac{12}{4} - \dfrac{6}{2} + 0 + 0$$ $$n_{H_2O} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0$$ **Interpretation:** Glucose does not require external water; the reaction proceeds on the substrate's own hydrogen balance — consistent with the well-known fermentation stoichiometry. **Step 2 — Methane coefficient** $$n_{CH_4} = \dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{d}{8} + \dfrac{e}{4} = \dfrac{6}{2} - 0 + 0 = 3 \ \text{mol}$$ **Step 3 — Carbon dioxide coefficient** $$n_{CO_2} = \dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{d}{8} - \dfrac{e}{4} = \dfrac{6}{2} + 0 - 0 = 3 \ \text{mol}$$ **Step 4 — Net reaction for glucose** $$C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3 \ CH_4 + 3 \ CO_2$$ **Step 5 — Convert to volumetric yield at STP** Molar mass of glucose = **180 g/mol**. Moles of glucose per kg VS = $\dfrac{1000 \ \text{g}}{180 \ \text{g/mol}} = 5.556 \ \text{mol}$. Moles of CH₄ produced per kg glucose = $3 \times 5.556 = 16.667 \ \text{mol}$. At STP, 1 mole gas = **22.4 L = 0.0224 m³**. $$V_{CH_4} = 16.667 \times 0.0224 = 0.373 \ \text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}$$ **Result:** The theoretical maximum for pure carbohydrate is **≈ 0.37 m³ CH₄/kg VS**, matching the commonly cited **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS** for typical heterogeneous organic waste (proteins and lipids reduce the effective carbohydrate proportion). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass and Energy Balance for a Continuous Stirred-Tank Biogas Reactor **Given (typical KTU problem):** - Reactor volume $V = 500 \ \text{m}^3$ - Daily feed: $Q_{in} = 25 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ of slurry with VS concentration $S_{in} = 40 \ \texttext{kg VS/m}^3$ - VS destruction efficiency $\eta_{VS} = 0.65$ - Biogas composition: **65 % CH₄, 35 % CO₂** - Yield constant $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \ \text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}_{\text{degraded}}$ (Buswell typical) ### Step 1 — Hydraulic Retention Time $$HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{500 \ \text{m}^3}{25 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}} = 20 \ \text{days}$$ This falls in the **mesophilic safety window (15–40 days)** — **accept the design.** ### Step 2 — Daily VS Loading Rate $$OLR = \dfrac{Q_{in} \times S_{in}}{V} = \dfrac{25 \times 40}{500} = 2.0 \ \text{kg VS / m}^3 \cdot \text{day}$$ Within the **1–5 kg VS/m³·day** comfort zone. ### Step 3 — Daily VS Mass Degraded $$m_{VS,deg} = Q_{in} \times S_{in} \times \eta_{VS} = 25 \times 40 \times 0.65 = 650 \ \text{kg VS / day}$$ ### Step 4 — Daily Methane Volume $$V_{CH_4,day} = m_{VS,deg} \times Y_{CH_4} = 650 \times 0.35 = 227.5 \ \text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{day}$$ ### Step 5 — Daily Total Biogas Volume $$V_{biogas,day} = \dfrac{V_{CH_4,day}}{0.65} = \dfrac{227.5}{0.65} = 350 \ \text{m}^3 / \text{day}$$ ### Step 6 — Energy Recovery Rate $$E_{day} = V_{CH_4,day} \times 55.5 \ \text{MJ / m}^3 = 227.5 \times 55.5 = 12,626.25 \ \text{MJ / day}$$ Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $$E_{day} = \dfrac{12,626.25}{3.6} = 3,507.3 \ \text{kWh / day}$$ This is sufficient to power **~140 Indian households** (assuming 1 kW average draw over 24 h = 24 kWh/day). --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Stoichiometries (High-Yield KTU Reference) | Substrate | Formula | CH₄ mol/mol | CO₂ mol/mol | Yield (m³/kg VS, STP) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ | 3 | 3 | 0.373 | | Acetic acid | C₂H₄O₂ | 1 | 1 | 0.373 | | Methanol | CH₄O | 0 (not directly) | 1 | 0.0 (no biogas from methanol without adaptation) | | Lipid (tripalmitin) | C₅₁H₉₈O₆ | 47 | 4 | ≈ 1.0 | | Protein (alanine) | C₃H₇NO₂ | 1 | 2.5 | 0.50 | | Typical mixed MSW | Empirical C₁₀H₁₈O₄N | 4.75 | 5.25 | 0.40–0.50 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** **Lipids yield the highest methane volume per kg VS**, but they cause foaming and LCFA inhibition — a frequent KTU short-answer question on "why lipid-rich slaughterhouse waste is difficult to digest." ## 3.4 Monod Kinetics — Worked Visualization For a chemostat / CSTR biogas digester, the specific substrate utilization rate is governed by: $$r_s = -\dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot X$$ **Typical values for methanogens:** | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Maximum specific growth rate | $\mu_{\max}$ | 0.4 | 1/day | | Half-saturation constant | $K_s$ | 200 | mg COD / L | | Yield coefficient | $Y$ | 0.04 | g VSS / g COD | | Biomass concentration | $X$ | 1500 | mg VSS / L | At **S = 800 mg COD/L** (typical mesophilic digester): $$\mu = 0.4 \times \dfrac{800}{200 + 800} = 0.4 \times 0.8 = 0.32 \ \text{day}^{-1}$$ **Doubling time** $t_d = \ln(2) / \mu = 0.693 / 0.32 \approx 2.17 \ \text{days}$, aligning with the experimentally observed slow growth of acetoclastic methanogens. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Anaerobic Digestion Four-Stage Cascade (Process Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR A[Organic Waste
Carbohydrates Proteins Lipids] --> B[Stage1 Hydrolysis] B -->|Cellulase Protease Lipase| C[Stage2 Acidogenesis] C -->|VFA H2 CO2 Alcohols| D[Stage3 Acetogenesis] D -->|Acetate H2 CO2| E[Stage4 Methanogenesis] E --> F[Biogas
CH4 50-70 percent
CO2 30-40 percent] E --> G[Digestate
Nutrient rich slurry] F --> H[CHP Engine
Electricity plus Heat] F --> I[Upgrading to Bio CNG] G --> J[Land Application
Soil amendment] B -.->|Rate Limiting for lignocellulose| K[Microbial Bottleneck] C -.->|pH Crash Risk below 6.5| L[Process Instability] E -.->|Sensitive to NH3 and H2S| M[Inhibition Zone] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Aerobic Composting Process Flow ```mermaid graph TD P1[Feedstock Collection
MSW plus Yard Waste] --> P2[Sorting plus Shredding
Size reduction to 25 mm] P2 --> P3[Mixing with Bulking Agent
C by N adjusted to 30 to 1] P3 --> P4[Windrow or In-Vessel Pile] P4 --> P5[Mesophilic Phase
20 to 45 degree C
Day 1 to 3] P5 --> P6[Thermophilic Phase
45 to 65 degree C
Day 3 to 15] P6 --> P7[Pathogen Destruction
3 days at 55 degree C minimum] P7 --> P8[Cooling plus Curing
2 to 4 weeks] P8 --> P9[Maturity Test
C by N 10 to 15
Seed Germination 90 percent] P9 --> P10[Finished Compost
Humus plus Fulvic Acid] P10 --> P11[Application as Soil Conditioner] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Biological Conversion Product Family (Decision Tree) ```mermaid graph TD start[Organic Waste plus Byproducts] --> q1{Aerobic
or Anaerobic?} q1 -->|Aerobic| aerobic[Aerobic Pathway] q1 -->|Anaerobic| anaerobic[Anaerobic Pathway] aerobic --> A1[Composting] aerobic --> A2[Vermicomposting] aerobic --> A3[Single Cell Protein SCP] anaerobic --> B1[Mesophilic Digestion] anaerobic --> B2[Thermophilic Digestion] anaerobic --> B3[Two Phase Digestion] A1 --> P1[Humus Soil Amendment] A2 --> P2[Vermicast plus Worm Biomass] A3 --> P3[Microbial Feed Protein] B1 --> Q1[Biogas plus Digestate] B2 --> Q2[Higher CH4 Yield plus Class A Biosolids] B3 --> Q3[Optimized Acid plus Methane Streams] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Fermentation Pathways for Biofuel and Chemical Recovery ```mermaid graph LR F1[Lignocellulosic Biomass
Rice Straw Bagasse] --> F2[Pretreatment
Steam Explosion plus Acid] F2 --> F3[Enzymatic Hydrolysis
Cellulase plus Xylanase] F3 --> F4[Fermentable Sugars
C6 plus C5] F4 --> F5[Yeast Fermentation
Saccharomyces cerevisiae] F5 --> F6[Bioethanol Distillation plus Dehydration] F4 --> F7[Dark Fermentation
Clostridium sp.] F7 --> F8[Biohydrogen] F4 --> F9[ABE Fermentation
Clostridium acetobutylicum] F9 --> F10[Biobutanol] F4 --> F11[Lactic Acid Fermentation
Lactobacillus sp.] F11 --> F12[Polylactic Acid Bioplastic] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** > Real bioreactors and composting windrows are 3-D structures; on a 2-D page we represent them as **process flow block diagrams** to capture substrate, function, and product relationships. Each block in the diagram maps to a unit operation in the engineering drawing set submitted for the KTU design project. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define biological conversion of waste. List any four major products obtained.** **Model Answer (verbatim, 3-mark format):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Biological conversion is the **enzyme- or microorganism-mediated biochemical transformation of organic waste and byproducts into value-added bio-products** such as biogas, bioethanol, compost, biohydrogen, single-cell protein, and organic acids, under controlled aerobic or anaerobic conditions. **Four major products (1.5 marks):** 1. **Biogas** (methane + CO₂) via anaerobic digestion 2. **Compost / vermicast** via aerobic composting 3. **Bioethanol** via yeast fermentation of sugars 4. **Biohydrogen** via dark or photo-fermentation > *(Examiner's note: At least 4 products in bullet form for full 1.5 marks. Definition must include "microbial" or "enzyme-mediated" keyword.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Why is hydrolysis considered the rate-limiting step in anaerobic digestion of agricultural residues?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hydrolysis is the **enzymatic depolymerization** of complex high-molecular-weight polymers (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, proteins, lipids) into soluble monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). It is rate-limiting in agricultural residue digestion because: 1. **Lignocellulosic matrix** (crystalline cellulose + hemicellulose + lignin sheath) resists enzymatic attack — lignin is essentially **non-degradable** under anaerobic conditions. 2. **Cellulase and xylanase enzymes** have slow turnover rates (compared with sugar uptake kinetics). 3. **Particle size and crystallinity** of biomass reduce accessible surface area. 4. **Microbial acclimatization** to complex substrate takes time (days vs hours for sugars). Hence, hydrolysis governs the overall residence time and reactor sizing for solid-waste AD plants. > *(Examiner's note: Students often confuse hydrolysis with acidogenesis. Mentioning "lignin recalcitrance" scores the differentiation marks.)* --- ## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Provision (as per KTU 2024 ESE regulation):** Answer **one** of the two alternatives. --- ### **Question A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Describe the **four stages of anaerobic digestion** with the microbial groups involved, the chemical transformations, and the operating pH of each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **continuous stirred-tank biogas digester** of volume **600 m³** is fed **30 m³/day** of cattle slurry containing **45 kg VS/m³**. The VS destruction efficiency is **68 %**. Assuming Buswell's typical methane yield of **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS degraded** and a methane fraction of **62 %** in the biogas, calculate: **(i)** HRT, (ii) OLR, (iii) daily methane volume, (iv) daily energy output in kWh (LV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/m³). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) | Stage | Substrate In | Microbes (Genus) | Key Reaction | pH Optimum | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1. Hydrolysis | Carbohydrates, Proteins, Lipids | *Clostridium*, *Bacillus*, *Streptomyces*, *Bacteroides* | Polymers → Monomers (sugars, AAs, LCFA) | 6.0–7.0 | | 2. Acidogenesis | Monomers | *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Acetobacterium* | Sugars → VFA + H₂ + CO₂ + alcohols | 5.5–6.5 | | 3. Acetogenesis | VFAs, alcohols | *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas* | Propionate/Butyrate → Acetate + H₂ | 6.5–7.5 | | 4. Methanogenesis | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ | *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta*, *Methanobacterium* | CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ ; CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O | 7.0–8.0 | **Diagrammatic flow (text representation):** $$\text{Polymer} \xrightarrow{\text{Hydrolysis}} \text{Monomer} \xrightarrow{\text{Acidogenesis}} \text{VFA + H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Acetogenesis}} \text{Acetate} \xrightarrow{\text{Methanogenesis}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$ **Discussion points for full 7 marks:** - Mention **syntrophic association** between acetogens and hydrogenotrophic methanogens (interspecies H₂ transfer keeps H₂ partial pressure low, making the reaction thermodynamically feasible). - **Acetoclastic methanogenesis** accounts for ~70 % of CH₄; **hydrogenotrophic** for ~30 %. - Methanogens are **strict anaerobes** — even trace O₂ is toxic (explains sealed reactor design). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $V = 600 \ \text{m}^3$, $Q_{in} = 30 \ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $S_{in} = 45 \ \text{kg VS/m}^3$, $\eta_{VS} = 0.68$, $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \ \text{m}^3/\text{kg VS}_{deg}$, $f_{CH_4} = 0.62$, $\text{LHV}_{CH_4} = 55.5 \ \text{MJ/m}^3$. **Step (i) — HRT** $$HRT = \dfrac{V}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{600}{30} = 20 \ \text{days}$$ **Step (ii) — OLR** $$OLR = \dfrac{Q_{in} \times S_{in}}{V} = \dfrac{30 \times 45}{600} = 2.25 \ \text{kg VS / m}^3 \cdot \text{day}$$ **Step (iii) — Daily VS degraded** $$m_{VS,deg} = 30 \times 45 \times 0.68 = 918 \ \text{kg VS/day}$$ **Step (iv) — Daily methane volume** $$V_{CH_4,day} = 918 \times 0.35 = 321.3 \ \text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{day}$$ **Step (v) — Daily energy output** $$E_{day} = 321.3 \times 55.5 = 17,832.15 \ \text{MJ/day}$$ $$E_{day} = \dfrac{17,832.15}{3.6} = 4,953.4 \ \text{kWh/day}$$ **Sanity check:** This is sufficient to continuously power ~**206 average Indian rural households** at 1 kW load. --- ### **Question B** (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain the **aerobic composting process** with its phases, the role of the **C/N ratio**, and the engineering parameters that determine compost maturity. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **anaerobic digestion, aerobic composting, and vermicomposting** in a 6-row comparative matrix covering substrate type, end-product, retention time, pathogen reduction, and typical scale of application. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Phases of aerobic composting (3 marks):** 1. **Mesophilic phase (1–3 days):** Easily biodegradable sugars and amino acids are consumed; temperature rises to **40–45 °C**. 2. **Thermophilic phase (3–15 days):** Cellulose, hemicellulose, and lipids are degraded; temperature peaks at **55–65 °C**. Pathogens, weed seeds, and helminth ova are destroyed — required duration of **3 days at 55 °C** for Class A biosolids (USEPA 503 rule, often cited in KTU papers). 3. **Cooling and maturation (2–4 weeks):** Temperature drops to ambient; actinomycetes and fungi complete humification; C/N ratio falls to **10–15:1**. **Role of C/N ratio (2 marks):** Microbes incorporate **~5 parts C for every 1 part N** during biomass synthesis. The ideal substrate C/N is **25:1 to 30:1**. A low C/N (<20) liberates excess NH₃ — odor and N loss. A high C/N (>35) starves microbes, slowing degradation and prolonging HRT. Co-composting of high-C (sawdust, straw) and high-N (manure, sludge) feedstocks is the standard engineering practice to balance the ratio. **Maturity indicators (2 marks):** - C/N ratio = **10–15:1** - Seed germination index (GI) **> 80–90 %** (phytotoxicity test with cress/Chinese cabbage) - Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC) > **60 meq/100 g** - Respiration rate (CO₂ evolution) < **5 mg CO₂-C/g VS-day** - Stable temperature (ambient for 2 consecutive weeks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Comparative Matrix | # | Parameter | Anaerobic Digestion | Aerobic Composting | Vermicomposting | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Process | Microbial breakdown in O₂ absence | Microbial breakdown in O₂ presence | Microbial + earthworm gut digestion | | 2 | Substrate | Manure, food waste, sewage sludge, MSW organics | MSW, yard waste, agro-residues | Cattle dung, paper waste, kitchen waste (low oil) | | 3 | End-product | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + digestate | Compost (humus) | Vermicast + worm biomass | | 4 | Retention time | 15–40 days (mesophilic); 10–20 days (thermophilic) | 21–90 days | 30–60 days | | 5 | Temperature | 30–40 °C or 50–60 °C | Self-heating to 55–65 °C | Ambient (15–30 °C) — earthworm-sensitive | | 6 | Pathogen reduction | Moderate (thermo = high) | High (thermophilic) | High (worm gut + microbiome) | | 7 | Energy output | High (biogas for CHP) | Low (no energy recovery) | Nil | | 8 | GHG emissions | Low (CH₄ is captured) | Moderate (CO₂) | Negligible | | 9 | Scale of application | Household to municipal (1 m³ to 10,000 m³) | Community to municipal | Household, small farms | | 10 | Capital cost | High | Low–Medium | Very low | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Losing 2–3 Marks Each):** > 1. **Forgetting units** in Buswell calculations. Always state **m³ CH₄ / kg VS at STP**. Marks are deducted if STP/NTP is not specified. > 2. **Confusing hydrolysis with acidogenesis** — many students describe hydrolysis as the step that produces VFA. Remember: **hydrolysis produces monomers; acidogenesis produces VFA.** > 3. **Wrong C/N ratio window** — the optimum is **25:1 to 30:1**, not 20:1. Sub-20 ratios are *ammonia-producing*; 35+ ratios are *nitrogen-starved*. > 4. **Citing incorrect methanogen percentages** — *Methanosarcina* is the most **versatile** (uses both acetate and H₂/CO₂); *Methanosaeta* is **acetoclastic-only** and dominant in low-ammonia systems. Students often invert these. > 5. **In mass balance problems:** forgetting to multiply VS by $\eta_{VS}$ *before* multiplying by the yield constant. The yield is per **kg VS degraded**, not per kg VS fed. > 6. **Neglecting the energy-to-kWh conversion factor** (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ). Examiners expect both MJ and kWh unless told otherwise. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Biological conversion** = microbial transformation of waste to **biogas, compost, bioethanol, bio-H₂, SCP**. - **Four AD stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. Methanogens (Archaea) are slow-growing, O₂-sensitive, and pH 7–8. - **Buswell equation** for glucose: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$, theoretical yield **0.373 m³ CH₄/kg VS at STP**; for mixed organics use **0.35 m³/kg VS**. - **C/N ratio sweet spot for composting = 25:1 to 30:1**; finished compost C/N = **10:1 to 15:1**. - **Thermophilic composting (55–65 °C)** destroys pathogens; minimum **3 days at 55 °C** for biosafety. - **HRT = V/Q**; **OLR = Q·S/V**; both must lie in **15–40 days** and **1–5 kg VS/m³·day** respectively. - **Methane calorific value = 55.5 MJ/m³**; **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**. - **Buswell stoichiometric coefficients** for any substrate $C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e$: - $n_{CH_4} = \dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{d}{8} + \dfrac{e}{4}$ - $n_{CO_2} = \dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{d}{8} - \dfrac{e}{4}$ - **Lipids yield the highest CH₄ per kg VS but cause foaming and LCFA inhibition** — a high-frequency viva question. - **Vermicomposting** uses epigeic earthworms (*Eisenia foetida*, *Eudrilus eugeniae*); cannot tolerate > 35 °C — hence **must occur in ambient conditions**. - **Biohydrogen** is generated by **dark fermentation** (Clostridium sp., yields ~2 mol H₂/mol glucose) and **photo-fermentation** (Rhodobacter sp., uses VFAs). - **India policy alignment:** India's **SATAT scheme** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) targets **5,000 compressed biogas plants by 2025–26** — directly tied to biological conversion engineering outcomes. - **Syntrophy** between acetogens and hydrogenotrophic methanogens keeps H₂ partial pressure low (< 10⁻⁴ atm) — without it, acetogenesis is thermodynamically **infeasible**.

topics.title: Anaerobic digestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Anaerobic digestion # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is a biochemical process in which biodegradable organic matter is broken down by a consortium of **microorganisms in the strict absence of molecular oxygen (O₂)**, producing a gaseous mixture called **biogas** (primarily CH₄ and CO₂) and a nutrient-rich slurry called **digestate**. The overall generalized stoichiometric reaction for a generic carbohydrate substrate is represented as: $$C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobic bacteria}} 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$ The standard methane yield from carbohydrates is theoretically **0.415 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed**, while for fats and proteins it ranges from **0.6 to 1.0 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of Anaerobic Digestion as "Decomposition in a Sealed Lunch Box" 🍱** Imagine you seal wet kitchen waste inside an airtight steel container and leave it untouched for weeks. Without air (oxygen), a silent, invisible army of bacteria begins dismantling the food. They do not rot it in the open-air, smelly way; instead, they "eat" the organic carbon and "breathe out" a combustible gas (methane) and a nutrient-rich soup (digestate). - **Open composting** = aerobic (with air) → produces CO₂ + heat + compost - **Sealed bucket digestion** = anaerobic (no air) → produces CH₄ + CO₂ (biogas) + digestate This is exactly how a **continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR)** or a **bio-gas plant** works at municipal scale: a controlled, oxygen-free chamber where nature's microbial food chain converts waste into renewable fuel. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 AD Problems The following standard design metrics are highlighted by KTU examiners: - **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time)**: typically **15–30 days** for mesophilic digesters; **10–20 days** for thermophilic. - **SRT (Solid Retention Time)**: must be ≥ **2 × HRT** to retain slow-growing methanogens. - **OLR (Organic Loading Rate)**: typically **1.0–4.0 kg VS / m³·day** for CSTR; up to **10–30 kg COD / m³·day** for UASB. - **C : N : P ratio (ideal)**: **100 : 5 : 1** for balanced microbial nutrition. - **pH window for methanogens**: **6.5 – 7.8** (critical failure boundary). - **Mesophilic temperature**: **30 – 38 °C** (optimum ~35 °C). - **Thermophilic temperature**: **50 – 57 °C** (optimum ~55 °C). - **Standard methane calorific value**: **55.5 MJ / kg** (≈ 9.97 kWh / m³ at STP). --- ## 1.4 Why Anaerobic Digestion? (Engineering Relevance) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 frequently asks *"Why AD over aerobic treatment?"*. The examiner expects a comparative justification. Memorize the **four pillars**: 1. **Energy Positive** – Net energy yield ≈ **8 – 18 MJ / kg VS** (only wastewater treatment process that produces energy). 2. **Sludge Reduction** – VS destruction of **30 – 60 %** (vs. 30–40 % for aerobic). 3. **Pathogen Reduction** – Thermophilic AD achieves **> 99.9 % pathogen kill** (Class A biosolids). 4. **Greenhouse Gas Offset** – Captured CH₄ displaces fossil fuel combustion; CO₂ from biogas is biogenic (carbon neutral). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Biochemical Stages of Anaerobic Digestion KTU examiners LOVE asking the sequence and the **rate-limiting step**. Memorize the four-stage cascade below. ### Stage 1: Hydrolysis (Rate-Limiting Step for Particulate Wastes) - **Reactants**: Complex polymers — carbohydrates, proteins, lipids. - **Products**: Monomers — sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids (LCFAs). - **Key Bacteria**: *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus*. - **Time Scale**: Hours to days. - **Equation**: $$\text{Polymers} + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{hydrolases}} \text{Monomers}$$ ### Stage 2: Acidogenesis (Fermentation) - **Reactants**: Monomers. - **Products**: Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs — acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, CO₂. - **Key Bacteria**: *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Syntrophomonas*. - **Critical Issue**: VFAs drop pH. If unchecked, methanogens are inhibited. ### Stage 3: Acetogenesis (Syntrophic Oxidation) - **Reactants**: VFAs (other than acetate) + H₂. - **Products**: Acetate, H₂, CO₂. - **Key Bacteria**: *Syntrophobacter*, *Syntrophomonas*. - **Critical Syntrophy**: Reactions are thermodynamically unfavorable unless H₂ partial pressure remains **< 10⁻⁴ atm**. ### Stage 4: Methanogenesis (Rate-Limiting for Soluble Wastes) - **Path A — Acetoclastic (≈ 70 % of CH₄)**: $$CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$$ - **Path B — Hydrogenotrophic (≈ 30 % of CH₄)**: $$4H_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$$ --- ## 2.2 Process Parameters — Engineering Levers | Parameter | Mesophilic Range | Thermophilic Range | Failure Threshold | |---|---|---|---| | Temperature | **30–38 °C** | **50–57 °C** | < 20 °C or > 60 °C → inhibition | | pH | **6.5–7.8** | **6.5–7.8** | < 6.2 or > 8.3 → sour digester | | C : N ratio | **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** | same | < 15 or > 35 | | VFA / Alkalinity | **< 0.4** | same | > 0.8 → imbalanced | | Free NH₃ | **< 80 mg/L** | **< 80 mg/L** | > 150 mg/L → toxic | | HRT | **15–30 days** | **10–20 days** | < 10 d (mesophilic) → washout | | OLR (CSTR) | **1–4 kg VS/m³·d** | **2–6 kg VS/m³·d** | > 5 kg VS/m³·d | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = Q \times \text{HRT}$ | Reactor volume from flow rate | m³ | | 2 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Organic loading rate | kg VS/m³·d | | 3 | $\text{CH}_4 \text{ yield (theor.)} = \dfrac{22.4 \times n_{CH_4}}{M_{substrate}}$ | Theoretical methane volume | m³/kg | | 4 | $\%\text{CH}_4 = \dfrac{V_{CH_4}}{V_{biogas}} \times 100$ | Methane content | % | | 5 | $G = \dfrac{(CH_4\%)(\text{Eff})}{100} \times \rho_{CH_4} \times \text{CV}$ | Gross energy of biogas | MJ/m³ | | 6 | $D = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Dilution rate (reciprocal of HRT) | d⁻¹ | | 7 | $\mu_{max, meth} \approx 0.37 \, d^{-1}$ (mesophilic) | Max specific growth rate | d⁻¹ | | 8 | $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d}$ | Minimum SRT to avoid washout | days | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility**: These equations are used by **process engineers designing biogas plants**, environmental consultants sizing UASB reactors for distilleries/dairy units, and **CDM (Clean Development Mechanism) auditors** calculating carbon credits under the UNFCCC. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Methane Yield (Buswell Equation) The Buswell equation gives the **complete stoichiometric gas yield** for any organic substrate $C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e$: $$C_aH_bO_cN_dS_e + \left(a - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{c}{2} + \dfrac{3d}{4} + \dfrac{e}{2}\right)H_2O \longrightarrow$$ $$\left(\dfrac{a}{2} - \dfrac{b}{8} + \dfrac{c}{4} + \dfrac{3d}{8} + \dfrac{e}{4}\right)CH_4$$ $$+ \left(\dfrac{a}{2} + \dfrac{b}{8} - \dfrac{c}{4} - \dfrac{3d}{8} - \dfrac{e}{4}\right)CO_2 + d\,NH_3 + e\,H_2S$$ **Worked Example**: Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$, i.e., a=6, b=12, c=6, d=e=0) **Step 1** — Coefficient of H₂O required: $$\left(6 - \dfrac{12}{4} - \dfrac{6}{2} + 0 + 0\right) = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0$$ So glucose requires **zero water stoichiometrically**. **Step 2** — Coefficient of CH₄ produced: $$\left(\dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4} + 0 + 0\right) = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$$ So 3 moles of CH₄ per mole of glucose. **Step 3** — Coefficient of CO₂ produced: $$\left(\dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4}\right) = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$$ So 3 moles of CO₂ per mole of glucose. **Step 4** — Final balanced equation: $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$ **Step 5** — Mass of CH₄ per kg glucose: - 1 kg glucose = 1000/180 = **5.556 mol** - CH₄ produced = 5.556 × 3 = **16.667 mol** - Volume at STP = 16.667 × 22.4 L = **373.3 L CH₄ / kg glucose** This matches the standard textbook value of **0.373 m³ CH₄ / kg glucose**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reactor Sizing from Hydraulic Retention Time **Problem (KTU Style)**: A dairy wastewater of **1000 m³/day** with influent VS concentration **8 kg/m³** must be treated in a mesophilic CSTR. Design HRT = **20 days**. Compute the reactor volume and OLR. **Step 1** — Compute reactor volume: $$V = Q \times HRT = 1000 \times 20 = 20{,}000 \, m^3$$ **Step 2** — Compute daily VS load: $$\text{VS}_{daily} = Q \times S_0 = 1000 \times 8 = 8000 \, \text{kg VS/day}$$ **Step 3** — Compute OLR: $$\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \dfrac{8000}{20{,}000} = 0.4 \, \text{kg VS/m}^3 \cdot d$$ **Step 4** — Biogas estimation (assume 50 % VS destruction, 0.5 m³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed): - VS destroyed per day = 8000 × 0.50 = **4000 kg VS/day** - CH₄ produced = 4000 × 0.5 = **2000 m³ CH₄/day** - Total biogas (assume 60 % CH₄) = 2000 / 0.60 = **3333 m³ biogas/day** **Step 5** — Energy recovery: - Energy = 2000 × 9.97 kWh/m³ = **19,940 kWh/day ≈ 20 MWh/day** --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Methanogen Washout Criterion For a CSTR, methanogens wash out if HRT < τ_min: $$\tau_{min} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{max} - k_d}$$ Using **μ_max = 0.37 d⁻¹** (mesophilic acetate utilizers) and **k_d = 0.04 d⁻¹**: $$\tau_{min} = \dfrac{1}{0.37 - 0.04} = \dfrac{1}{0.33} \approx 3.03 \text{ days}$$ Therefore, for safety, designers use **SRT ≥ 2 × τ_min ≈ 6 days** minimum, and 15–30 days in practice. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Reactor Types | Reactor Type | HRT (days) | OLR (kg COD/m³·d) | CH₄ Content | Best For | |---|---|---|---|---| | Conventional CSTR | **15–40** | **1–4** | **55–65 %** | MSW, manure | | High-Rate CSTR | **10–20** | **5–10** | **55–65 %** | Manure + co-substrates | | UASB | **0.5–5** | **10–30** | **70–80 %** | Soluble industrial WW | | Plug Flow | **20–40** | **1–3** | **55–65 %** | High-solids manure | | Anaerobic Filter | **1–5** | **5–15** | **65–75 %** | Soluble WW with biofilm | | Fixed Film | **1–3** | **8–20** | **70–80 %** | Low-strength WW | | ABR (Baffled) | **1–10** | **5–15** | **65–75 %** | Decentralized plants | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram A — The Four-Stage Biochemical Cascade ```mermaid graph LR A["POLYMERS
Carbohydrates Proteins Lipids"] --> B["MONOMERS
Sugars Amino Acids LCFAs"] B --> C["INTERMEDIATES
VFAs Alcohols H2 CO2"] C --> D["METHANOGENIC SUBSTRATES
Acetate H2 CO2"] D --> E["BIOGAS
CH4 60% and CO2 40%"] A -. Hydrolysis .-> B B -. Acidogenesis .-> C C -. Acetogenesis .-> D D -. Methanogenesis .-> E style A fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 style C fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000 style D fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000 style E fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#351c75,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Diagram B — Continuous Stirred-Tank Reactor (CSTR) Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph FEED["FEED PREPARATION UNIT"] S1["Screening and Grit Removal"] --> S2["Size Reduction Shredder"] S2 --> S3["Mixing and Blending Tank"] end S3 --> P1["Feed Pump"] subgraph REACTOR["ANAEROBIC DIGESTER CSTR"] P1 --> V1["Inlet Port"] V1 --> MIX["Sludge Mixing Zone"] MIX --> BIO["Biomass Zone"] BIO --> GAS["Gas Collection Dome"] GAS --> OUT["Outlet to Dewatering"] end GAS --> UTIL["Biogas Utilization
CHP Boiler Upgrading"] OUT --> DEW["Digestate Dewatering"] DEW --> LIQ["Liquid Fraction Fertilizer"] DEW --> SOL["Solid Fraction Compost"] style FEED fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#b45f06 style REACTOR fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79 style UTIL fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d style DEW fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram C — UASB Reactor Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TB IN["Influent Wastewater
Q m3 per day"] --> DIST["Distribution Header
Even flow split"] DIST --> SLUDGE["Sludge Blanket Zone
Dense biomass granules"] SLUDGE --> SLACK["Slack Zone
Low biomass"] SLACK --> SEP["3-Phase Separator
Gas Solid Liquid"] SEP --> GASCOL["Biogas collected
to gas holder"] SEP --> EFF["Treated Effluent
to polishing"] SEP --> SLUDGERET["Sludge Return
back to blanket"] SLUDGERET --> SLUDGE style IN fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79 style SLUDGE fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d style SEP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 style GASCOL fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#351c75 ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram D — Process Control Decision Tree for Digester Failure ```mermaid graph TD FAIL["Alarms: pH Drop or
Low CH4 Yield"] --> Q1{"Check pH Level"} Q1 -- "pH less than 6.5" --> A1["VFA Accumulation Suspected"] A1 --> A2["Reduce OLR by 30 percent"] A2 --> A3["Add Alkalinity
NaHCO3 or CaCO3"] Q1 -- "pH greater than 7.8" --> B1["Ammonia Toxicity Suspected"] B1 --> B2["Reduce Feed N Content"] B2 --> B3["Dilute with Water"] FAIL --> Q2{"Check Temperature"} Q2 -- "Below 30 C" --> C1["Heater Failure"] C1 --> C2["Restart Heating Jacket"] Q2 -- "Above 57 C" --> D1["Hyperthermophilic Shift"] D1 --> D2["Cool Down Slowly"] style FAIL fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000 style A1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 style B1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 style C1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 style D1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** List the **four sequential stages** of anaerobic digestion and identify the **rate-limiting step** for a high-solids municipal solid waste (MSW) feed. **Model Answer (3 marks)**: 1. **Hydrolysis** — Conversion of complex polymers into soluble monomers. 2. **Acidogenesis** — Conversion of monomers into VFAs, alcohols, H₂, CO₂. 3. **Acetogenesis** — Conversion of VFAs into acetate, H₂, and CO₂. 4. **Methanogenesis** — Conversion of acetate/H₂ into CH₄ and CO₂. For particulate MSW, **Hydrolysis** is the rate-limiting stage because MSW contains long-chain lignocellulosic polymers that require slow enzymatic breakdown. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q:** What is **Syntrophy** in anaerobic digestion? Explain with an example. **Model Answer (3 marks)**: Syntrophy is a **mutualistic metabolic relationship** between two microbial species where the reaction catalyzed by one organism is **thermodynamically unfavorable** unless the product (typically H₂) is consumed by the partner organism, keeping H₂ partial pressure below **10⁻⁴ atm**. **Example**: *Syntrophomonas wolfei* oxidizes propionate to acetate + H₂ + CO₂. This reaction has ΔG°′ = +76 kJ/mol (non-spontaneous). However, when methanogens simultaneously consume H₂ to form CH₄, the ΔG becomes negative, and the reaction proceeds. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Loss-of-Marks Warning** > > 1. **Forgetting the units of OLR** (must be kg VS/m³·d — NOT kg/m³·d). > 2. **Confusing HRT with SRT** — In a CSTR they are equal; in UASB, SRT ≫ HRT. > 3. **Writing '4H₂ + CO₂' as the only methanogenic pathway** — Examiners deduct **2 marks** for omitting acetoclastic methanogenesis. > 4. **Not writing the balanced reaction** before calculating theoretical CH₄ yield. > 5. **Ignoring the H₂S component** of biogas during calorific value calculations. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four stages** of anaerobic digestion in detail, including the **microorganisms involved**, the **substrates/products**, and the **rate-limiting conditions** for each stage. **(b) [7 Marks]** A food-processing industry generates **500 m³/day** of wastewater with a biodegradable COD of **15 kg/m³**. Design a **mesophilic CSTR** for AD with HRT = **20 days**. Compute (i) reactor volume, (ii) OLR, (iii) expected methane production assuming **70 % COD destruction** and **0.35 m³ CH₄/kg COD destroyed**. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks]** — Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion **Stage 1 — Hydrolysis**: - Substrate: complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids). - Products: monomers (sugars, amino acids, LCFA). - Bacteria: *Clostridium thermocellum*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus*. - Rate-limiting when feed is particulate (MSW, sludge). - Controlled by extracellular enzymes (cellulase, protease, lipase). **Stage 2 — Acidogenesis**: - Substrate: monomers. - Products: VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, CO₂. - Bacteria: *Clostridium butyricum*, *Bacteroides*, *Lactobacillus*. - Rapid reaction; can cause pH drop and digester souring. **Stage 3 — Acetogenesis**: - Substrate: VFAs (other than acetate). - Products: acetate, H₂, CO₂. - Bacteria: *Syntrophobacter wolinii*, *Syntrophomonas wolfei*. - Thermodynamically requires low H₂ partial pressure (syntrophy with methanogens). **Stage 4 — Methanogenesis**: - Substrate: acetate (70 %), H₂/CO₂ (30 %). - Products: CH₄, CO₂. - Bacteria: *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta* (acetoclastic); *Methanobacterium* (hydrogenotrophic). - Slowest for soluble wastes; sensitive to pH, NH₃, VFA. **(b) [7 Marks]** — CSTR Design **Step 1** — Reactor Volume: $$V = Q \times HRT = 500 \times 20 = 10{,}000 \, m^3$$ **Step 2** — Organic Loading Rate: $$\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \times S_0}{V} = \dfrac{500 \times 15}{10{,}000} = 0.75 \, \text{kg COD/m}^3 \cdot d$$ **Step 3** — Daily COD destroyed: $$\text{COD}_{dest} = 0.70 \times 500 \times 15 = 5250 \, \text{kg COD/day}$$ **Step 4** — Methane production: $$Q_{CH_4} = 5250 \times 0.35 = 1837.5 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}$$ **Step 5** — Energy recovery (assume 60 % CH₄ in biogas): - Biogas = 1837.5 / 0.60 = **3062.5 m³/day** - Energy = 1837.5 × 9.97 = **18,320 kWh/day ≈ 18.3 MWh/day** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **CSTR, UASB, and Plug Flow** anaerobic digesters in terms of HRT, OLR, methane content, and suitable applications. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of the **Buswell equation**, derive the theoretical methane yield (in m³/kg) for **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)** as the sole substrate. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks]** — Reactor Comparison | Feature | CSTR | UASB | Plug Flow | |---|---|---|---| | HRT (days) | **15–40** | **0.5–5** | **20–40** | | OLR (kg COD/m³·d) | **1–4** | **10–30** | **1–3** | | CH₄ content | **55–65 %** | **70–80 %** | **55–65 %** | | Biomass retention | Mixed in bulk | Granular sludge bed | Settled at bottom | | Best feed | Manure, MSW | Soluble industrial WW | High-solids manure | | Footprint | Large | Very small | Large | | Capital cost | Moderate | Low | Moderate | **(b) [7 Marks]** — Buswell Equation for CH₃COOH For CH₃COOH: a=2, b=4, c=2, d=e=0. **Step 1** — Water coefficient: $$\left(2 - \dfrac{4}{4} - \dfrac{2}{2}\right) = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0$$ **Step 2** — Methane coefficient: $$\left(\dfrac{2}{2} - \dfrac{4}{8} + \dfrac{2}{4}\right) = 1 - 0.5 + 0.5 = 1$$ **Step 3** — CO₂ coefficient: $$\left(\dfrac{2}{2} + \dfrac{4}{8} - \dfrac{2}{4}\right) = 1 + 0.5 - 0.5 = 1$$ **Step 4** — Balanced equation: $$CH_3COOH \longrightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$$ **Step 5** — Mass yield: - 1 mol CH₃COOH = 60 g → 1 mol CH₄ - 1 kg CH₃COOH = 1000/60 = 16.667 mol → 16.667 mol CH₄ - Volume at STP = 16.667 × 22.4 L = **373.3 L CH₄/kg CH₃COOH** Therefore **theoretical yield = 0.373 m³ CH₄ / kg acetic acid**. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔑 High-Yield Concepts to Memorize for KTU Exam Day** - ✅ Anaerobic digestion is **oxygen-free decomposition** by microbes producing **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + digestate**. - ✅ **Four stages**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. - ✅ **Rate-limiting step** = Hydrolysis (for solids) / Methanogenesis (for soluble waste). - ✅ **Two methanogenic pathways**: Acetoclastic (~70 %) and Hydrogenotrophic (~30 %). - ✅ **Ideal pH for methanogens**: **6.5 – 7.8**. - ✅ **Mesophilic**: 30–38 °C ; **Thermophilic**: 50–57 °C. - ✅ **Standard HRT**: 15–30 days (mesophilic CSTR). - ✅ **OLR formula**: $OLR = Q \cdot S_0 / V$. - ✅ **Reactor volume formula**: $V = Q \times HRT$. - ✅ **Buswell equation** is used to derive **theoretical methane yield** for any substrate. - ✅ **Syntrophy** = mutualistic metabolism requiring H₂ partial pressure < **10⁻⁴ atm**. - ✅ **Glucose digestion** yields 3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂ (balanced stoichiometry). - ✅ **Acetic acid digestion** yields 1 CH₄ + 1 CO₂. - ✅ **Methane calorific value**: **55.5 MJ/kg** or **9.97 kWh/m³**. - ✅ **VFA/TA ratio**: must be **< 0.4** for stable digester operation. - ✅ **C : N : P ideal ratio**: **100 : 5 : 1** for balanced nutrition. - ✅ **SRT must be ≥ 2 × τ_min** to prevent methanogen washout. - ✅ Reactor types in declining order of OLR capacity: **UASB > AF > CSTR > Plug Flow**. - ✅ Biogas typically contains **55–65 % CH₄, 30–40 % CO₂, trace H₂S, NH₃, water vapor**.

topics.title: Biochemical digestion process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biochemical digestion process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
