# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1. Define anaerobic digestion. List the four microbiological stages involved.**

**Model Answer:**
Anaerobic digestion (AD) is the oxygen-free, microbiologically mediated decomposition of organic matter into biogas (mainly CH₄ and CO₂), water, microbial biomass, and inorganic residues, carried out in a sealed reactor called a digester.

The four stages are:
1. **Hydrolysis** — polymers cleaved to monomers by extracellular enzymes.
2. **Acidogenesis** — monomers fermented to VFAs, alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs (C3+) oxidized to acetate, H₂, and CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — acetate and H₂/CO₂ converted to CH₄ by Archaea.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between mesophilic and thermophilic digestion. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mesophilic | Thermophilic |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | 30–38 °C | 50–58 °C |
| HRT | 15–30 d | 10–20 d |
| Pathogen kill | Moderate | High |
| Energy demand | Lower | Higher |
| Process stability | High | Lower (more sensitive to toxins) |
| **Advantage** | Stable, lower heating cost | Faster kinetics, better pathogen destruction |
| **Disadvantage** | Larger reactor volume | Higher energy input and ammonia-inhibition risk |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Answer ONE of the following. Each sub-question carries 7 marks.**

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
> **Q.A (a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Continuously Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** anaerobic digester. Discuss its merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.A (b)** A community biogas plant receives **25 m³/d** of food waste slurry at **VS = 35 kg/m³**. The digester volume is **500 m³**, effluent VS = **15 kg/m³**, and yield coefficient = **0.40 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed**. Calculate (i) HRT, (ii) OLR, (iii) VS reduction, (iv) daily CH₄ production, (v) daily energy in MJ. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Construction:**
A CSTR consists of a cylindrical (or rectangular) reinforced-concrete or steel tank, typically with a flat or conical bottom, a mechanical mixer (paddle or turbine impeller) mounted on a top- or side-shaft, an external or internal heat-exchanger coil for temperature control, an inlet pipe for fresh slurry, an outlet overflow weir for effluent, a gas-tight dome on top, and a gas outlet pipe leading to a scrubbing and storage system.

**Working:**
Slurry is fed continuously (or semi-continuously) into the tank, mixed thoroughly so that the contents are assumed spatially uniform, and heated to the mesophilic (35 °C) or thermophilic (55 °C) range. Microbial degradation occurs uniformly throughout the vessel. Biogas rises and is collected under the dome; the digestate overflows through the outlet weir.

**Merits:**
- Uniform operating conditions simplify modelling and control.
- Handles variable feedstock composition.
- Well-established design equations.
- Easy to scale up.

**Demerits:**
- High energy demand for continuous stirring.
- Complete mixing exposes the entire microbial population to toxins.
- High CAPEX for mechanical agitator and seal.
- Not ideal for high-solids (> 10 % TS) feedstocks.

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

(i) $\theta = 500/25 = \mathbf{20\, d}$

(ii) $\text{OLR} = 25 \times 35 / 500 = \mathbf{1.75\, kg\, VS/(m^3 \cdot d)}$

(iii) $\text{VS}_{\text{red}} = (35-15)/35 \times 100 = \mathbf{57.14\,\%}$

(iv) $V_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.40 \times 25 \times (35-15) = 0.40 \times 500 = \mathbf{200\, m^3/d}$

(v) $E = 200 \times 35.8 = \mathbf{7160\, MJ/d}$

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary]**
> **Q.B (a)** Explain the **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB) reactor** with a diagram. List its salient features and typical applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.B (b)** Derive an expression for the **minimum Solids Retention Time (SRT)** in a CSTR digester using Monod kinetics and explain its design significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**UASB Reactor — Construction & Working:**
The UASB reactor is a tall, vertical cylindrical tank with an influent distributor at the bottom, an internal three-phase separator (gas–solid–liquid separator) near the top, and an effluent launder at the very top. Influent wastewater enters from below and flows upward through a dense bed of **granular sludge** (self-immobilized microbial aggregates 1–3 mm in diameter). The granules convert the dissolved organics into biogas; the rising gas bubbles cause gentle internal mixing and contact. The three-phase separator deflects biogas into a gas dome, returns sludge granules to the bed, and lets clarified effluent overflow.

**Salient Features:**
- Very high biomass concentration (50–100 kg VSS/m³ inside the blanket).
- Short HRT (0.5–5 days).
- No mechanical mixing or carrier media.
- Excellent for high-strength soluble wastewaters.

**Applications:**
- Distillery spent wash.
- Brewery, starch, and sugar effluents.
- Pulp and paper mill condensate.
- Municipal sewage (advanced treatment).

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

Refer to **Section 3.2 of these notes** (Derivation II). The complete derivation proceeds in five steps:

1. Biomass balance at steady state:
$$ 0 = -\frac{QX}{V} + \mu X - k_d X $$
2. Recognising $\theta_c = V/Q$ and rearranging:
$$ \mu = \frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d $$
3. Substituting Monod kinetics for $\mu$:
$$ \mu_{\max} \frac{S}{K_s + S} = \frac{1}{\theta_c} + k_d $$
4. At washout, $S \to S_0$ (Substrate fully bypasses reactor), so $\mu \to \mu_{\max}$:
$$ \mu_{\max} = \frac{1}{\theta_c^{\min}} + k_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_c^{\min} = \frac{1}{\mu_{\max} - k_d} $$
5. **Design significance:** Operating SRT must be at least **2–4 × $\theta_c^{\min}$** to ensure stable methanogen retention. For mesophilic methanogens ($\mu_{\max} \approx 0.3$–$0.4\, \text{d}^{-1}$, $k_d \approx 0.02\, \text{d}^{-1}$), $\theta_c^{\min} \approx 2.5$–3.5 days, hence the commonly prescribed SRT of 10–20 days. Methanogens grow slowly; the SRT must decouple from HRT, which is precisely why **UASB and biofilm systems outperform CSTR** for high-rate digestion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing HRT and SRT** — these are equal only in CSTR with no sludge recycle. In UASB, SRT ≈ 30–50 days while HRT is 1–5 days. Writing "$\theta_c = \theta$ for all reactors" is the **most common deduct-1-mark error**.
> 2. **Ignoring units** — OLR must come out in **kg VS / (m³·d)**. Submissions with mixed g/L and kg/m³ lose 1 mark even if the numerical answer is correct.
> 3. **Skipping stages in microbiology** — answering "AD is done by bacteria" loses 1–2 marks. Examiners expect explicit mention of *Archaea* (methanogens) and the four stages.
> 4. **Forgetting the energy conversion constant** — using LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg instead of 35.8 MJ/m³ for volumetric calculations is a frequent mistake.
> 5. **Writing "biogas = methane"** — biogas is a mixture; specifying the **CH₄ fraction (50–70 %)** and the **Buswell equation** earns full marks.
> 6. **Diagrams without labels** — A CSTR/UASB diagram without temperature, pH, and microbial labels loses 2 of the 3 diagram marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [x] **Anaerobic digestion = oxygen-free microbial decomposition**; principal products are **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**, **digestate**, and **microbial biomass**.
- [x] **Four stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. Methanogenesis is the *thermodynamic bottleneck* due to slow-growing Archaea.
- [x] **Standard conditions:** Mesophilic **35 °C**, Thermophilic **55 °C**, pH **6.8–7.4**, C:N **20–30:1**.
- [x] **HRT formula:** $\theta = V/Q$ (days).
- [x] **OLR formula:** $\text{OLR} = Q \cdot S_0 / V$ in kg VS / (m³·d).
- [x] **Methane yield equation:** $V_{\text{CH}_4} = Y_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S)$.
- [x] **Energy equation:** $E\, (\text{MJ/d}) = V_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot 35.8$.
- [x] **Minimum SRT:** $\theta_c^{\min} = 1 / (\mu_{\max} - k_d)$; design with **2–4 × safety factor**.
- [x] **Reactor types and their niche:** CSTR → slurry; UASB → soluble industrial; Plug-flow → high-solids manure; Garage → 20–40 % TS OFMSW; Covered lagoon → cheap low-rate.
- [x] **Buswell stoichiometry:** A carbohydrate-type VS (C₆H₁₀O₅) yields ~0.415 m³ CH₄/kg VS at STP, *in theory*. Real yields are 60–85 % of theoretical.
- [x] **Process failure signals:** pH drop below 6.5 → VFA accumulation → H₂ buildup → methanogen inhibition → ammonia toxicity above 3 g NH₃-N/L.
- [x] **VS reduction 40–60 %** is a healthy process; below 30 % indicates under-loading or toxicity.
- [x] **Biogas composition:** CH₄ 50–70 %, CO₂ 30–40 %, H₂S < 2 %, NH₃ trace, H₂O saturated.
- [x] **LHV of methane (volumetric) = 35.8 MJ/m³** at STP — *use this, not the gravimetric 50 MJ/kg*.
- [x] **Diagram must always be labelled** with temperature, pH, and dominant microbial group.
- [x] **Digestion systems are not "magic"** — they require: pre-treatment, mixing, heating, gas-tight sealing, and post-digestate management.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy from anaerobic digestion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy from anaerobic digestion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is a biological process in which biodegradable organic matter is broken down by microorganisms in the **complete absence of oxygen**, producing a gaseous mixture called **biogas** (primarily methane and carbon dioxide) and a nutrient-rich liquid/solid residue called **digestate**. The general stoichiometry of anaerobic digestion is described by the **Buswell Equation** (1936), which represents the complete mineralization of organic matter (CₙHₐOᵦNᵧSₓ): $$C_nH_aO_bN_cS_d + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} + \frac{3c}{4} + \frac{d}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8} - \frac{d}{4}\right)CH_4 + nCO_2 + cNH_3 + dH_2S$$ For a simple carbohydrate such as glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), the equation simplifies to: $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2 \quad \Delta G^{\circ'} = -390\ kJ/mol$$ The standard methane yield is **0.373 m³ CH₄/kg COD** at **STP (0°C, 1 atm)** and **0.350 m³ CH₄/kg COD** at standard conditions of **25°C, 1 atm**. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cow's Stomach Inside a Tank" Imagine a cow's rumen — a dark, oxygen-free stomach chamber where billions of microbes slowly ferment grass into energy. A biogas digester is essentially an **engineered artificial rumen**. Instead of grass, we feed it kitchen waste, sewage sludge, food processing residues, or animal manure. The microbes, working in shifts (some break down, some ferment, some make methane), release biogas that can be burned to generate **electricity and heat** — exactly like natural gas. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Students**: Anaerobic digestion does NOT burn waste. It *bio-transforms* it. The energy stored in chemical bonds of organic matter is released biologically as methane, which is later combusted for useful work. --- ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |--------|--------|--------------|------| | Methane fraction in biogas | x_CH₄ | 50 – 70 | % (v/v) | | Energy content of methane | LHV_CH₄ | 50.0 | MJ/kg | | Density of methane (STP) | ρ_CH₄ | 0.717 | kg/m³ | | Theoretical CH₄ yield per kg VS | Y_CH₄ | 0.35 – 0.50 | m³/kg | | Hydraulic Retention Time | HRT | 15 – 40 | days | | Optimal temperature (mesophilic) | T | 35 – 37 | °C | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Biological Stages of Anaerobic Digestion The process is governed by a syntrophic microbial consortium. Each stage is performed by a different group of microorganisms operating in a tightly coupled cascade. > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Point**: The rate-limiting step in AD of complex solid wastes (e.g., lignocellulosic biomass) is **Stage 1 (Hydrolysis)**, while in readily soluble wastes (e.g., sugars, VFA), the rate-limiting step is **Stage 4 (Methanogenesis)**. --- #### **Stage 1 — Hydrolysis** - **Function**: Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are enzymatically cleaved into monomers. - **Microbes**: *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, aerobic/facultative bacteria with extracellular enzymes. - **Key Enzymes**: Amylases (starch → glucose), proteases (proteins → amino acids), lipases (lipids → fatty acids + glycerol). - **General Reaction**: $$Polymers \xrightarrow{hydrolases} Monomers$$ Example for cellulose: $$(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O \xrightarrow{cellulase} nC_6H_{12}O_6$$ --- #### **Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)** - **Function**: Monomers are converted into Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂. - **Microbes**: *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Bacillus*. - **Key Products**: Acetate, propionate, butyrate, lactate, ethanol, H₂, CO₂. $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2CH_3CH_2OH + 2CO_2$$ $$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$$ --- #### **Stage 3 — Acetogenesis** - **Function**: VFAs and alcohols are oxidized into acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by obligate hydrogen-producing acetogens (OHPA). - **Microbes**: *Syntrophomonas*, *Syntrophobacter*. - **Thermodynamic Constraint**: This reaction is endergonic under standard conditions (ΔG°' > 0), but proceeds because methanogens (Stage 4) keep H₂ partial pressure extremely low (< 10⁻⁴ atm) — this is **interspecies hydrogen transfer**. $$CH_3CH_2COO^- + 2H_2O \rightarrow CH_3COO^- + 3H_2 + CO_2 \quad \Delta G^{\circ'} = +76.1\ kJ/mol$$ $$CH_3CH_2OH + H_2O \rightarrow CH_3COO^- + 2H_2 + H^+ \quad \Delta G^{\circ'} = +9.6\ kJ/mol$$ --- #### **Stage 4 — Methanogenesis** - **Function**: Acetate, H₂/CO₂, and other simple substrates are converted into CH₄. - **Microbes**: *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina* (acetoclastic), *Methanobacterium* (hydrogenotrophic). - **Two Main Pathways**: **Acetoclastic (~70% of CH₄ produced):** $$CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2 \quad \Delta G^{\circ'} = -31.0\ kJ/mol$$ **Hydrogenotrophic (~30% of CH₄ produced):** $$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O \quad \Delta G^{\circ'} = -131.0\ kJ/mol$$ --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Application | |---|------------------|------------|------|-------------| | 1 | Methane yield (theoretical) | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{0.35 \cdot (g\ COD\ removed)}{1000}$ | m³ CH₄ | Design of digester | | 2 | Biogas energy content | $E_{biogas} = V \cdot [x_{CH_4} \cdot 35.8 + x_{H_2S} \cdot 25.6]$ | MJ | Energy recovery | | 3 | Buswell stoichiometry | $C_nH_aO_bN_cS_d \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2 + NH_3 + H_2S$ | — | Mass balance | | 4 | Organic Loading Rate | $OLR = \dfrac{m_{VS}}{V_{reactor} \cdot HRT}$ | kg VS/m³·d | Reactor sizing | | 5 | Digester volume | $V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{OLR}$ | m³ | Civil design | | 6 | SRT (Sludge Retention Time) | $SRT = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w}$ | days | Microbiology control | | 7 | pH inhibition threshold | $pH_{min} = 6.2$, $pH_{max} = 7.8$ | — | Process control | | 8 | C:N ratio optimum | $C/N_{opt} = 20:1$ to $30:1$ | — | Co-digestion | | 9 | Free ammonia inhibition | $[NH_3] = \dfrac{[TAN]}{1 + 10^{(pK_a - pH)}}$ | mg/L | Toxicity check | | 10 | Specific gas yield | $SGY = \dfrac{V_{CH_4}}{m_{VS\,added}}$ | m³/kg VS | Performance KPI | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip**: The Buswell equation in its general form is a high-weightage question. Memorize the simplified carbohydrate, protein, and lipid forms. These are 14-markers' worth of derivations. --- ### 2.3 Practical Real-World Utility - **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) Management**: Landfill-diversion and decentralized energy. - **Wastewater Treatment Plants (WWTPs)**: Sludge stabilization and energy neutrality (e.g., ENERWATER concept). - **Agriculture**: Biogas from cattle/pig manure; digestate as biofertilizer. - **Industries**: Food processing, breweries, dairy, pulp & paper — high-strength wastewater valorization. - **Rural Electrification**: Off-grid biogas gensets in developing economies. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Methane Yield from Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) > **Theorem**: For every 1 kg of COD stabilized under anaerobic conditions, **0.35 Nm³ of methane (at STP, 0°C, 1 atm)** is theoretically produced. **Step 1 — State the substrate half-reaction** The organic matter is oxidized with oxygen (definition of COD): $$CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$$ Conversely, oxidation of a generic organic compound in COD test yields electrons equivalent to: $$C_xH_yO_z + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{z}{2}\right)O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}H_2O$$ **Step 2 — Equivalence in anaerobic digestion** In AD, the same electrons are accepted by CO₂ to form CH₄. Therefore, **1 mole of O₂ (i.e., 32 g) ≡ 1 mole of CH₄ (i.e., 16 g)**. **Step 3 — Convert to volume at STP** - Molar volume at STP = **22.4 L/mol** - Volume of 1 mole CH₄ = 22.4 L = 0.0224 Nm³ - Per mole of O₂: 0.0224 Nm³ CH₄ - Per kg O₂: (1/32) × 1000 × 0.0224 = **0.700 Nm³ CH₄/kg O₂** **Step 4 — Convert per kg COD (which is equivalent to kg O₂ demand)** $$Y_{CH_4} = 0.700\ Nm^3/kg\ O_2 \cdot 0.5 = 0.350\ Nm^3\ CH_4/kg\ COD$$ The factor 0.5 arises because each CH₄ produced in methanogenesis is matched to one CO₂ (acetate is split 50:50), so only half the electrons end up as CH₄ in the acetoclastic pathway. **Step 5 — Energy equivalence** Energy in 1 Nm³ CH₄ (HHV) = **39.8 MJ**, hence: $$E_{CH_4} = 0.350 \times 39.8 = 13.93\ MJ/kg\ COD\ removed$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometry of Methane from Glucose (Simplified Buswell) > **Given**: Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is fully converted to CH₄ and CO₂. > **Find**: Mole ratio of CH₄ : CO₂. **Step 1 — Carbon balance** Each glucose has 6 carbons. They distribute between CH₄ and CO₂. Let $n_{CH_4} = a$ and $n_{CO_2} = b$. Then: $$a + b = 6 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 2 — Oxidation-state balance (electron balance)** In glucose: C has average oxidation state = 0. In CH₄: C has oxidation state = -4. In CO₂: C has oxidation state = +4. Total electrons gained by C in forming CH₄ = $4a$. Total electrons lost by C in forming CO₂ = $4b$. Sum of oxidation-state changes must equal **zero** (no external oxidant/reductant): $$-4a + 4b = 0 \Rightarrow a = b \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 3 — Solve** From (i) and (ii): $a = b = 3$. **Step 4 — Write final equation** $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$$ **Step 5 — Verify hydrogen and oxygen balances** - H: 12 = 4(3) + 0 = 12 ✓ - O: 6 = 0 + 3(2) = 6 ✓ **Step 6 — Volume ratio** Since equal moles occupy equal volume at the same T & P: $$\dfrac{V_{CH_4}}{V_{CO_2}} = \dfrac{3}{3} = 1 \Rightarrow x_{CH_4} = 50\%$$ > This explains why carbohydrate-rich substrates yield biogas with ~50% CH₄. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Stoichiometry of Methane from a Generic Lipid (Tripalmitin) > **Given**: Tripalmitin C₅₁H₉₈O₆ (a triglyceride). > **Find**: Theoretical CH₄ and CO₂ volumes per mole. **Step 1 — Hydrolysis (Lipolysis)** Tripalmitin first hydrolyzes to glycerol + 3 palmitic acid: $$C_{51}H_{98}O_6 + 3H_2O \rightarrow C_3H_8O_3 + 3C_{16}H_{32}O_2$$ **Step 2 — Apply Buswell to glycerol** Glycerol (C₃H₈O₃) per Buswell: $$4C_3H_8O_3 \rightarrow 7CH_4 + 5CO_2$$ **Step 3 — Apply Buswell to palmitic acid** Palmitic acid (C₁₆H₃₂O₂) per Buswell: $$C_{16}H_{32}O_2 + 8H_2O \rightarrow 7CH_4 + 9CO_2 + 8H_2$$ (H₂ is later consumed by hydrogenotrophic methanogens.) **Step 4 — Net per mole tripalmitin** $$C_{51}H_{98}O_6 + (8+3\times 8 - 3)H_2O \rightarrow \dfrac{7}{4}CH_4 + \dfrac{5}{4}CO_2 + 3\left(7CH_4 + 9CO_2 + 8H_2\right)$$ Simplifying: $$C_{51}H_{98}O_6 + 29H_2O \rightarrow 21.75CH_4 + 28.25CO_2 + 24H_2$$ The 24 H₂ reacts via hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis: $$24H_2 + 6CO_2 \rightarrow 6CH_4 + 12H_2O$$ **Step 5 — Final stoichiometry** $$C_{51}H_{98}O_6 + 17H_2O \rightarrow 27.75CH_4 + 22.25CO_2$$ **Step 6 — Methane fraction** $$x_{CH_4} = \dfrac{27.75}{27.75 + 22.25} = 55.5\%$$ > **Observation for KTU**: Lipids yield the **highest** CH₄ fraction (~55–60%) because they are highly reduced. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Theoretical CH₄ Yield & Biogas Composition by Substrate | Substrate | Formula | g CH₄ / g substrate | m³ CH₄ / kg VS | x_CH₄ (vol%) | Biogas yield (m³/kg VS) | |-----------|---------|---------------------|----------------|--------------|-------------------------| | Carbohydrate (glucose) | C₆H₁₂O₆ | 0.267 | 0.373 | 50.0 | 0.747 | | Protein (albumin) | C₅H₇NO₂ | 0.355 | 0.496 | 50.0 | 0.992 | | Lipid (tripalmitin) | C₅₁H₉₈O₆ | 0.631 | 0.882 | 55.5 | 1.589 | | Acetic acid | C₂H₄O₂ | 0.267 | 0.373 | 50.0 | 0.747 | | Methanol | CH₃OH | 0.187 | 0.262 | 50.0 | 0.524 | | Ethanol | C₂H₆O | 0.437 | 0.612 | 75.0 | 0.816 | | Municipal sludge | empirical | ~0.20 | 0.28 | 60 | 0.47 | | Cattle manure | empirical | ~0.18 | 0.25 | 60 | 0.42 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic**: *Lipids give the most, carbs give the middle, and proteins give the most CH₄ per carbon but a lot of NH₃ (inhibition risk).* ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Four Biological Stages ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Organic Waste Input
Carbohydrates, Proteins, Lipids] --> B[Stage 1 Hydrolysis
Extracellular Enzymes] B --> C[Stage 2 Acidogenesis
Fermentative Bacteria] C --> D[Stage 3 Acetogenesis
Syntrophic Acetogens] D --> E[Stage 4 Methanogenesis
Archaea] E --> F[Biogas Output
CH4 50 to 70 percent
CO2 30 to 50 percent] E --> G[Digestate Output
Liquid and Solid Residue] H[Hydrolytic Enzymes
Amylase, Protease, Lipase] --> B I[VFAs, Alcohols, H2, CO2] --> D J[Acetate, H2, CO2] --> E style A fill:#e1f5ff style F fill:#fff4b3 style G fill:#d4f4dd style B fill:#ffd6d6 style C fill:#ffd6d6 style D fill:#ffd6d6 style E fill:#ffd6d6 ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — CSTR Anaerobic Digester Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FeedSystem [Feed System] FS1[Substrate Hopper] --> FS2[Crusher or Grinder] --> FS3[Slurry Mixing Tank] end FS3 --> DIG1 subgraph DigesterUnit [Anaerobic Digester Reactor] direction TB DIG1[Feed Inlet] --> DIG2[Heating Coil
Mesophilic 35 to 37 C] DIG2 --> DIG3[Mixing Impeller
Continuous or Intermittent] DIG3 --> DIG4[Microbial Consortium
Hydrolysis to Methanogenesis] DIG4 --> DIG5[Gas Collection Dome] end DIG5 --> GS1 subgraph GasHandling [Gas Handling Unit] GS1[Biogas Outlet] --> GS2[Desulfurization H2S Scrubber] --> GS3[Moisture Trap] --> GS4[Biogas Storage Holder] end GS4 --> EU1 subgraph EndUse [Energy End Use] EU1[Combined Heat and Power
CHP Engine] --> EU2[Electricity to Grid] EU1 --> EU3[Thermal Heat Recovery] GS4 --> EU4[Boiler Direct Thermal] end DIG4 --> DS1 subgraph DigestateProcessing [Digestate Management] DS1[Overflow Outlet] --> DS2[Solid Liquid Separator] --> DS3[Liquid Fraction
Biofertilizer] DS2 --> DS4[Solid Fraction
Compost or Soil Conditioner] end style FS1 fill:#e1f5ff style FS2 fill:#e1f5ff style FS3 fill:#e1f5ff style DIG1 fill:#ffe1e1 style DIG2 fill:#ffe1e1 style DIG3 fill:#ffe1e1 style DIG4 fill:#ffe1e1 style DIG5 fill:#ffe1e1 style GS1 fill:#fff4b3 style GS2 fill:#fff4b3 style GS3 fill:#fff4b3 style GS4 fill:#fff4b3 style EU1 fill:#d4f4dd style EU2 fill:#d4f4dd style EU3 fill:#d4f4dd style EU4 fill:#d4f4dd style DS1 fill:#e6d4f4 style DS2 fill:#e6d4f4 style DS3 fill:#e6d4f4 style DS4 fill:#e6d4f4 ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Tree — Reactor Types for AD ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Anaerobic Reactor Configurations] --> R1[Continuously Stirred Tank Reactor CSTR] R --> R2[Anaerobic Contact Reactor ACR] R --> R3[Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket UASB] R --> R4[Anaerobic Filter AF] R --> R5[Expanded Granular Sludge Bed EGSB] R --> R6[Plug Flow Reactor PFR] R --> R7[Anaerobic Baffled Reactor ABR] R --> R8[Two Stage Digester] R1 --> R1a[Low to Medium Solids
5 to 10 percent TS
HRT 20 to 40 days] R3 --> R3a[High Rate
HRT 4 to 12 hours
Granular Sludge Blanket] R8 --> R8a[Acid Phase Reactor in Series with Methane Phase Reactor] style R fill:#fff4b3 style R1 fill:#e1f5ff style R2 fill:#e1f5ff style R3 fill:#e1f5ff style R4 fill:#e1f5ff style R5 fill:#e1f5ff style R6 fill:#e1f5ff style R7 fill:#e1f5ff style R8 fill:#e1f5ff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PGAET753]** **Q1.** Define anaerobic digestion. List the four biochemical stages involved in the process. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Anaerobic digestion is the controlled biological decomposition of biodegradable organic matter in the absence of oxygen, carried out by a mixed microbial consortium, producing **biogas** (CH₄ + CO₂) and **digestate**. > > **Four stages (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** > 1. **Hydrolysis** — polymers → monomers > 2. **Acidogenesis** — monomers → VFAs, alcohols, H₂, CO₂ > 3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs → acetate + H₂ + CO₂ > 4. **Methanogenesis** — acetate/H₂ → CH₄ --- **Q2.** What is the **Buswell equation**? Write it for the substrate C₁₀H₁₉O₃N. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** The Buswell equation is the generalized stoichiometric expression for complete anaerobic mineralization of organic matter, predicting CH₄, CO₂, NH₃, and H₂S yields. > > **General form (1 mark):** > > $$C_nH_aO_bN_cS_d + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4} + \dfrac{d}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4}\right)CH_4 + nCO_2 + cNH_3 + dH_2S$$ > > **For C₁₀H₁₉O₃N (1 mark):** Here n=10, a=19, b=3, c=1, d=0. > Substituting: H₂O coefficient = 10 - 19/4 - 3/2 + 3/4 + 0 = 10 - 4.75 - 1.5 + 0.75 = **4.5** > CH₄ coefficient = 5 + 19/8 - 3/4 - 3/8 = 5 + 2.375 - 0.75 - 0.375 = **6.25** > Final balanced equation: > $$C_{10}H_{19}O_3N + 4.5H_2O \rightarrow 6.25CH_4 + 10CO_2 + NH_3$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PGAET753]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain in detail the **four biochemical stages of anaerobic digestion** with the help of microbial groups, key enzymes/reactions, and the role of interspecies hydrogen transfer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A continuously stirred tank anaerobic digester is fed with **500 m³/day** of distillery wastewater having **COD = 50,000 mg/L**. The digester achieves **80% COD removal efficiency**. The methane fraction in the biogas is **60%**. Calculate: (i) The daily methane gas production in Nm³/day. (ii) The daily energy generation in MJ/day (use LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg, density of CH₄ at STP = 0.717 kg/Nm³). (iii) The OLR if the digester volume is 2000 m³ and influent VS = 30,000 mg/L. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:** > **Stage 1: Hydrolysis (1.5 marks)** — Polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are hydrolysed by extracellular enzymes (amylases, cellulases, proteases, lipases) secreted by hydrolytic bacteria (*Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*) into soluble monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). Rate-limiting for solid wastes. > > **Stage 2: Acidogenesis (1.5 marks)** — Monomers are fermented by acidogenic bacteria (*Clostridium*, *Bacillus*) into VFAs (acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂. Rapid process; pH drops if not buffered. > > **Stage 3: Acetogenesis (2 marks)** — VFAs (C₃+) and alcohols are oxidized to acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by OHPA (*Syntrophomonas*, *Syntrophobacter*). Reactions are endergonic at standard conditions; they proceed only when H₂ partial pressure is < 10⁻⁴ atm — this is **interspecies hydrogen transfer (IHT)**, a syntrophic coupling with methanogens. > > **Stage 4: Methanogenesis (2 marks)** — Methanogenic Archaea convert substrates to CH₄. Two pathways: > - **Acetoclastic (~70%):** CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ (carried by *Methanosaeta*, *Methanosarcina*). > - **Hydrogenotrophic (~30%):** CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O (carried by *Methanobacterium*). > > **Conclusion:** The process is a tightly coupled cascade; disruption at any stage (e.g., VFA accumulation from too fast acidogenesis vs. slow methanogenesis) leads to process failure. **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:** > **(i) Daily methane production — 3 marks** > > Daily COD removed: > $$m_{COD} = Q \cdot C_{COD} \cdot \eta = 500\ m^3/d \cdot 50\ kg/m^3 \cdot 0.80 = 20{,}000\ kg\ COD/d$$ > > Methane yield (theoretical): > $$Y_{CH_4} = 0.350\ Nm^3/kg\ COD$$ > > Total CH₄ produced: > $$V_{CH_4} = 20{,}000 \times 0.350 = 7{,}000\ Nm^3/d$$ > > **(ii) Daily energy generation — 2 marks** > > Mass of methane: > $$m_{CH_4} = 7{,}000 \times 0.717 = 5{,}019\ kg/d$$ > > Energy: > $$E = 5{,}019 \times 50 = 250{,}950\ MJ/d \approx 2.51 \times 10^5\ MJ/d$$ > > **(iii) OLR — 2 marks** > > $$OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_{VS}}{V_{reactor}} = \dfrac{500 \times 30}{2000} = 7.5\ kg\ VS/m^3 \cdot d$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the working of a **CSTR-based biogas plant** including feed preparation, digester operation, gas collection, and digestate handling. List the major operating parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **theoretical methane yield (0.35 Nm³ CH₄/kg COD)** from first principles. Discuss how this yield is **modified in practice** for proteins, lipids, and complex solid waste. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:** > **Flowchart (3 marks)** — Refer to the Block Diagram in Section 4.2 (Mermaid block diagram). > > **Working description (3 marks):** > - **Feed prep:** Substrate is ground, mixed with water to 5–10% TS, fed to digester. > - **Digester operation:** Sealed, heated (35–37°C mesophilic or 50–55°C thermophilic), continuously mixed. > - **Microbial action:** Four stages occur in one tank; HRT typically 20–40 days. > - **Gas collection:** Biogas accumulates in dome, desulfurized, stored, sent to CHP. > - **Digestate handling:** Overflow → dewatered → liquid as biofertilizer, solid as compost. > > **Operating parameters (1 mark):** > - Temperature, pH (6.8–7.5), HRT/SRT, OLR, C:N ratio (20–30), mixing intensity. **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:** > **Derivation (4 marks):** See Section 3.1, Steps 1–5. Key result: > $$Y_{CH_4} = 0.350\ Nm^3/kg\ COD_{removed}$$ > > **Practical modifications (3 marks):** > - **Proteins:** Yield is ~0.50 Nm³ CH₄/kg COD (theoretically higher) but reduced by ammonia inhibition at high TAN (> 3000 mg/L); practical yield ~0.35–0.40. > - **Lipids:** Highest theoretical yield (~0.88 Nm³ CH₄/kg VS); but inhibited by long-chain fatty acids (LCFA); practical yield 0.6–0.8. > - **Complex solids (lignocellulose):** Hydrolysis is rate-limiting; pretreatment (thermal, mechanical, enzymatic) needed; practical yield drops to 0.20–0.30 Nm³/kg VS. > - **Real digesters** operate at 70–90% of theoretical yield due to losses, gas leakage, dissolved CH₄, and microbial decay. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting the H₂O term** in the Buswell equation — students often write only products, losing **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing STP (0°C) and NTP (25°C)** — the methane yield constant changes (0.373 vs 0.350 Nm³/kg COD). Always state the reference condition. > 3. **Mixing up HRT and SRT** — HRT = hydraulic residence time; SRT = solids residence time. In CSTR they are equal, but in UASB they differ greatly. > 4. **Ignoring free ammonia (NH₃) inhibition** in protein-rich substrates — a complete AD design must check TAN and FA concentrations. > 5. **Neglecting CH₄ dissolved in liquid effluent** — at 35°C, up to 8% of CH₄ produced can be lost this way; this affects mass-balance questions. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Anaerobic digestion** = biological conversion of organic waste → **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** + **digestate**, in **absence of O₂**. - **Four stages**: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. - **Rate-limiting step**: Hydrolysis (solids) or Methanogenesis (soluble). - **Buswell equation** gives stoichiometry for any CₙHₐOᵦNᵧSₓ substrate. - **Methane yield** = 0.350 Nm³/kg COD removed (theoretical) — at **0°C, 1 atm (STP)**. - **Methane fraction**: Carbohydrates ~50%, Proteins ~50%, Lipids ~55–60%. - **Energy content** of CH₄: LHV = 50 MJ/kg = 35.8 MJ/Nm³. - **Optimal conditions**: T = 35–37°C (mesophilic) or 50–55°C (thermophilic); pH = 6.8–7.5; C:N = 20–30:1; OLR = 1–5 kg VS/m³·d (CSTR) or up to 30 (UASB). - **Inhibitors**: NH₃ (> 3000 mg/L TAN), H₂S, LCFA, heavy metals, O₂ ingress. - **Reactor types**: CSTR (low-rate, high HRT), UASB/EGSB (high-rate, low HRT), Two-stage (phase-separated). - **Energy applications**: CHP for electricity + heat, direct boiler firing, upgraded to biomethane. - **KTU-favorite equations**: Buswell, COD-based yield, OLR, free ammonia equilibrium, pH buffering by bicarbonate.

topics.title: Cellulose degradation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cellulose degradation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cellulose Degradation** is the process of cleaving the β-1,4-glycosidic bonds of the cellulose polymer chain (a linear homopolymer of D-glucopyranose units) to produce shorter oligosaccharides, cellobiose, or ultimately monomeric glucose, using chemical, physical, enzymatic, or biological methods. In the context of **Waste and Byproducts Utilization**, cellulose degradation is the foundational **unit operation** that unlocks the fermentable sugar fraction locked inside lignocellulosic agricultural residues (rice straw, sugarcane bagasse, corn stover, sawdust, cotton waste, etc.). Without effective degradation, the **theoretical glucose yield** of waste biomass cannot be realized into value-added bioproducts. **Cellulose** is the most abundant biopolymer on Earth, with an estimated annual biosynthetic production of approximately **1.0 × 10¹¹ tonnes/year**. The repeating unit is **cellobiose** (two glucose units linked β-1,4), giving the polymer a formal chemical formula of **(C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ**, where *n* is the degree of polymerization. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Pearl Necklace":** > Imagine cellulose as a very long, tightly knotted pearl necklace, where each pearl is a glucose molecule and each knot is a β-1,4-glycosidic bond. > - **Amorphous regions** of the chain are like a loose, tangled heap of necklaces — easy to pull apart. > - **Crystalline regions** are like multiple necklaces braided together and sealed in wax — very hard to break. > - **Acid hydrolysis** is like throwing the whole necklace into strong acid — it cuts the knots indiscriminately everywhere, but also damages the pearls. > - **Enzymatic hydrolysis** is like hiring a careful jeweller with three specialized tools (endoglucanase, exoglucanase, β-glucosidase) who snips the necklace cleanly, pearl by pearl, without breaking the pearls. > - **Pretreatment** is like first soaking the wax-sealed braids in warm water to soften them, so the jeweller can work faster. ## 1.3 Physical & Chemical Constants of Native Cellulose | Parameter | Value / Range | |---|---| | Molecular weight of one anhydroglucose unit (AGU) | **162 g/mol** | | Repeating unit (cellobiose) | **342 g/mol** | | Native Degree of Polymerization (DP) | **10,000 – 15,000** | | Density (crystalline) | **1.55 – 1.59 g/cm³** | | Theoretical glucose yield from pure cellulose | **1.111 g glucose / g cellulose** | | Bond energy of β-1,4-glycosidic linkage | **~ 320 kJ/mol** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Features Governing Degradability Cellulose resistance to degradation arises from its **hierarchical architecture**: - **Level 1 — Molecular:** β-1,4-glycosidic bond is stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding (O3-H···O5 and O2-H···O6), forming a rigid linear chain. - **Level 2 — Supramolecular:** Chains pack via intermolecular H-bonds (O6-H···O3) into **crystalline microfibrils** interspersed with **amorphous domains**. - **Level 3 — Morphological:** Microfibrils are encased by **hemicellulose** and cemented by **lignin** in the plant cell wall (Lignocellulosic Complex). > [!TIP] > **Why does this matter for the KTU examiner?** > Any degradation strategy must address (a) bond cleavage, (b) decrystallization, and (c) lignin/hemicellulose removal. This 3-tier resistance concept is a favourite 7-mark answer. ## 2.2 Degradation Pathways — Operational Logic ### A. Acid Hydrolysis (Chemical) 1. **Protonation** of the glycosidic oxygen by H⁺ (Brønsted acid catalysis). 2. **Heterolytic cleavage** of the C–O bond → formation of a cyclic **oxocarbenium ion** intermediate. 3. **Nucleophilic attack by water** → release of free sugar and regeneration of H⁺. - **Dilute acid** (0.5–5 % H₂SO₄, 120–200 °C): favours the amorphous fraction; faster kinetics, more sugar degradation byproducts (HMF, furfural). - **Concentrated acid** (30–70 % H₂SO₄ or HCl, < 100 °C): dissolves crystalline cellulose; milder thermal load, but acid-recovery cost is high. ### B. Enzymatic Hydrolysis (Biochemical) The complete **cellulase enzyme system** comprises three synergistically acting classes: - **Endoglucanase (EG, EC 3.2.1.4):** Randomly hydrolyses internal β-1,4 bonds in the **amorphous** regions, generating new chain ends. - **Exoglucanase / Cellobiohydrolase (CBH, EC 3.2.1.91/176):** Processively attacks chain ends, releasing **cellobiose**. - **β-Glucosidase (BGL, EC 3.2.1.21):** Hydrolyses cellobiose → 2 glucose; alleviates end-product inhibition of CBH. ### C. Biological & Physical Pretreatment - **Mechanical:** Milling, grinding, extrusion (reduces particle size, increases surface area). - **Physicochemical:** Steam explosion, AFEX (Ammonia Fibre Explosion), CO₂ explosion, organosolv. - **Biological:** White-rot fungi (lignin peroxidase, manganese peroxidase), brown-rot, soft-rot. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Variables / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cellulose mass balance | $C_0 \rightarrow C \rightarrow G \rightarrow D$ | $C_0$ = initial cellulose, $C$ = residual cellulose, $G$ = glucose, $D$ = degradation products | | 2 | First-order hydrolysis rate | $-\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C$ | $k$ = first-order rate constant (min⁻¹) | | 3 | Integrated first-order | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C}\right) = k \cdot t$ | Linear plot of $\ln(C_0/C)$ vs. $t$ gives slope $k$ | | 4 | Glucose yield (glu yield) | $Y_G = \dfrac{[G]_t}{C_0} \times 1.111$ | Stoichiometric factor **1.111 g glucose / g cellulose** | | 5 | Saeman's two-step model | $C \xrightarrow{k_1} G \xrightarrow{k_2} D$ | $k_1$ (cellulose → glucose), $k_2$ (glucose → HMF/levulinic acid) | | 6 | Theoretical max glucose conc. | $G_{\max} = 1.111 \times C_0$ | For 100 % conversion efficiency | | 7 | Crystallinity Index (XRD) | $CrI = \dfrac{I_{002} - I_{am}}{I_{002}} \times 100$ | $I_{002}$ ≈ 22.5°, $I_{am}$ ≈ 18° (Segal method) | | 8 | Degree of Polymerization | $DP = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{162}$ | $\overline{M}_n$ = number-average MW; AGU MW = **162 g/mol** | | 9 | Degree of Substitution (for cellulose derivatives) | $DS = \dfrac{\text{moles of substituent}}{\text{moles of AGU}}$ | Range **0 ≤ DS ≤ 3** (three free –OH groups) | | 10 | Michaelis–Menten (enzyme) | $v = \dfrac{V_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | $V_{\max}$ = max rate, $K_m$ = substrate affinity | | 11 | Arrhenius temperature dependence | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $E_a$ ≈ 110–150 kJ/mol for cellulose acid hydrolysis | | 12 | Synergy factor (cellulase) | $SF = \dfrac{\text{combined activity}}{\sum \text{individual activities}}$ | $SF > 1$ confirms synergism | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Kinetic Model for Acid Hydrolysis **Starting assumption:** The cleavage of β-1,4-glycosidic bonds is a pseudo-homogeneous reaction with respect to acid (used in large excess), so the rate is first-order in cellulose concentration only. **Step 1.** Write the differential rate law. $$ -\frac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C $$ **Step 2.** Separate variables. $$ \frac{dC}{C} = -k \cdot dt $$ **Step 3.** Integrate both sides from $C_0$ at $t=0$ to $C$ at $t=t$. $$ \int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt $$ **Step 4.** Evaluate the integrals. $$ \left[ \ln C \right]_{C_0}^{C} = -k \cdot t $$ $$ \ln(C) - \ln(C_0) = -k \cdot t $$ **Step 5.** Final integrated form. $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = k \cdot t $$ $$ \boxed{\,C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}\,} $$ > **Engineering interpretation:** A *semilog plot* of residual cellulose (or $C_0/C$) vs. time gives a straight line whose slope is the rate constant $k$. Faster hydrolysis → steeper slope. This linearization is **exactly** what a KTU examiner expects in a 7-mark numerical. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Saeman's Two-Step Sequential Model This is the **canonical KTU model** for dilute-acid hydrolysis where glucose is itself unstable at the reaction temperature and degrades to HMF, levulinic acid, and formic acid. **Reactions:** $$ \text{Cellulose} \xrightarrow{k_1} \text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{Degradation products (HMF, etc.)} $$ **Step 1.** Rate of cellulose disappearance. $$ -\frac{dC}{dt} = k_1 \cdot C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C(t) = C_0 \, e^{-k_1 t} $$ **Step 2.** Rate of glucose formation (net) — glucose is **produced** from cellulose and **consumed** to degradation products. $$ \frac{dG}{dt} = k_1 \cdot C - k_2 \cdot G $$ **Step 3.** Substitute $C(t)$ from Step 1. $$ \frac{dG}{dt} = k_1 \, C_0 \, e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 \, G $$ **Step 4.** Solve this linear first-order ODE using integrating factor $e^{k_2 t}$. $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left( G \cdot e^{k_2 t} \right) = k_1 C_0 \, e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} $$ **Step 5.** Integrate from 0 to $t$, applying $G(0) = 0$. $$ G(t) \cdot e^{k_2 t} = \frac{k_1 C_0}{k_2 - k_1}\!\left( e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} - 1 \right) $$ **Step 6.** Final closed-form solution. $$ \boxed{\,G(t) = \frac{k_1 \, C_0}{k_2 - k_1}\!\left( e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t} \right)\,} $$ > **Key engineering insight:** The maximum glucose concentration occurs at the **optimal residence time** $$ t_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1} $$ Beyond $t_{\text{opt}}$, the second reaction (sugar degradation) dominates and yield drops. KTU examiners often ask the student to derive or quote this $t_{\text{opt}}$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Glucose Yield & Conversion Efficiency Define the **fractional cellulose conversion** as $X = \dfrac{C_0 - C(t)}{C_0}$. **Step 1.** From the first-order model, $C(t) = C_0 e^{-k_1 t}$, so: $$ X = 1 - e^{-k_1 t} $$ **Step 2.** The stoichiometric limit — every anhydroglucose unit (162 g/mol) liberates one free glucose (180 g/mol) on hydrolysis. $$ \text{Mass ratio} = \frac{180}{162} = 1.111 $$ **Step 3.** Therefore the **actual glucose concentration** is: $$ G_{\text{actual}}(t) = 1.111 \cdot C_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k_1 t}) \cdot e^{-k_2 t} $$ (The final $e^{-k_2 t}$ term accounts for loss to HMF/degradation.) **Step 4.** Overall **glucose yield efficiency**: $$ \eta_G = \frac{G_{\text{actual}}}{G_{\text{theoretical}}} = \frac{G_{\text{actual}}}{1.111 \, C_0} \times 100 \% $$ > A KTU board-level numerical will typically give values of $C_0$, $k_1$, $k_2$, and $t$, and ask you to compute $X$, $G(t)$, and $\eta_G$. Practice this three-step loop until it becomes second nature. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Board Pattern) **Problem:** A 1 % (w/v) cellulose slurry is hydrolysed with dilute H₂SO₄ at 160 °C. Given $k_1 = 0.05 \,\text{min}^{-1}$ and $k_2 = 0.01 \,\text{min}^{-1}$, find (a) optimum time, (b) maximum glucose concentration, (c) overall yield at $t_{\text{opt}}$. ($C_0 = 10\,\text{g/L}$). **Solution:** **(a) Optimum time** $$ t_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(0.01/0.05)}{0.01 - 0.05} = \frac{\ln(0.2)}{-0.04} = \frac{-1.6094}{-0.04} = 40.24 \,\text{min} $$ **(b) Maximum glucose concentration** (substitute into Saeman's equation) $$ G_{\max} = \frac{0.05 \times 10}{0.01 - 0.05}\left(e^{-0.05 \times 40.24} - e^{-0.01 \times 40.24}\right) $$ $$ = \frac{0.5}{-0.04}\left(e^{-2.012} - e^{-0.4024}\right) $$ $$ = -12.5 \left(0.1339 - 0.6688\right) = -12.5 \times (-0.5349) = 6.686 \,\text{g/L} $$ **(c) Overall glucose yield efficiency** $$ \eta_G = \frac{6.686}{1.111 \times 10} \times 100 = \frac{6.686}{11.11} \times 100 = 60.18 \,\% $$ > **Valuation insight:** KTU examiners reward *unit consistency* (min vs. h), correct *sign handling* in Saeman's model, and a clear final *physical interpretation* (e.g., "60 % of the theoretical maximum glucose is recovered, with the rest lost to HMF/levulinic acid side reactions"). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Lignocellulosic Biomass Degradation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Biomass Feedstock
Rice straw, Bagasse, Sawdust] --> B[Mechanical Size Reduction
Milling, Grinding] B --> C[Pretreatment Stage
Physical / Chemical / Biological] C --> D[Lignocellulosic Matrix
Decrystallization, Delignification] D --> E[Hydrolysis Step
Acid OR Enzymatic] E --> F[Sugar Stream
Glucose + Cellobiose + Xylose] F --> G[Fermentation OR Catalytic Conversion] G --> H[Value-Added Products
Bioethanol, Bioplastics, Platform Chemicals] F --> I[Residual Lignin Stream
Heat, Power, Phenolics] E --> J[Inhibitor Stream
HMF, Furfural, Acetic Acid] J --> K[Detoxification Unit
Overliming, Adsorption] K --> F ``` ## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Mechanism of Enzymatic Cellulose Degradation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CRY[Crystalline Cellulose Microfibril] direction LR c1[Glucose Unit 1] --- c2[Glucose Unit 2] --- c3[Glucose Unit 3] --- c4[Glucose Unit 4] --- c5[Glucose Unit 5] --- c6[Glucose Unit 6] end c1 --> eA[Step 1: Endoglucanase Attack
Random Internal Cleavage in Amorphous Region] eA --> eB[Step 2: New Chain Ends Created
Exoglucanase CBH Binds] eB --> eC[Step 3: Cellobiohydrolase Action
Processive Release of Cellobiose] eC --> eD[Step 4: Cellobiose to Glucose
Beta-Glucosidase Hydrolysis] eD --> eE[Final Product: D-Glucose Monomers] eE --> eF[Fermentation to Bioethanol
or Other Platform Chemicals] ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Flow — Acid vs Enzymatic Hydrolysis ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Cellulose Source] --> Choice{Route Selection} Choice -- Acid Hydrolysis --> A1[Acid Concentration 0.5 to 70 percent] A1 --> A2[High Temperature 100 to 200 C] A2 --> A3[Fast Kinetics, Low Capital for Dilute Acid] A3 --> A4[Drawback: Inhibitor Formation HMF, Furfural] Choice -- Enzymatic Hydrolysis --> E1[Mild Conditions 45 to 55 C, pH 4.8] E1 --> E2[Cellulase Cocktail EG, CBH, BGL] E2 --> E3[Slow Kinetics, High Enzyme Cost] E3 --> E4[Drawback: End Product Inhibition by Cellobiose] A4 --> End[Neutralization and Detoxification] E4 --> End End --> Final[Fermentable Sugar Hydrolysate] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Saeman's Two-Step Reaction Network ```mermaid flowchart LR C0[Initial Cellulose C0] -- k1 First Step --> G[Intermediate Glucose Pool G t] G -- k2 Second Step --> D[Degradation Products HMF, Levulinic Acid] G -- Fermentation --> P[Bioethanol or Platform Chemical] subgraph Kinetic Balance direction LR C0 -. Mass Balance Check .-> G G -. Mass Balance Check .-> D end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Explain the hierarchical structure of cellulose and discuss how each level of structure affects its degradation. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]` **Model Answer:** - **Molecular level:** Linear β-1,4-glucan with intramolecular H-bonds (O3-H···O5, O2-H···O6) stabilises the chain and reduces flexibility. - **Supramolecular level:** Intermolecular H-bonds (O6-H···O3) pack chains into crystalline microfibrils; amorphous regions are more accessible to water, acid, or enzymes. - **Morphological level:** Lignin and hemicellulose sheath physically encases cellulose microfibrils, blocking access. - **Effect on degradation:** Crystallinity and lignin barrier are the two main rate-limiting factors; any effective degradation strategy must address both via pretreatment and hydrolysis. ### Question 2 **With a neat sketch, describe the synergistic action of the cellulase enzyme system. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 model]` **Model Answer:** The cellulase system has three enzyme classes acting in synergy: - **Endoglucanase (EG, EC 3.2.1.4):** Randomly cleaves internal β-1,4 bonds in amorphous regions, creating new chain ends. - **Exoglucanase / Cellobiohydrolase (CBH, EC 3.2.1.91/176):** Processively acts on chain ends to release cellobiose units. - **β-Glucosidase (BGL, EC 3.2.1.21):** Converts cellobiose to two glucose molecules, preventing product inhibition of CBH. - **Synergy factor** $SF > 1$ confirms the combined activity exceeds the sum of individual enzyme activities, making this combination industrially essential. ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) **(a) Derive the first-order kinetic model for dilute-acid hydrolysis of cellulose, stating all assumptions. (7 Marks)** **(b) A cellulose slurry ($C_0 = 25\,\text{g/L}$) is hydrolysed at 160 °C with $k_1 = 0.08\,\text{min}^{-1}$ and $k_2 = 0.015\,\text{min}^{-1}$. Calculate (i) optimum residence time, (ii) maximum glucose concentration, and (iii) overall yield. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) 1. **Assumption 1:** Acid is in large excess, so the reaction is pseudo-first-order in cellulose. 2. **Assumption 2:** The reaction proceeds homogeneously (well-mixed slurry). 3. **Assumption 3:** No mass-transfer limitation (ideally after pretreatment). 4. **Rate law:** $-\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k_1 \cdot C$. 5. **Separation of variables:** $\dfrac{dC}{C} = -k_1 \, dt$. 6. **Integration:** $\int_{C_0}^{C} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k_1 \int_0^t dt$. 7. **Result:** $\ln(C_0 / C) = k_1 t$ → $C(t) = C_0 e^{-k_1 t}$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(i) Optimum residence time** $$ t_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1} = \frac{\ln(0.015/0.08)}{0.015 - 0.08} = \frac{\ln(0.1875)}{-0.065} = \frac{-1.6740}{-0.065} = 25.75 \,\text{min} $$ **(ii) Maximum glucose concentration** (Saeman's formula) $$ G_{\max} = \frac{k_1 C_0}{k_2 - k_1}\!\left(e^{-k_1 t_{\text{opt}}} - e^{-k_2 t_{\text{opt}}}\right) $$ $$ = \frac{0.08 \times 25}{0.015 - 0.08}\left(e^{-0.08 \times 25.75} - e^{-0.015 \times 25.75}\right) $$ $$ = \frac{2.0}{-0.065}\left(e^{-2.060} - e^{-0.3863}\right) = -30.77 \times (0.1275 - 0.6796) $$ $$ = -30.77 \times (-0.5521) = 16.99 \,\text{g/L} $$ **(iii) Overall glucose yield efficiency** $$ \eta_G = \frac{16.99}{1.111 \times 25} \times 100 = \frac{16.99}{27.78} \times 100 = 61.16 \,\% $$ > **Interpretation:** At the optimum residence time of 25.75 min, a maximum glucose concentration of 16.99 g/L is achievable, corresponding to a 61.16 % overall yield. Operating beyond this time increases HMF formation and lowers sugar recovery. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) **(a) Discuss in detail the different pretreatment methods employed for lignocellulosic biomass, highlighting their effect on cellulose accessibility. (7 Marks)** **(b) Explain Saeman's two-step model for cellulose hydrolysis. Derive the expression for optimum residence time and discuss its industrial significance. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Physical Pretreatment:** Mechanical comminution (ball milling, hammer milling, extrusion) reduces crystallinity and particle size, increasing accessible surface area. No chemical modification; high energy input. **2. Chemical Pretreatment:** - **Dilute acid** (H₂SO₄, 0.5–5 %, 120–200 °C): solubilises hemicellulose, disrupts lignin seal. - **Alkaline** (NaOH, Ca(OH)₂, ammonia): swells cellulose, removes lignin via saponification. - **Organosolv** (ethanol/water): extracts lignin with organic solvent. - **Ozonolysis:** Uses ozone to degrade lignin selectively. **3. Physicochemical Pretreatment:** - **Steam Explosion:** High-pressure saturated steam (160–240 °C, 1–5 MPa) followed by rapid decompression → explosive defibration. - **AFEX:** Liquid ammonia at 60–100 °C, high pressure, then sudden release. - **CO₂ Explosion:** Supercritical CO₂; milder than steam. **4. Biological Pretreatment:** White-rot fungi (Phanerochaete chrysosporium), brown-rot, soft-rot fungi produce ligninolytic enzymes (LiP, MnP, laccase). Low energy, slow, mild. **Effect on cellulose accessibility:** Pretreatment **disrupts the lignin-hemicellulose shield**, **reduces crystallinity**, and **increases pore volume**, all of which enhance downstream enzymatic or acid hydrolysis rates by 4–10 fold. #### Model Solution — Part (b) Saeman's two-step model accounts for both **sugar generation** and **sugar degradation**: $$ \text{Cellulose} \xrightarrow{k_1} \text{Glucose} \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{HMF} \rightarrow \text{levulinic acid + formic acid} $$ The rate equations are: - $dC/dt = -k_1 C$ - $dG/dt = k_1 C - k_2 G$ **Derivation of optimum time:** Differentiate the Saeman's closed-form solution $$ G(t) = \frac{k_1 C_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) $$ with respect to $t$ and set to zero: $$ \frac{dG}{dt} = \frac{k_1 C_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(-k_1 e^{-k_1 t} + k_2 e^{-k_2 t}\right) = 0 $$ Solving for $t$: $$ k_1 e^{-k_1 t} = k_2 e^{-k_2 t} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{(k_2 - k_1)t} = \frac{k_2}{k_1} $$ $$ \boxed{\,t_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln(k_2 / k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}\,} $$ **Industrial significance:** - Reactor designers use $t_{\text{opt}}$ to size plug-flow or CSTR reactors, maximizing fermentable sugar yield. - Operating beyond $t_{\text{opt}}$ leads to fermentation-inhibiting byproducts (HMF > 1 g/L inhibits *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*). - Coupling with rapid quenching and detoxification ensures downstream fermentability. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers** > 1. **Sign error in Saeman's equation:** Students often miss the negative sign in $(k_2 - k_1)$ denominator and get a negative glucose concentration. Always check that the result is **physically positive**. > 2. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert *hours* to *minutes* (or vice versa) when using $k$ in min⁻¹. Always declare units in Step 1. > 3. **Forgetting the 1.111 stoichiometric factor:** A 1 g cellulose yields 1.111 g glucose, **not** 1 g. Examiners test this explicitly. > 4. **Omitting the synergy factor** when describing cellulase — a 3-mark question requires all three enzymes *and* the synergy concept. > 5. **Confusing HMF, furfural, and levulinic acid origins:** HMF comes from hexoses (glucose), furfural from pentoses (xylose), and levulinic acid is a secondary dehydration product of HMF. Mixing these up is a sure mark loser. > 6. **Vague answers on pretreatment:** Do not just list methods — always link the method to *which barrier* it removes (lignin, hemicellulose, or crystallinity). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cellulose Degradation** - **Cellulose = (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ**, repeating unit is **cellobiose (342 g/mol)**, AGU mass **162 g/mol**, DP **10,000–15,000**. - The **β-1,4-glycosidic bond** is the chemical target of all degradation strategies. - **Three-tier resistance:** (i) crystalline microfibrils, (ii) hemicellulose sheath, (iii) lignin cement. - **Acid hydrolysis mechanism:** protonation → oxocarbenium ion → water attack → sugar + H⁺. - **Enzymatic hydrolysis** uses **EG + CBH + BGL** with **synergy factor > 1**. - **First-order kinetics:** $C(t) = C_0 e^{-k_1 t}$ (acid hydrolysis, dilute conditions). - **Saeman's two-step model:** $G(t) = \dfrac{k_1 C_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right)$. - **Optimum residence time:** $t_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{k_2 - k_1}$ — the heart of KTU board numericals. - **Glucose yield = 1.111 × cellulose mass** (stoichiometric conversion factor). - **Pretreatment classes:** Physical, Chemical (acid/alkaline/organosolv/ozone), Physicochemical (steam explosion/AFEX), Biological (fungi). - **Factors affecting enzymatic hydrolysis:** crystallinity, DP, lignin content, accessible surface area, enzyme loading, temperature (45–55 °C), pH (4.5–5.0), end-product inhibition. - **Inhibitors of fermentation:** HMF (from hexoses), furfural (from pentoses), acetic acid (from hemicellulose deacetylation), levulinic acid, formic acid. - **Crystallinity Index (Segal method):** $CrI = \dfrac{I_{002} - I_{am}}{I_{002}} \times 100$ % (XRD peaks at 22.5° and 18°). - **Michaelis–Menten kinetics:** $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ applies to single-enzyme kinetics. - **Arrhenius dependence:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$, with $E_a \approx 110–150$ kJ/mol for cellulose acid hydrolysis. - **Industrial relevance:** Bioethanol (2G), bio-PET, lactic acid, succinic acid, microbial cellulose, pulp & paper, animal feed, and biogas via coupled fermentation.

topics.title: Fermentation process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fermentation process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Official Terminology):**
> *Fermentation is an enzyme-catalyzed, energy-yielding metabolic process in which organic substrates (chiefly carbohydrates) are transformed by microorganisms — under anaerobic or micro-aerobic conditions — into alcohols, organic acids, gases, or other value-added biochemical products, while regenerating oxidized cofactors such as NAD⁺ to sustain glycolysis.*

In the context of **PGAET753 (Waste and Byproduct Utilization)**, fermentation is the **central biological unit operation** used to convert agri-food waste, lignocellulosic biomass, dairy whey, molasses, and municipal organic waste into **biofuels (ethanol, bio-butanol), platform chemicals (lactic acid, citric acid), enzymes, biopolymers (PHAs), and Single Cell Protein (SCP).**

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Idli" Intuition 🍲

Imagine a sealed pressure cooker (the *fermenter*) into which you pour a batter of rice + dal (the *substrate*). Inside, invisible workers (*microbes — yeast or bacteria*) consume the sugars, and because no fresh air is allowed in, they respire differently. They release two things: **alcohol/CO₂** (which makes cooked rice soft and fluffy by trapping gas bubbles) and **heat**. This is exactly what happens in a fermenter — microbes "eat" waste sugars and "breathe out" useful industrial products, all inside a controlled, sealed vessel.

| Terminology | Kitchen Analogy | Industrial Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Batter / Rice | Sugar / Glucose substrate | Molasses, whey, starch hydrolysate |
| Yeast / Bacteria | Invisible workers | *Saccharomyces, Lactobacillus, Aspergillus* |
| Pressure Cooker | Sealed vessel | Bioreactor / Fermenter |
| CO₂ bubbles | Fluffy texture | Off-gas stream (collected) |
| Alcohol produced | Slight sour smell | Bioethanol / Bio-butanol product |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Answers:**
> - **Narrow (Biochemical) Sense:** Strictly *anaerobic* glycolysis regenerating NAD⁺.
> - **Industrial (Process Engineering) Sense:** *Any* controlled microbial cultivation — aerobic or anaerobic — for producing a useful product. **Always use the broader industrial meaning** unless the question specifies "metabolic pathway."

---

## 1.2 Historical Context & Scope

The term was coined by **Louis Pasteur (1857)** after observing that French wine was turning to vinegar. Pasteur described fermentation as *"la vie sans l'air"* (life without air). In modern waste-to-value engineering, fermentation spans from traditional food fermentations (kimchi, dosa, curd) to advanced **2G bio-refineries** that produce ethanol from agricultural residues.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Memorize for KTU)

- **Theoretical ethanol yield from glucose:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (**51.1 % w/w**)
- **Theoretical CO₂ yield from glucose:** **0.489 g CO₂ / g glucose**
- **Theoretical biomass yield (Yₓ/s):** **0.5 g DCW / g substrate**
- **Operating temperature (mesophilic yeast):** **30 °C ± 2 °C**
- **Optimal pH (yeast):** **4.5 – 5.0**
- **Typical fermentation cycle time:** **48 – 72 hours**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fermentation Processes

The classification can be done on **three orthogonal axes** — a common KTU question structure.

### A) Based on Oxygen Requirement

| Type | Oxygen Status | Typical Microbe | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anaerobic** | Strictly absent | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | Ethanol from molasses |
| **Micro-aerobic** | Trace O₂ (< 0.5 ppm) | *Saccharomyces* (crabtree) | Beer brewing |
| **Aerobic** | Continuous sparging | *Aspergillus niger* | Citric acid production |
| **Facultative** | Switchable | *E. coli, Zymomonas* | Recombinant protein |

### B) Based on Physical State of Medium

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked classification** in KTU Module 1.

1. **Submerged Fermentation (SmF)**
   - Free-flowing liquid broth in stirred-tank or airlift bioreactor.
   - Used for: ethanol, lactic acid, beer, antibiotics.
   - Advantages: better heat/mass transfer, easy scale-up.
   - Disadvantages: high water footprint, expensive downstream.

2. **Solid-State Fermentation (SSF)**
   - Microbes grow on moist solid substrate (no free water); water activity (aᵥ) ≈ 0.4 – 0.8.
   - Used for: enzyme production (amylase, cellulase), tempeh, biopulping, compost teas.
   - **Particularly relevant for waste utilization** because it directly ferments solid agri-residues (rice bran, wheat bran, sugarcane bagasse, pressmud) without costly liquefaction.

### C) Based on Product / Pathway

| Pathway | End Products | Net Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Alcoholic** | Ethanol + CO₂ | C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ |
| **Lactic Acid (Homolactic)** | 2 Lactic acid | C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 CH₃CHOHCOOH |
| **Lactic Acid (Heterolactic)** | Lactate + Ethanol + CO₂ | C₆H₁₂O₆ → CH₃CHOHCOOH + C₂H₅OH + CO₂ |
| **Acetic Acid** | Acetic acid + H₂O | C₂H₅OH + O₂ → CH₃COOH + H₂O |
| **Butyric / ABE** | Acetone-Butanol-Ethanol | 5 C₆H₁₂O₆ → 4 Butanol + 2 Acetone + 8 CO₂ + 10 H₂ |
| **Mixed-Acid (Enteric)** | Lactate + Acetate + Formate + CO₂ + H₂ | (Used in biogas precursors) |

## 2.2 The Central Metabolic Logic (Why Fermentation Works)

Cells oxidize glucose to harvest energy (ATP) and reducing power (NADH). In the absence of a terminal electron acceptor like O₂, NADH cannot be re-oxidized through the Electron Transport Chain. So microbes use an **organic intermediate** as the electron acceptor — this is fermentation.

1. **Step 1 — Glycolysis (EMP pathway):** Glucose (6C) → 2 Pyruvate (3C) + 2 ATP + 2 NADH
2. **Step 2 — NADH regeneration:** Pyruvate (or its derivative acetaldehyde) is reduced using NADH, regenerating NAD⁺ so that glycolysis can continue.
3. **Step 3 — Product formation:** The reduced product — ethanol, lactate, etc. — is exported from the cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The ATP yield of fermentation is only **2 ATP/glucose**, vs. **38 ATP** for full aerobic respiration. Industrialists still use fermentation because the *product value* (e.g., ethanol, antibiotics, enzymes) far outweighs the low energy yield.

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters (CPP) Affecting Fermentation

These are **CPPs / KPAs** for which KTU expects control strategies:

- **Temperature** — affects enzyme kinetics (Q₁₀ rule ≈ 2); too high → enzyme denaturation, too low → low productivity.
- **pH** — affects microbial growth rate and product stability (organic acids drop pH, requiring in-situ neutralization with CaCO₃ or NaOH).
- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** — for aerobic SmF: maintained 20 – 80 % saturation.
- **Substrate concentration** — must avoid catabolite repression / Crabtree effect (typically < 100 g/L glucose for yeast to prevent ethanol inhibition).
- **Water activity (aᵥ)** — critical for SSF; below 0.6 most bacteria stop, but molds still grow.
- **Inoculum size and age** — typically 5 – 10 % v/v of a log-phase seed culture.
- **Shear stress** — high agitation damages mycelial morphology.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these — they appear in nearly every numerical Part B question on fermentation.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{zymase}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + 2\,ATP$ | Gay-Lussac stoichiometry (alcoholic fermentation) |
| 2 | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} \; [\text{g product / g substrate consumed}]$ | Product yield coefficient |
| 3 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} \; [\text{g DCW / g substrate consumed}]$ | Biomass yield coefficient (typical 0.5 for yeast) |
| 4 | $\mu = \dfrac{\ln N_2 - \ln N_1}{t_2 - t_1} \; [\text{h}^{-1}]$ | Specific growth rate from log-phase counts |
| 5 | $X = X_0\,e^{\mu t}$ | Exponential growth equation |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{fermentation}} = \dfrac{Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}}{0.511} \times 100\,[\%]$ | Fermentation efficiency (vs theoretical max) |
| 7 | $P_{\text{ethanol}} = Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}} \times S_0 \times V \times \rho$ | Total ethanol mass produced (kg) |
| 8 | $a_w = \dfrac{P_{\text{water in sample}}}{P^{0}_{\text{pure water}}}$ | Water activity (SSF criterion) |
| 9 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | Temperature coefficient of reaction rate |
| 10 | $r_{P} = q_{P} \cdot X$ | Volumetric productivity = specific rate × biomass density |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In KTU's waste-utilization context, fermentation is the **bio-refinery workhorse**:

- **Molasses / sugarcane juice** → ethanol via *Saccharomyces* (B molasses, India's EBP program).
- **Rice / wheat straw** → 2G ethanol (after dilute-acid pretreatment + enzymatic hydrolysis + fermentation with *Zymomonas mobilis*).
- **Cheese whey** → lactic acid via *Lactobacillus helveticus*; → ethanol via *Kluyveromyces marxianus*.
- **Municipal solid waste (OFMSW)** → biogas (anaerobic digestion is *methanogenic fermentation*).
- **Pressmud** from sugar mills → SCP via *Candida utilis*.
- **Fruit/vegetable waste** → organic acids via *Aspergillus niger* (citric acid SSF).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose (Gay-Lussac Equation)

This is a **stoichiometric derivation** that anchors every ethanol-from-waste problem.

**Given:**
- Molecular weight of glucose: **MW(C₆H₁₂O₆) = 180 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of ethanol: **MW(C₂H₅OH) = 46 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of CO₂: **MW(CO₂) = 44 g/mol**
- Balanced reaction: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$

**Step 1 — Moles of product per mole of glucose.**

$$
n_{\text{ethanol}} = 2 \text{ mol per mol glucose}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of ethanol produced per mole of glucose.**

$$
m_{\text{ethanol}} = 2 \times 46 = 92 \text{ g ethanol per 180 g glucose}
$$

**Step 3 — Yield coefficient (Y_P/S, theoretical).**

$$
Y_{P/S}^{\text{theo}} = \frac{92 \text{ g ethanol}}{180 \text{ g glucose}} = 0.5111 \,\frac{\text{g}}{\text{g}}
$$

**Step 4 — Fermentation efficiency (η).**

$$
\eta = \frac{Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}}{0.5111} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of CO₂ simultaneously released (mass balance check).**

$$
m_{CO_2} = 2 \times 44 = 88 \text{ g per 180 g glucose}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify mass balance.**

$$
\underbrace{180}_{\text{Reactant}} \stackrel{?}{=} \underbrace{92}_{\text{EthOH}} + \underbrace{88}_{\text{CO}_2} = 180 \;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **stoichiometry only** — it does not include the ATP yield (2 ATP/mol glucose) because ATP stays inside the cell and is not part of the mass balance on the fermenter outlet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Ethanol Productivity from a Batch Fermenter

**Statement:** A **500 L** stirred-tank fermenter is charged with **80 g/L glucose**. After **48 h** of batch fermentation, residual glucose is **8 g/L** and ethanol is **34 g/L**. Calculate (a) Y_P/S, (b) fermentation efficiency η, (c) total ethanol mass produced, (d) volumetric productivity r_P.

**Step a — Product yield coefficient (actual).**

$$
Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} = \frac{P_f - P_i}{S_i - S_f} = \frac{34 - 0}{80 - 8} = \frac{34}{72} = 0.472 \;\frac{\text{g}}{\text{g}}
$$

**Step b — Fermentation efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{0.472}{0.511} \times 100\,\% = 92.4\,\%
$$

**Step c — Total ethanol mass.**

$$
m_{\text{ethanol}} = P_f \times V = 34 \,\tfrac{\text{g}}{\text{L}} \times 500 \,\text{L} = 17{,}000 \,\text{g} = 17 \,\text{kg}
$$

**Step d — Volumetric productivity.**

$$
r_P = \frac{P_f}{t} = \frac{34 \,\tfrac{\text{g}}{\text{L}}}{48 \,\text{h}} = 0.708 \,\frac{\text{g}}{\text{L}\cdot\text{h}}
$$

**Result Summary Table**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product yield coefficient | $Y_{P/S}$ | **0.472** | g/g |
| Fermentation efficiency | $\eta$ | **92.4** | % |
| Total ethanol produced | $m_{ethanol}$ | **17.0** | kg |
| Volumetric productivity | $r_P$ | **0.708** | g/(L·h) |

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Energy Yield of Fermentation vs Aerobic Respiration

Glycolysis (EMP pathway) net reaction:

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2\,NAD^+ + 2\,ADP + 2\,P_i \rightarrow 2\,Pyruvate + 2\,NADH + 2\,H^+ + 2\,ATP + 2\,H_2O
$$

In alcoholic fermentation, pyruvate is decarboxylated by **pyruvate decarboxylase** to acetaldehyde + CO₂, then reduced by NADH via **alcohol dehydrogenase** to ethanol.

**Step 1 — ATP from substrate-level phosphorylation (glycolysis investment vs payoff).**

Glycolysis consumes 2 ATP (hexokinase + phosphofructokinase steps) and produces 4 ATP (1,3-BPG → 3-PG and PEP → pyruvate steps).

$$
\text{Net ATP from glycolysis} = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ ATP}
$$

**Step 2 — ATP from fermentation-specific steps.**

No additional ATP; in fact, the decarboxylation step releases CO₂ but not ATP. Alcohol dehydrogenase step has no net ATP.

$$
\text{Net ATP (alcoholic fermentation)} = 2 \text{ ATP per mol glucose}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to aerobic respiration (theoretical 38 ATP via ETC).**

$$
\text{Efficiency ratio} = \frac{2}{38} \approx 5.3\,\%
$$

**Engineering Implication:** Industrial fermentation sacrifices energy yield for **product specificity and redox balance**. Because NAD⁺ is regenerated internally, microbes can operate in environments devoid of oxygen — a critical advantage when handling **pulp-paper black liquor, rice straw hydrolysate, or landfill leachate** where aeration is impractical or uneconomical.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generalized Industrial Fermentation Process

This diagram maps the **engineering flow** of any fermentation-based waste-to-product conversion. Every node is a unit operation that KTU expects you to be able to describe.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["WASTE FEEDSTOCK"] --> nodeB["PRE-TREATMENT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SUBSTRATE HYDROLYSIS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["NUTRIENT BROTH FORMATION"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STERILIZATION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["INOCULUM PREP"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["BIOREACTOR FERMENTATION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["CELL SEPARATION"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["PRODUCT PURIFICATION"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["VALUE ADDED PRODUCT"]
    nodeG --> nodeK["OFF GAS CO2 OR BIOGAS"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["SPENT BROTH WASTEWATER TREATMENT"]
```

**Reading the diagram for KTU answers:**

- The **bioreactor (nodeG)** is the heart of the system.
- **nodeK (off-gas)** recovery is critical for CO₂ credit or biogas upgrading.
- **nodeL (spent broth)** must be treated (anaerobic digestor) before discharge — circular-economy compliance.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selecting Fermentation Type Based on Waste

This flow chart is a **high-yield KTU sketch answer** — examiners reward clear flowcharts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START WASTE STREAM"] --> q1{"IS THE WASTE LIQUID"}
    q1 -- YES --> q2{"TARGET PRODUCT"}
    q1 -- NO --> q3["SOLID STATE FERMENTATION SSF"]
    q2 -- BIOETHANOL --> q4["ANAEROBIC SmF WITH SACCHAROMYCES"]
    q2 -- ORGANIC ACID --> q5["AEROBIC SmF WITH ASPERGILLUS OR RHIZOPUS"]
    q2 -- ENZYMES --> q6["SUBMERGED OR SSF WITH FUNGAL CULTURE"]
    q2 -- METHANE --> q7["ANAEROBIC DIGESTION WITH MIXED CONSORTIA"]
    q3 --> q8{"TARGET ENZYME OR SCP"}
    q8 -- CELLULASE / AMYLASE --> q9["SSF ON TRITICALE OR WHEAT BRAN"]
    q8 -- ORGANIC ACID --> q10["SSF WITH ASPERGILLUS NIGER"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inside a Stirred-Tank Bioreactor

Useful when a KTU question asks to *"draw and label a fermenter."* The Mermaid renderer cannot draw true cylindrical schematics, so we map the **functional subsystems** instead.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL AND INSTRUMENTATION LAYER"]
        sensorA["pH SENSOR"]
        sensorB["DO PROBE"]
        sensorC["TEMPERATURE RTD"]
        sensorD["FOAM SENSOR"]
    end
    subgraph VESSEL["BIOREACTOR VESSEL JACKETED STERILE"]
        media["STERILE BROTH MEDIA"]
        impeller["RUSHTON IMPELLER 2 TO 4"]
        sparger["RING SPARGER FOR AIR OR N2"]
        baffles["FOUR BAFFLES FOR TURBULENCE"]
        samplePort["SAMPLE AND INOCULATION PORT"]
    end
    subgraph UTIL["UTILITIES LAYER"]
        steam["STERILE STEAM INLET 121 C"]
        cool["COOLING WATER JACKET"]
        acidBase["ACID OR BASE PUMP FOR PH CONTROL"]
        antifoam["ANTIFOAM PROBE ADDITION"]
    end
    CTRL --> VESSEL
    UTIL --> VESSEL
    media --> impeller
    impeller --> sparger
    sparger --> baffles
    baffles --> samplePort
```

**Engineering Interpretation for KTU:**

- The **jacket** maintains the 30 °C mesophilic set-point (heat removal is critical because fermentation is exothermic, releasing ~ 26 kJ/g glucose).
- **DO probe** is relevant only for aerobic fermentations (citric acid, antibiotics).
- The **sparger** can switch between air (aerobic) and N₂/CO₂ (anaerobic) — this is how the same vessel handles both modes.
- **Four baffles** prevent vortex formation and improve mixing (impeller Reynolds > 10⁴).

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PGAET753]`

**Define fermentation. List any four industrial products obtained by fermentation of waste biomass.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ≈ 90 words):**

> Fermentation is the **enzyme-catalyzed microbial conversion of organic substrates into useful products** under controlled conditions, typically with regeneration of NAD⁺. Four industrially important products obtained by fermentation of waste biomass are:
> 1. **Bioethanol** — from molasses / lignocellulose using *S. cerevisiae*.
> 2. **Lactic acid** — from whey or starchy waste using *Lactobacillus*.
> 3. **Citric acid** — from molasses / cassava peel via *Aspergillus niger*.
> 4. **Single Cell Protein (SCP)** — from pressmud / bagasse using *Candida utilis*.

**Mark split (KTU pattern):** Definition — 1 M; four products — ½ M each (2 M).

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PGAET753]`

**Compare Submerged Fermentation (SmF) and Solid-State Fermentation (SSF) with respect to water activity, typical product, and one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, table format):**

| Parameter | Submerged Fermentation (SmF) | Solid-State Fermentation (SSF) |
|---|---|---|
| Water activity (aᵥ) | > 0.95 (free water) | 0.40 – 0.80 (moist, no free water) |
| Typical product | Ethanol, antibiotics, lactic acid | Enzymes (cellulase, amylase), tempeh |
| Key advantage | Excellent heat & mass transfer, easy scale-up, easy product recovery | Low water footprint, can directly ferment solid agri-residue (no liquefaction needed), higher product titres for fungal enzymes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - Students often confuse **water content** with **water activity (aᵥ)** — KTU specifically tests the distinction. aᵥ is the ratio of vapour pressures; it controls microbial physiology even when total moisture is moderate.
> - Writing "SSF uses solid substrate, SmF uses liquid substrate" is **incomplete** for full marks. Always mention **aᵥ** explicitly.
