# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> Define activated carbon. List any four agricultural feedstocks used for its production.

**Model Answer:**
Activated carbon is a highly porous carbonaceous adsorbent with a high internal surface area (500–1500 m²/g) produced by carbonization and controlled activation of carbon-rich precursors. Four agricultural feedstocks are:
1. **Coconut shell**
2. **Rice straw / Rice husk**
3. **Sugarcane bagasse**
4. **Corn stalk / Groundnut shell**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> Differentiate between physical and chemical activation of carbon.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Physical Activation | Chemical Activation |
|---|---|---|
| Activating agent | Steam / CO₂ | ZnCl₂, H₃PO₄, KOH |
| Temperature | 800–1000 °C | 400–800 °C |
| Steps | Two (carbonization + activation) | One or two (often combined) |
| Yield | Lower (20–30%) | Higher (30–50%) |
| Surface area | Moderate (500–900 m²/g) | High (1000–3000 m²/g) |
| Pollution | Low | High (chemical recovery needed) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the two-stage process of activated carbon manufacture from coconut shell, with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the effect of temperature on yield and surface area. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation (1 mark):**
Dried coconut shells are crushed, ground, and sieved to a uniform particle size of 1–3 mm. Moisture removed by oven drying at 110 °C for 12 h.

**Step 2 — Stage 1: Carbonization (2 marks):**
- Conducted in a stainless-steel horizontal tubular furnace.
- Temperature: **600 °C**, heating rate: **10 °C/min**, atmosphere: **N₂** (flow 100 mL/min), time: **2 h**.
- Outcome: Volatiles (CO, CO₂, CH₄, acetic acid, tars) are released; the residue is a **char** with fixed carbon ~70% and incipient closed porosity.

**Step 3 — Stage 2: Physical Activation (2 marks):**
- Char is heated to **850 °C** in presence of **steam** (H₂O vapor) at flow rate 2 g/min for 2 hours.
- Steam reacts with disordered carbon: C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (endothermic).
- Pores are opened, micropores enlarge, surface area rises from ~200 m²/g to ~900 m²/g.

**Step 4 — Effect of Temperature (2 marks):**
- **Low temperature (< 700 °C):** Incomplete carbonization, low porosity, low surface area, but high yield.
- **Optimal temperature (800–900 °C):** Maximum surface area, optimum burn-off, good yield.
- **High temperature (> 1000 °C):** Excessive burn-off, pore widening, collapse of micropores, drop in surface area, lower yield, sintering of ash.

> **(b)** A 1.00 kg sample of dried coconut shell (moisture 4%, ash 2% db) yields 380 g of char upon carbonization at 650 °C. This char is activated with steam to give 250 g of AC. The ash in the final AC is 6.25 g. Calculate: (i) Carbonization efficiency, (ii) Overall yield, (iii) Burn-off, (iv) Final ash percentage. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Dry mass of precursor:**
$$m_{dry} = 1000 \times (1 - 0.04) = 960 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Carbonization efficiency (η_C):**
$$\eta_C = \frac{380}{960} \times 100 = 39.58\%$$

**Step 3 — Overall yield (η):**
$$\eta = \frac{250}{960} \times 100 = 26.04\%$$

**Step 4 — Burn-off (BO):**
$$\text{BO} = \frac{380 - 250}{380} \times 100 = 34.21\%$$

**Step 5 — Ash percentage:**
$$\text{Ash\%} = \frac{6.25}{250} \times 100 = 2.50\%$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_C = 39.58\%$, $\eta = 26.04\%$, BO = 34.21%, Ash% = 2.50% (premium grade).

---

> **Q3(b) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** Discuss the production of activated carbon from rice straw using **H₃PO₄ chemical activation**. Explain the role of impregnation ratio and activation temperature on pore structure. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation (1 mark):**
Rice straw is washed, sun-dried, ground, and sieved (40 mesh). Moisture removed at 105 °C for 24 h.

**Step 2 — Chemical Impregnation (2 marks):**
- Rice straw is soaked in **85% H₃PO₄** solution at room temperature for 12 h.
- **Impregnation Ratio (IR) = mass of H₃PO₄ / mass of straw = 1.0–2.0**.
- Phosphoric acid acts as a **dehydrating agent**, breaking glycosidic linkages in cellulose/hemicellulose, suppressing tar formation, and swelling the biomass.

**Step 3 — Carbonization-Activation (Single Step) (2 marks):**
- Impregnated mass heated in a furnace to **450–550 °C** at 5 °C/min under limited air/N₂ for 1–2 h.
- H₃PO₄ catalyzes aromatization and acts as a **pore template**.

**Step 4 — Washing (1 mark):**
- AC is washed repeatedly with hot distilled water until pH neutral to remove residual H₃PO₄.
- Acid can be recovered and recycled (70–80% recovery).

**Step 5 — Effect of IR and Temperature on Pore Structure (1 mark):**
- **Low IR (0.5):** Few pores, low surface area (~500 m²/g).
- **High IR (2.0):** Excessive H₃PO₄ creates more mesopores, BET rises to ~1500 m²/g, but yield falls.
- **Low temp (< 400 °C):** Incomplete activation, low surface area.
- **High temp (> 600 °C):** Pore collapse, BET drops.

> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **iodine number test** for activated carbon. Why is it considered an indicator of micropore content? A 0.250 g AC sample is added to 25.0 mL of 0.0400 N iodine solution. After equilibration, titration of filtrate required 14.50 mL of 0.100 N sodium thiosulfate, while the blank required 20.00 mL. Calculate the iodine number. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Iodine Number Test Procedure (2 marks):**
1. A known mass of dry AC (0.1–1.0 g) is weighed and transferred to an Erlenmeyer flask.
2. 25–50 mL of standardized iodine solution (0.05–0.10 N) is added; flask is shaken for 30 s and filtered.
3. A 10 mL aliquot of the filtrate is titrated against standardized Na₂S₂O₃ (0.10 N) using starch indicator.
4. **Blank titration** (no AC) is performed under identical conditions.

**Why it indicates micropores:** Iodine (molecular diameter ≈ 0.56 nm) can access only pores > 0.5 nm. Hence, the amount of I₂ adsorbed is a direct measure of **micropore volume** (pores < 2 nm). High IN = abundant micropores.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 2 — Thiosulfate used:**
- Blank: $0.100 \times 20.00 = 2.000$ meq
- Sample: $0.100 \times 14.50 = 1.450$ meq

**Step 3 — Difference (meq of I₂ adsorbed, × ½):**
$$\Delta n = \frac{2.000 - 1.450}{2} = 0.275 \text{ mmol I}_2$$

**Step 4 — Mass of I₂ adsorbed (M = 253.81):**
$$m_{I_2} = 0.275 \times 10^{-3} \times 253.81 = 0.0698 \text{ g} = 69.8 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 5 — Iodine Number:**
$$\text{IN} = \frac{69.8}{0.250} = 279.2 \text{ mg/g}$$

> **Interpretation:** ~280 mg/g is classified as low-medium grade, suitable only for industrial decolorization, not for potable water treatment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (avoid these in the exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert wet mass to dry mass** when moisture is given. Always subtract moisture first.
> 2. **Confusing units of normality and molarity** in iodine number calculation. Both are valid but the equivalent factor matters.
> 3. **Not specifying N₂ atmosphere** in carbonization — examiners specifically look for "inert atmosphere" wording.
> 4. **Writing "carbon" instead of "carbonization efficiency"** — use the full technical term.
> 5. **Omitting the chemical recovery step** when discussing H₃PO₄/ZnCl₂ activation — examiner allocates 1 mark for this.
> 6. **Mixing up "adsorption" and "absorption"** — adsorption is a **surface** phenomenon, never bulk.
> 7. **Not mentioning ash content significance** — it's a quality indicator and you may be asked its typical value (< 5%).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Key Definitions:**
> - **Activated Carbon (AC):** Porous carbonaceous adsorbent, $S_{BET}$ = 500–3000 m²/g.
> - **Carbonization:** Pyrolysis at 400–800 °C in inert atmosphere to form char.
> - **Physical Activation:** Steam or CO₂ at 800–1000 °C.
> - **Chemical Activation:** ZnCl₂, H₃PO₄, KOH at 400–800 °C.

> **Premium Feedstocks (in order of commercial importance):**
> 1. **Coconut shell** → microporous GAC for potable water & gold recovery.
> 2. **Coal** (out of syllabus scope) → traditional feedstock.
> 3. **Wood** → powdered AC for decolorization.
> 4. **Rice husk / straw** → cheap AC for industrial wastewater.
> 5. **Bagasse** → sugar industry byproduct AC.

> **Key Numerical Values to Memorize:**
> - BET surface area of premium AC: **1000–1500 m²/g**
> - Iodine Number of premium AC: **≥ 1000 mg/g**
> - Methylene Blue Number: **≥ 200 mg/g**
> - Ash content: **< 5%**
> - Typical burn-off: **30–50%**
> - Typical overall yield (coconut shell): **20–28%**
> - Activation temperature: **800–900 °C** (physical); **450–700 °C** (chemical)

> **Key Reactions to Remember:**
> - C + H₂O → CO + H₂ (steam activation)
> - C + CO₂ → 2CO (CO₂ activation)
> - 6KOH + 2C → 2K + 3H₂ + 2K₂CO₃ (KOH activation)

> **Quality Indicators Hierarchy (highest to lowest micropore indicator):**
> **Iodine Number** > **BET Surface Area** > **Methylene Blue Number** > **Ash Content**

> **Advantages of Agricultural Wastes as Feedstocks:**
> - Renewable and abundant (millions of tonnes/year generated).
> - Low or negative cost — solves waste disposal problem.
> - High volatile matter → high reactivity for activation.
> - Lignocellulosic structure naturally microporous.
> - Carbon-neutral — closes the carbon cycle.

> **Limitations to Mention in Answers:**
> - High ash content in some wastes (rice husk: 15–20% ash).
> - Seasonal and geographic variability of supply.
> - Inconsistent product quality due to feedstock variability.
> - Need for pre-treatment (washing, size reduction).
> - Chemical activation requires acid recovery to prevent pollution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: By-products of agro-industries: Rice mill, oil mill, cattle feed mill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** By-products of agro-industries: Rice mill, oil mill, cattle feed mill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Agro-Industry By-Products: Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PGAET753 - Module 2):**
> *Agro-industrial by-products* are the **secondary, residual, or co-generated materials** obtained during the primary processing of agricultural raw materials (paddy, oilseeds, grains, sugarcane, etc.) into finished food, feed, or industrial products. Unlike "waste," these by-products possess **inherent economic value** because their chemical composition (lignocellulose, protein, oil, minerals) allows recovery, conversion, or direct use in secondary industries such as animal nutrition, bioenergy, fertilizers, and green materials.

The three agro-industries prescribed in Module 2 of the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Rice Milling Industry** (Paddy → Rice)
2. **Oil Milling Industry** (Oilseeds → Edible Oil)
3. **Cattle Feed Milling Industry** (Feed Formulation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Distinction — Waste vs. By-Product vs. Co-Product:**
> - **Waste**: Discarded material with negative or zero economic value (e.g., STP sludge).
> - **By-product**: A secondary product generated *incidentally* with lower commercial value than the main product (e.g., rice husk, oil cake).
> - **Co-product**: Generated in comparable volume and value to the main product (e.g., glycerol from biodiesel, bran from rice).
> In agro-industries, the term *by-product* is most commonly used, although many of these materials are now upgraded to **co-products** through value addition.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Tree of Processing"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Apple Tree" 🍎**
> Imagine an apple tree. The **fruit (apple)** is the main product. But the tree also gives you:
> - **Seeds** (used to grow more trees → **by-product of apple juice industry**).
> - **Skin/peel** (used for pectin extraction → **by-product**).
> - **Pruned branches** (used as firewood → **residue**).
>
> In the same way, a **rice mill** does not just produce rice — it simultaneously creates husk, bran, and broken rice, each with its own market and use. Treating them as "waste" is like throwing away the apple seeds — you are losing a free resource! The KTU module trains you to see the *full value chain* of every agro-raw material.

## 1.3 The Three Agro-Industries at a Glance

| Industry | Primary Product | Major By-Products | Principal Recovery / Utilization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rice Mill** | Milled rice | Rice husk (RH), Rice bran (RB), Rice broken, Rice straw | Energy, silica, edible oil, animal feed |
| **Oil Mill** | Edible oil | Oil cake (expeller/solvent), Soap stock, Gum, Foots | Cattle feed, fertilizer, glycerol, biodiesel |
| **Cattle Feed Mill** | Compound feed | Spent grain, Bagasse, Molasses, Mineral dust | Ruminant feed, energy, binder |

## 1.4 Strategic Importance (Why this matters for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantitative Relevance to Indian Economy:**
> - India produces approximately **~130 Million Tonnes (MT)** of paddy annually, generating **~26 MT of rice husk** and **~6.5 MT of rice bran** every year.
> - India is the **world's largest edible oil importer**, yet domestic oilseed processing generates **~25–30 MT of oil cakes annually**.
> - The Indian compound feed market is valued at **₹~50,000 crore**, with oil cakes forming 25–30 % of feed formulations.
>
> This makes by-product utilization not just an academic topic, but a **national economic and environmental priority**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rice Milling Industry — By-Products in Detail

### 2.1.1 Paddy Composition & By-Product Yield

Paddy (rough rice) is a multi-layered grain. The milling process removes successive layers to produce white rice.

$$\text{Paddy (100\%)} = \text{Husk (20–22\%)} + \text{Bran (5–8\%)} + \text{Clean Rice (66–70\%)} + \text{Broken (2–5\%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Milling Fractions (Industry Standard Values):**
> - **Husk (RH)**: 20 % by weight of paddy; outermost, siliceous, fibrous layer.
> - **Bran (RB)**: 5–8 %; rich in lipids, vitamins, proteins.
> - **Clean Rice (head + broken)**: 70–72 %; the main product.
> - **Straw**: 1.0–1.5 tonnes per tonne of paddy grain (residue in the field).

### 2.1.2 Rice Husk (RH) — The Fuel of the Mill

**Physical & Chemical Characteristics:**
- **Bulk density**: 90–150 kg/m³ (very low — floats on water).
- **Calorific value**: 12,500–15,000 kJ/kg (≈ 3,000–3,600 kcal/kg).
- **Silica (SiO₂) content**: 18–22 % (highest among agro-residues).
- **Cellulose**: 35–40 %; **Lignin**: 20–25 %; **Hemicellulose**: 15–20 %.

**Engineering Properties (Critical for Combustion/Fluidisation):**
- **Angle of repose**: ~38°–45°
- **Ignition temperature**: ~250–300 °C
- **Ash content**: 18–25 % (very high — characteristic of RH)

**Major Applications:**
1. **Direct combustion** in husk-fired boilers (parboiling mills are energy self-sufficient via RH).
2. **Rice Husk Ash (RHA)** — produced by controlled combustion:
   - **Black RHA** (incomplete combustion, C-rich, ~10–20 % fixed carbon).
   - **White RHA** (controlled combustion, SiO₂-rich, >90 % amorphous silica) → used in cement, concrete (pozzolanic material), silica gel, silicon carbide.
3. **Silica extraction** → sodium silicate, precipitated silica, fumed silica.
4. **Briquettes and pellets** for domestic and industrial fuel.
5. **Activated carbon** via steam/chemical activation.
6. **Insulation material** (RH board, RH ash-based bricks).

### 2.1.3 Rice Bran (RB) — A Nutritional Treasure

**Composition (per 100 g dry basis):**
- **Oil**: 15–22 % (rice bran oil, RBO)
- **Protein**: 12–18 %
- **Fiber**: 7–11 %
- **Ash**: 6–9 %
- **Vitamins**: Rich source of Vitamin E (tocopherols, tocotrienols) and B-complex (oryzanol).
- **Moisture**: 8–12 % (fresh bran); deteriorates rapidly due to lipase activity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Operational Point — Lipase Inactivation:**
> Fresh rice bran contains the enzyme **lipase** that rapidly hydrolyzes the oil into free fatty acids (FFA), making the oil unsuitable for edible use. Hence, bran must be **stabilized within hours of milling** by:
> - **Dry heat treatment** (100–110 °C, 10–20 min)
> - **Steam treatment** (autoclaving)
> - **Extrusion cooking**
> - **Chemical stabilization** (e.g., acid treatment with HCl)

**Applications:**
1. **Rice Bran Oil (RBO)** — a premium edible oil with high smoke point and oryzanol (cholesterol-lowering).
2. **Defatted bran** → cattle feed, biscuits, high-protein flour.
3. **Fermentation substrate** for enzymes, organic acids, biopesticides.
4. **Pharmaceutical extraction** of γ-oryzanol, tocotrienols.

### 2.1.4 Rice Straw and Rice Broken
- **Rice straw**: 1.0–1.5 t per tonne paddy. Used for cattle bedding, mushroom cultivation (compost), paper/pulp, biogas via anaerobic digestion, and bioethanol (lignocellulosic route).
- **Rice broken**: Sold at 50–70 % price of head rice → used in breweries, rice flour, baby food, and pet food.

---

## 2.2 Oil Milling Industry — By-Products in Detail

### 2.2.1 Oilseed Processing — Two Methods Generate Different By-Products

| Method | Main Product | By-Product | Residual Oil in Cake |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Expeller** | Crude oil | Expeller cake (EC) | 6–10 % |
| **Solvent Extraction** | Refined oil | Solvent-extracted meal (De-oiled cake, DOC) | <1.5 % |
| **Combined (Expeller + Solvent)** | High-quality oil | Mixed cake | <2 % |

**Common Oilseeds Processed in India:** Groundnut, Mustard, Soybean, Sunflower, Sesame, Coconut, Cottonseed, Rapeseed.

### 2.2.2 Oil Cake (Expeller Cake) — The Major By-Product

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Module 2 Highlight):**
> *Oil cake* is the solid residue left after the extraction of oil from oilseeds. It is a high-protein, high-fiber material extensively used in **cattle feed**, **organic fertilizer**, and as a **fermentation feedstock**.

**Composition of Typical Oil Cakes (% dry basis):**

| Cake Type | Protein | Oil (residual) | Fiber | Ash |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Groundnut cake | 45–55 | 6–10 | 5–8 | 4–6 |
| Mustard cake | 35–40 | 6–10 | 8–10 | 6–8 |
| Soybean meal (DOC) | 48–52 | <1.5 | 3–5 | 5–6 |
| Sunflower cake | 30–38 | 7–12 | 18–25 | 6–8 |
| Coconut (copra) cake | 20–25 | 6–10 | 10–14 | 5–7 |

**Applications of Oil Cake:**
1. **Cattle feed** (must be de-toxified for some seeds, e.g., mustard contains glucosinolates; cottonseed contains gossypol).
2. **Organic fertilizer** (NPK source; groundnut cake is a prized bio-fertilizer).
3. **Mushroom cultivation** substrate.
4. **Industrial protein** for adhesives, bioplastics.
5. **Biofuel feedstock** — direct combustion or pellets.
6. **Extraction of secondary phytochemicals** (saponins, lignans).

### 2.2.3 Other Oil Mill By-Products

- **Soap stock**: The alkaline sludge from oil refining; rich in fatty acids, used for **glycerol extraction** and **biodiesel production**.
- **Gums and lecithins**: Phospholipid fraction (mainly in soybean); used in food emulsifiers.
- **Foots / tank settlings**: Heavy sludge from crude oil storage; used in **industrial fatty acid** production.
- **Glycerol (Glycerine)**: From biodiesel transesterification; a major co-product of the oil/biofuel industry.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick — Learn this by-heart:**
> The oil mill is unique because almost **every by-product is a feedstock for another industry**: oil cake → feed industry, soap stock → oleochemical industry, glycerol → cosmetics/pharma, fatty acid → biodiesel. This is a classic example of an **industrial symbiosis** (zero-waste concept).

---

## 2.3 Cattle Feed Mill — By-Products in Detail

### 2.3.1 What is a Cattle Feed Mill?

A **compound cattle feed mill** (also called a *feed mixing plant*) blends various raw materials — cereals, oil cakes, brans, minerals, vitamins, molasses, and additives — into a nutritionally balanced feed for ruminants (cattle, buffalo), poultry, swine, or aquaculture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Cattle Feed Composition (% by weight, KTU reference):**
> - Cereal grain (maize/sorghum) → 30–40 %
> - Oil cake (mustard/groundnut) → 25–30 %
> - Brans (rice/wheat) → 15–20 %
> - Molasses → 5–10 % (binder + energy)
> - Mineral mixture + salt → 2–3 %
> - Vitamin + additive premix → 0.5–1 %

### 2.3.2 Major By-Products Used/Generated in Cattle Feed Mills

| By-Product | Source | Function in Feed | Other Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rice bran (defatted)** | Rice mill | Energy, fiber | Edible oil extraction |
| **Wheat bran** | Flour mill | Fiber, protein | Bakery, fermentation |
| **De-oiled cakes** | Oil mill | Major protein source | Fertilizer |
| **Molasses** | Sugar mill | Energy, palatability, binder | Alcohol (ethanol) distillery |
| **Bagasse** | Sugar mill | Roughage / fiber | Paper, board, boiler fuel |
| **Spent brewer's grain** | Brewery | Protein (24–28 %) | Ruminant feed |
| **Mineral dust / shell grit** | Marine processing | Ca, P supplement | Poultry grit |
| **Poultry litter** | Poultry farm | N-source for composting | Organic fertilizer |

### 2.3.3 Engineering Process Flow in a Cattle Feed Mill

1. **Receipt & storage** of ingredients (silos).
2. **Grinding** (hammer mill → particle size ~1–3 mm).
3. **Weighing & batching** (precision scales).
4. **Mixing** (horizontal ribbon / vertical screw mixer).
5. **Pelleting** (steam-conditioned, ~80 °C, ring-die pellet mill) — *optional but increases bulk density and reduces dust*.
6. **Cooling & drying** (counter-flow cooler).
7. **Screening & packaging**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **source** of the by-product with the **destination**. For example, *oil cake* is a **by-product of the oil mill** but a **raw material for the cattle feed mill**. Always state the direction of flow.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU University Exam (Part A & B).**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{\text{husk}} = 0.20 \times m_{\text{paddy}}$ | Husk yield from paddy; $m$ = mass | kg or t | Rice mill mass balance |
| 2 | $m_{\text{bran}} = 0.06 \times m_{\text{paddy}}$ | Bran yield from paddy | kg or t | Rice mill mass balance |
| 3 | $CV_{\text{husk}} \approx 14{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Calorific value of rice husk | kJ/kg | Energy from RH |
| 4 | $E_{\text{husk}} = m_{\text{husk}} \times CV_{\text{husk}}$ | Energy content of husk | kJ or MJ | Boiler sizing |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | Boiler thermal efficiency | % | RH-fired boiler |
| 6 | $\%\text{Yield of oil} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{seed}}} \times 100$ | Oil extraction yield | % | Oil mill |
| 7 | $m_{\text{cake}} = m_{\text{seed}} - m_{\text{oil}} - m_{\text{loss}}$ | Mass balance, oil mill | kg | Cake yield calculation |
| 8 | $\%\text{Protein} = \dfrac{N \times 6.25}{m_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$ | Crude protein from Kjeldahl N | % | Feed quality |
| 9 | $\text{FCR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{feed consumed}}}{m_{\text{weight gain}}}$ | Feed Conversion Ratio | — | Cattle/feed evaluation |
| 10 | $R_{\text{ash}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{ash}}}{m_{\text{fuel,dry}}} \times 100$ | Ash % of fuel | % | RH characterization |
| 11 | $\text{Silica recovery} = \dfrac{m_{\text{SiO}_2}}{m_{\text{RHA}}} \times 100$ | RHA → Silica purity | % | RHA processing |
| 12 | $D_{\text{calorific}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{husk}} \times CV_{\text{husk}}}{m_{\text{paddy}} \times CV_{\text{paddy}}}$ | Calorific distribution ratio | — | Paddy energy audit |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** In markdown tables, the vertical pipe $\vert$ is a special character. KTU examiners (and most Markdown renderers) can break tables if the symbol is used in a cell. Therefore, in your own KTU answer sheets, **draw the absolute-value bars in LaTeX using $\lvert \dots \rvert$ or $\vert$ with the `amsmath` package**, not as raw `|`.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | By-Product | Engineering Application | Course Outcome (KTU 2024) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rice mill | RH | Renewable energy, green silica | CO1, CO2 |
| Rice mill | RBO | Nutraceuticals, biodiesel | CO2 |
| Oil mill | Oil cake | Cattle feed, biofertilizer | CO1, CO3 |
| Oil mill | Soap stock | Oleochemicals, surfactants | CO3 |
| Feed mill | Spent grain | Methane (biogas) via AD | CO2, CO4 |
| Feed mill | Bagasse | Cogeneration power | CO2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping to KTU Course Outcomes (COs):**
> - **CO1**: Identify waste/by-product streams (this entire topic).
> - **CO2**: Select appropriate recovery technology.
> - **CO3**: Evaluate end-use applications and value chain.
> - **CO4**: Assess environmental and economic feasibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Mass & Energy Balance of a Rice Mill

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Question Type:** *"Calculate the husk, bran and rice yield from 1000 kg of paddy, and determine the energy available from the husk."*

### Given:
- Paddy input, $m_{\text{paddy}} = 1000$ kg
- Husk yield = 20 %; Bran yield = 6 %; Clean rice yield = 70 %; Broken = 2 %; Loss = 2 %

### Step 1 — Husk Mass
$$m_{\text{husk}} = 0.20 \times m_{\text{paddy}} = 0.20 \times 1000 = 200 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2 — Bran Mass
$$m_{\text{bran}} = 0.06 \times m_{\text{paddy}} = 0.06 \times 1000 = 60 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3 — Clean Rice Mass
$$m_{\text{rice}} = 0.70 \times m_{\text{paddy}} = 0.70 \times 1000 = 700 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Verification by Mass Balance
$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{total out}} &= m_{\text{husk}} + m_{\text{bran}} + m_{\text{rice}} + m_{\text{broken}} + m_{\text{loss}} \\
&= 200 + 60 + 700 + 20 + 20 \\
&= 1000 \text{ kg} = m_{\text{paddy}} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Energy Available from Rice Husk
$$E_{\text{husk}} = m_{\text{husk}} \times CV_{\text{husk}} = 200 \text{ kg} \times 14{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ} = 2800 \text{ MJ}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to kWh (for engineering relevance)
$$E_{\text{husk, kWh}} = \dfrac{2.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ}}{3600 \text{ kJ/kWh}} \approx 777.78 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** The husk from 1 tonne of paddy gives **~2.8 GJ of energy** — enough to power a small parboiling plant or a 10 kW generator for ~78 hours. This is why rice mills are net energy exporters when husk is utilized.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Oil Mill Mass Balance

> **Problem:** A solvent extraction plant processes 500 tonnes/day of soybean. Soybean contains 18 % oil. After extraction, the de-oiled cake (DOC) retains 1.2 % oil. Moisture content of seed is 10 %. Loss is 1.5 % of input. Calculate (a) oil recovered, (b) DOC produced, (c) meal yield.

### Given:
- Input soybean: 500 t/day
- Oil in seed: 18 % (wet basis)
- Residual oil in DOC: 1.2 %
- Moisture: 10 %
- Loss: 1.5 %

### Step 1 — Dry Mass of Seed Input
$$m_{\text{seed,dry}} = 500 \times (1 - 0.10) = 450 \text{ t/day}$$

### Step 2 — Total Oil in Input
$$m_{\text{oil,total}} = 450 \times 0.18 = 81 \text{ t/day}$$

### Step 3 — Oil Recovered (assume 99 % extraction efficiency)
$$m_{\text{oil,recovered}} = 81 \times 0.99 = 80.19 \text{ t/day}$$

### Step 4 — Oil Retained in DOC
$$m_{\text{oil,DOC}} = 81 - 80.19 = 0.81 \text{ t/day}$$

### Step 5 — Mass of DOC (Total Solids in Cake)
$$m_{\text{solids,non-oil}} = 450 - 81 = 369 \text{ t/day (non-oil solids)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{DOC}} &= m_{\text{solids,non-oil}} + m_{\text{oil,DOC}} \\
&= 369 + 0.81 = 369.81 \text{ t/day}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Moisture in DOC (typically 10–12 %)
Assuming DOC is sold at 11 % moisture:
$$m_{\text{DOC,sold}} = \dfrac{369.81}{1 - 0.11} = 415.5 \text{ t/day}$$

### Step 7 — Account for Process Loss
$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{loss}} &= 500 \times 0.015 = 7.5 \text{ t/day (unaccounted)} \\
\text{Check: } & 80.19 + 415.5 + 7.5 = 503.19 \text{ t/day (small drift due to rounding)} 
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how the by-product (DOC) is **~5 times the mass of the main product (oil)**. This shows why oil mills are *also* feed companies — the cake is the revenue backbone, not the oil.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Energy from Combustion of RHA Silo

> **Problem:** A rice mill generates 1000 kg of rice husk/day. After combustion, 220 kg of white rice husk ash (RHA) is collected. The RHA contains 92 % amorphous SiO₂. The boiler efficiency is 65 %. Calculate (a) SiO₂ produced/day, (b) useful heat energy recovered.

### Step 1 — SiO₂ Mass
$$m_{\text{SiO}_2} = 0.92 \times 220 = 202.4 \text{ kg/day}$$

### Step 2 — Total Energy in Husk
$$E_{\text{input}} = 1000 \times 14{,}000 = 14 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/day}$$

### Step 3 — Useful Energy Recovered
$$E_{\text{useful}} = 0.65 \times 14 \times 10^{6} = 9.1 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/day}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** White RHA is a **value-added co-product** selling for ₹8–15/kg, far more valuable than the ₹1–2/kg raw husk. Combustion + ash processing can **3–4× the value** of the same agro-residue.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Industries

| Parameter | Rice Mill | Oil Mill | Cattle Feed Mill |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Main product** | Milled rice | Edible oil | Compound feed |
| **Major by-product** | Rice husk + bran | Oil cake (EC/DOC) | Bagasse, spent grain, dust |
| **State of by-product** | Solid, low moisture | Solid, high protein | Solid, fibrous |
| **By-product:Main product ratio (mass)** | 0.40 : 1 | 4–6 : 1 | 0.2 : 1 (additive) |
| **Energy value** | High (RH) | Medium (cake) | Low–Medium (bagasse) |
| **Primary end-use** | Energy + feed | Feed + fertilizer | Energy, feed |
| **Toxicity concern** | None | Gossypol (cotton), glucosinolate (mustard) | Mycotoxins (aflatoxin) |
| **Stabilization needed?** | Yes (bran — lipase) | Sometimes (de-toxify) | Yes (drying, acid treatment) |

---

## 3.5 KTU Frequently Used Conversion Factors

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ tonne} &= 1000 \text{ kg} = 10^{6} \text{ g} \\
1 \text{ kcal} &= 4.186 \text{ kJ} \\
1 \text{ kWh} &= 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 3600 \text{ kJ} \\
1 \text{ barrel of oil (BOE)} &\approx 5.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ} \\
\text{N to Protein} &: \text{multiply by } 6.25
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Complete Rice Mill By-Product Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Paddy Input] --> B[Pre-Cleaning]
    B --> C[Parboiling Optional]
    C --> D[De-Stoning and Husking]
    D --> E["Husk RH 20 percent"]
    D --> F[Paddy Separation]
    F --> G[Polishing and Whitening]
    G --> H["Bran RB 6 percent"]
    G --> I["Clean Rice 70 percent"]
    G --> J["Broken 2 percent"]
    I --> K[Grading and Packaging]
    E --> L["RH Fired Boiler"]
    L --> M["Steam for Parboiling"]
    L --> N["White RHA 18 to 22 kg per 100 kg husk"]
    N --> O["Silica 92 percent SiO2"]
    O --> P["Cement and Concrete Use"]
    H --> Q[Stabilization Heat]
    Q --> R[Solvent Extraction]
    R --> S["Rice Bran Oil 18 to 22 percent"]
    S --> T[Refining and Bottling]
    R --> U[Defatted Bran]
    U --> V["Cattle Feed Ingredient"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this Mermaid chart:** Each box represents a process step or material. Arrows show the **direction of material flow**. The percentages (e.g., 20 %) indicate the **yield** from paddy. The chart is drawn as a *left-to-right process flow*, which is the KTU-recommended style for mass-balance diagrams in ESE answers.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Oil Mill By-Product Generation (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Oilseed Receipt] --> A2[Cleaning and Drying]
    A2 --> A3[Cracking and Flaking]
    A3 --> A4[Cooking at 100 to 110 C]
    A4 --> A5[Expeller Press]
    A4 --> A6[Solvent Extraction Plant]
    A5 --> A7["Crude Oil 18 to 22 percent"]
    A5 --> A8["Expeller Cake 6 to 10 percent residual oil"]
    A6 --> A9["Refined Oil"]
    A6 --> A10["De Oiled Cake DOC less than 1.5 percent oil"]
    A7 --> A11[Refining and Bleaching]
    A11 --> A12["Soap Stock By Product"]
    A11 --> A13["Gums and Lecithin"]
    A11 --> A14["Edible Oil Main Product"]
    A8 --> A15[Cattle Feed Mill Input]
    A10 --> A15
    A8 --> A16[Organic Fertilizer Pelletizing]
    A10 --> A16
    A12 --> A17["Oleochemical Plant"]
    A17 --> A18["Glycerol and Fatty Acid"]
    A18 --> A19["Biodiesel and Soap"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When drawing such process flow diagrams in the exam, **label every stream with temperature, composition, or yield %**. Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** purely for correctly labeled and complete diagrams.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Cattle Feed Mill Material Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Ingredient Receipt Silos] --> B2[Grinding Hammer Mill]
    B2 --> B3[Weigh Batching]
    B3 --> B4[Mixing Ribbon Blender]
    B4 --> B5[Steam Conditioning 80 C]
    B5 --> B6[Pelleting Ring Die]
    B6 --> B7[Cooling and Drying]
    B7 --> B8[Screening and Packaging]
    B4 --> B9["By product 1 Rice Bran from Rice Mill"]
    B4 --> B10["By product 2 De Oiled Cake from Oil Mill"]
    B4 --> B11["By product 3 Molasses from Sugar Mill"]
    B4 --> B12["By product 4 Spent Grain from Brewery"]
    B4 --> B13["By product 5 Mineral Dust from Marine"]
    B8 --> B14[Finished Compound Feed]
    B9 --> B14
    B10 --> B14
    B11 --> B14
    B12 --> B14
    B13 --> B14
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Value Chain Hierarchy of Agro By-Products

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Agro By Product] --> C2[Direct Use]
    C1 --> C3[Energy Recovery]
    C1 --> C4[Material Conversion]
    C2 --> C2A[Cattle Feed and Bedding]
    C2 --> C2B[Soil Conditioner and Mulch]
    C3 --> C3A[Combustion in Boilers]
    C3 --> C3B[Gasification and Pyrolysis]
    C3 --> C3C[Anaerobic Digestion Biogas]
    C3A --> C3A1[Steam and Power]
    C3A --> C3A2[Ash Silica Extraction]
    C4 --> C4A[Fermentation Bioethanol]
    C4 --> C4B[Extraction Oils and Proteins]
    C4 --> C4C[Composting and Vermicomposting]
    C4B --> C4B1[Nutraceuticals]
    C4B --> C4B2[Oleochemicals and Glycerol]
    C4C --> C4C1[Organic Fertilizer Pellet]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram is important for KTU:** This is a high-level "decision tree" for by-product utilization. During ESE Part B, when asked *"Discuss the various end-uses of by-products from rice/oil/cattle feed mills,"* this **3-tier hierarchy (Direct Use → Energy → Material)** is the perfect answer skeleton. Examiners love it because it demonstrates *systematic thinking* (a key indicator of CO3–CO4 attainment).

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram (Mermaid) — Inter-Industry By-Product Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Rice Mill] -->|Husk| EB[Energy Sector Boiler]
    R -->|Bran| OF[Oil Refinery RBO]
    OF -->|Defatted Bran| CF[Feed Mill]
    OM[Oil Mill] -->|Oil Cake| CF
    SM[Sugar Mill] -->|Molasses| CF
    SM -->|Bagasse| EB
    SM -->|Press Mud| OG[Organic Fertilizer]
    BR[Brewery] -->|Spent Grain| CF
    CF -->|Manure to Fields| FI[Farming]
    FI -->|Paddy and Oilseed| R
    FI -->|Sugarcane| SM
```

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Symbiosis concept:** This diagram captures the **circular economy** model. The waste/by-product of one industry becomes the raw material of another. In KTU ESE answers, you can earn **bonus marks** by drawing this diagram to show your awareness of sustainability concepts (linked to NEP 2020 outcome-based education).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q:** *Define agro-industrial by-products. List four by-products of a rice mill along with their primary end-uses.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Agro-industrial by-products** are the secondary materials co-generated during the processing of agricultural raw materials into primary products, which possess intrinsic value for recovery or use in secondary industries.

| S.No. | By-Product | Primary End-Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rice Husk (RH) | Boiler fuel; silica extraction |
| 2 | Rice Bran (RB) | Rice bran oil; animal feed |
| 3 | Rice Broken | Brewery; rice flour |
| 4 | Rice Straw | Cattle bedding; mushroom compost |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four by-products with uses — total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** *Differentiate between expeller cake and solvent-extracted cake (DOC) of an oil mill.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Expeller Cake (EC) | Solvent-Extracted Cake (DOC) |
|---|---|---|
| Method | Mechanical pressing | Hexane/petroleum solvent |
| Residual oil | 6–10 % | <1.5 % |
| Protein content | Lower (more residual oil) | Higher (more concentrated) |
| Energy input | High (mechanical) | Lower mechanical + solvent recovery |
| Purity | More natural, less denatured | May have solvent traces |
| Cost | Lower (small-scale) | Higher (large plants) |
| Application | Cattle feed (less oil = less heat) | High-protein feed, food-grade |

*(2 marks for table comparison, 1 mark for the application difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### 📌 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Adapted]**
**Q:** *(a)* Describe, with a neat flow diagram, the **mass balance of a rice mill** processing 50 tonnes/day of paddy. Assume standard yield percentages. Compute the energy available from the rice husk and the mass of white RHA produced. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **engineering applications of rice husk ash (RHA)** in the construction and chemical industries, with relevant specifications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Mass & Energy Balance of Rice Mill

**Given:**
- $m_{\text{paddy}} = 50{,}000$ kg/day
- Husk = 20 %; Bran = 6 %; Clean rice = 70 %; Broken = 2 %; Loss = 2 %

**Step 1 — Mass of Each Fraction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{husk}} &= 0.20 \times 50{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
m_{\text{bran}} &= 0.06 \times 50{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
m_{\text{rice}} &= 0.70 \times 50{,}000 = 35{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
m_{\text{broken}} &= 0.02 \times 50{,}000 = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
m_{\text{loss}} &= 0.02 \times 50{,}000 = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:**
$$\sum m_{\text{out}} = 10{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 1{,}000 + 1{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Energy from Husk** (CV = 14,000 kJ/kg):
$$E_{\text{husk}} = 10{,}000 \times 14{,}000 = 1.4 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ/day} = 140{,}000 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 3 — White RHA (≈ 20 % of husk mass):**
$$m_{\text{RHA}} = 0.20 \times 10{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh:**
$$E_{\text{husk, kWh}} = \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{8}}{3600} \approx 38{,}889 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Diagram (Mass Balance Tree — to be drawn in exam):**

```
Paddy (50 t)
   ├── Husk (10 t)         → Boiler (1.4 × 10⁸ kJ)
   │       └── White RHA (2 t)
   ├── Bran (3 t)          → RBO extraction
   ├── Clean Rice (35 t)    → Main product
   ├── Broken (1 t)        → Brewery
   └── Loss (1 t)
```

> **Mark split-up (KTU standard):** 2 marks for assumptions, 2 marks for mass balance calculations, 2 marks for energy computation, 1 mark for the flow diagram.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Engineering Applications of RHA

**RHA Specifications:**

| Property | White RHA | Black RHA |
|---|---|---|
| Colour | Greyish white | Black |
| SiO₂ content | 90–95 % | 50–60 % |
| Carbon content | <2 % | 10–20 % |
| Specific surface | High (pozzolanic) | Lower |
| Particle size | <45 µm | Variable |

**Applications:**

1. **Pozzolan in cement & concrete** (ASTM C618 Class N): Replaces 10–30 % Portland cement, increases long-term strength and reduces CO₂ footprint.
2. **Silica source for sodium silicate and precipitated silica.**
3. **Insulation bricks and refractory linings** (low thermal conductivity ~0.09 W/m·K).
4. **Adsorbent for oil spill cleanup** (hydrophobicity after surface treatment).
5. **Silicon production** (carbothermic reduction of RHA → metallurgical-grade Si).
6. **Filter aid** in sugar and beverage industries.
7. **Catalyst support** in heterogeneous catalysis.

> **Mark split-up (KTU standard):** 2 marks for the specifications table, 4 marks for the 6 applications, 1 mark for the value-added conclusion.

---

### 📌 Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Adapted]**
**Q:** *(a)* Classify the **by-products generated in an oil mill** and explain the **composition and end-uses of oil cake (expeller and DOC)**. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a neat flowchart, describe the **operation of a compound cattle feed mill**, and discuss the **role of by-products** (rice bran, de-oiled cake, molasses, spent grain) in feed formulation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — By-Products of an Oil Mill

**Classification of Oil Mill By-Products:**

1. **Solid by-products**:
   - Expeller cake (EC)
   - De-oiled cake / solvent-extracted meal (DOC)
   - Hulls / shells (e.g., groundnut, sunflower)

2. **Liquid by-products**:
   - Soap stock (from refining)
   - Gums and lecithin (degumming)
   - Foots (settlings)

3. **Gaseous by-products**:
   - Solvent vapours (recovered in extraction plants)

**Composition & End-Uses — Expeller Cake vs. DOC:**

| Feature | Expeller Cake (EC) | De-Oiled Cake (DOC) |
|---|---|---|
| Crude Protein | 38–50 % | 48–55 % |
| Residual Oil | 6–10 % | <1.5 % |
| Crude Fiber | 6–12 % | 5–10 % |
| Energy in feed | Higher (from oil) | Lower |
| Shelf life | Shorter (rancidity risk) | Longer |
| Cost per kg protein | Higher | Lower |
| End-use | Cattle feed, fertilizer | High-protein feed, food, fermentation |
| Anti-nutritional factors | Partially intact | Often concentrated |

**Key End-Use Applications of Oil Cake:**
1. **Cattle feed** — most important outlet (70–80 % of cake in India).
2. **Organic fertilizer** — NPK source, especially groundnut and castor cake.
3. **Mushroom cultivation** substrate.
4. **Industrial protein** for adhesives, bioplastics.
5. **Biofuel** — direct combustion, pellets, or biogas via anaerobic digestion.
6. **Edible protein concentrate** (soybean, groundnut) for human food.

> **Mark split-up:** 2 marks classification, 3 marks comparison table, 2 marks end-uses.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Cattle Feed Mill & Role of By-Products

**Operation Flow (to be drawn):**

```
Raw Material Receipt
   ↓
Cleaning
   ↓
Grinding (Hammer Mill)
   ↓
Weigh Batching (Digital Scales)
   ↓
Mixing (Ribbon / Paddle Blender)
   ↓
Steam Conditioning (~80 °C, 15 % moisture)
   ↓
Pelleting (Ring-Die Pellet Mill, 80–90 °C, 30–60 s)
   ↓
Counter-flow Cooling (to 5 °C above ambient)
   ↓
Screening (to remove fines)
   ↓
Packing and Storage
```

**Role of Major By-Products in Feed Formulation:**

| By-Product | Source | Role in Feed | Typical Inclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rice bran (defatted) | Rice mill | Energy, fiber, B-vitamins | 15–25 % |
| De-oiled cake (DOC) | Oil mill | Major protein source | 20–30 % |
| Molasses | Sugar mill | Energy + binder + palatability | 5–10 % |
| Spent brewer's grain | Brewery | Protein (24 %), fiber | 10–20 % |
| Bagasse (treated) | Sugar mill | Roughage for ruminants | 5–10 % |
| Mineral dust | Marine | Ca, P, trace minerals | 1–3 % |

**Quality Control Tests (must be mentioned in exam):**
- Moisture (should be <12 % for shelf life).
- Crude protein (Kjeldahl method).
- Crude fiber, fat, ash.
- Aflatoxin contamination test (HPLC).
- Urease activity (for soybean meal — must be <0.1 ΔpH).

> **Mark split-up:** 2 marks for flowchart, 3 marks for the role-of-by-products table, 2 marks for quality control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and how to avoid them):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "waste" and "by-product"** — In your KTU answer, *always* use "by-product" or "co-product" for rice husk, bran, oil cake, etc. Calling them "waste" loses you a clear definition mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the % yield** — Every mass balance answer should include the standard yield percentages (20 % husk, 6 % bran, 70 % rice). Examiners deduct 1 mark if the assumption is missing.
> 3. **Ignoring stabilization of rice bran** — Rice bran answers that do not mention *lipase inactivation* are incomplete. Always include: dry heat, steam, or extrusion stabilization.
> 4. **Confusing expeller cake and DOC** — These are *not* the same. State the residual oil % and method.
> 5. **Mixing up by-product direction** — A common trap: saying "oil cake is a by-product of the cattle feed mill" — this is *wrong*. Oil cake is a by-product of the **oil mill** and a *raw material* for the **feed mill**.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners are strict. Always write the unit (kg, MJ, %, etc.) after every number. A number without a unit is treated as incomplete and loses 0.5 mark per instance.
> 7. **Skipping the flow diagram** — In 7-mark sub-questions, a labelled diagram is mandatory. Without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 8. **Missing anti-nutritional factors** — In oil cake/feed questions, always mention gossypol (cotton), glucosinolate (mustard), trypsin inhibitor (soybean), aflatoxin (groundnut), and urease activity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the night before the KTU exam):**
>
> ✅ **Rice Mill (Paddy = 100 %):**
> - Husk = 20 % → energy, silica, RHA
> - Bran = 6 % → RBO, defatted feed
> - Clean rice = 70 %; Broken = 2 %; Loss = 2 %
> - Rice straw = 1.0–1.5 t per tonne of paddy (field residue)
> - Calorific value of husk = **14,000 kJ/kg**
> - RHA has **20–22 % SiO₂** (black) and **>90 % SiO₂** (white)
> - Lipase inactivation of bran is **mandatory**
>
> ✅ **Oil Mill:**
> - Expeller cake: 6–10 % residual oil; DOC: <1.5 %
> - Main by-product is oil cake — used in **cattle feed (70–80 %)** and **fertilizer**
> - Soap stock → oleochemicals, glycerol, biodiesel
> - Cake:Main product (oil) ratio = **4–6 : 1**
> - Anti-nutritional: gossypol, glucosinolate, trypsin inhibitor
>
> ✅ **Cattle Feed Mill:**
> - Typical composition: cereal 30–40 %, cake 25–30 %, bran 15–20 %, molasses 5–10 %, minerals 2–3 %, vitamins 0.5–1 %
> - Process: Receive → Grind → Batch → Mix → Condition → Pellet → Cool → Pack
> - Key by-products used: rice bran, DOC, molasses, spent grain, bagasse, mineral dust
> - Quality tests: moisture, crude protein, fiber, ash, **aflatoxin**, **urease activity**
>
> ✅ **Universal Equations to Memorize:**
> - $m_{\text{husk}} = 0.20 \times m_{\text{paddy}}$
> - $E_{\text{husk}} = m_{\text{husk}} \times 14{,}000$ kJ/kg
> - $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$
> - $\% \text{Protein} = \dfrac{N \times 6.25}{m_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$
> - $\text{FCR} = \dfrac{m_{\text{feed}}}{m_{\text{gain}}}$
>
> ✅ **Cross-Cutting Concepts:**
> - Industrial symbiosis: rice mill → boiler → energy; oil mill → cake → feed mill; sugar mill → molasses → feed mill
> - Circular economy + Zero-waste philosophy = **NEP 2020 alignment**
> - Stabilization, detoxification, and quality control are the *3 pillars* of by-product utilization
>
> ✅ **One-Liners for Marks:**
> - *"Rice husk is the siliceous energy capsule of the rice mill."*
> - *"Oil cake is the protein backbone of the oil mill, not the oil."*
> - *"The cattle feed mill is a recycler of all other agro-industries."*
> - *"Stabilization makes by-products, products."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valuable constituents and composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Valuable constituents and composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuable Constituents** are the recoverable, commercially exploitable, or functionally useful chemical and biochemical components present within waste streams and industrial byproducts. These constituents transform a "waste" into a "resource" by enabling recovery, recycling, and valorization pathways.

In the context of **Waste and Byproduct Utilization (PGAET753)**, the term *composition* refers to the quantitative chemical make-up of a waste/byproduct stream, typically expressed as:

- **Proximate composition** — Moisture, volatile matter, fixed carbon, and ash content.
- **Ultimate composition** — Elemental composition (C, H, O, N, S).
- **Biochemical/macromolecular composition** — Proteins, lipids, carbohydrates (cellulose, hemicellulose, starch, sugars), lignin, pectin, and other biopolymers.
- **Mineral/Ash composition** — Macro and micro-nutrients (Ca, P, K, Mg, Fe, Zn, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Think of a waste stream as a used cardboard box..."**

Imagine you receive a delivery box packed with mixed items. Before discarding the box, you sort through it: you find some plastic bubble wrap (recyclable polymer), a few metal staples (recoverable metal), some wood shavings (combustible biomass), and traces of moisture from rain. Each item has a *value* and a *use*.

Similarly, an industrial byproduct is *not* truly "waste" — it is a **mixed parcel of valuable constituents** waiting to be separated and upgraded. The *composition* tells you **what is inside the parcel** so you can decide **how to extract, recover, or repurpose** each fraction.

For example:
- **Bagasse (sugarcane waste)** → contains cellulose (≈ 50%), hemicellulose (≈ 25%), and lignin (≈ 25%) — useful for bioethanol, paper, or biofuel pellets.
- **Dairy whey (cheese byproduct)** → contains lactose (≈ 5%), proteins (≈ 0.8%), and minerals — useful for food, feed, and bioplastics.
- **Fly ash (coal combustion byproduct)** → contains SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃, CaO — useful in cement and construction.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in Waste Composition Analysis:**
> - **Moisture Content (MC)** — expressed in **% wet basis (w.b.)** or **% dry basis (d.b.)** — *Standard reference: ISO 18134-1*.
> - **Volatile Solids (VS)** — indicator of organic fraction; expressed as **% of dry solids**.
> - **Ash Content** — inorganic residue after combustion at **550 °C** (Standard: ASTM E1755).
> - **Calorific Value (CV / HHV / LHV)** — measured in **MJ/kg** or **kcal/kg** (Standard: ASTM D5865, ISO 18125).
> - **C/N Ratio** — dimensionless ratio of Carbon to Nitrogen, critical for composting and anaerobic digestion.
> - **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD₅)** — mg O₂/L; **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** — mg O₂/L (Standards: IS 3025, APHA).

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic is the **foundation** of Module 2 because every downstream utilization decision (composting, biogas, bioethanol, extraction, animal feed, material recovery) depends on knowing **what is present in the waste** and **in what quantity**. Examiners frequently test:
- Identification of constituents in a given waste.
- Numerical interpretation of composition tables.
- Linking composition → suitable utilization pathway.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Valuable Constituents

Valuable constituents in wastes and byproducts are systematically classified into the following categories:

### 2.1.1 Organic Constituents
1. **Carbohydrates**
   - **Monosaccharides** (glucose, fructose, galactose) — found in fruit/vegetable wastes, molasses.
   - **Disaccharides** (sucrose, lactose, maltose) — found in sugar industry waste, dairy whey.
   - **Polysaccharides**:
     - **Cellulose** (β-1,4-glucan) — agricultural residues, forestry waste, MSW.
     - **Hemicellulose** (xylan, mannan, glucan) — agricultural residues.
     - **Starch** (α-1,4/α-1,6-glucan) — potato, cereal, fruit processing wastes.
     - **Pectin** (galacturonic acid polymer) — citrus peel, apple pomace.
     - **Chitin/Chitosan** — shellfish waste.
2. **Proteins** — dairy whey proteins, oilseed meals, slaughterhouse byproducts (blood meal, feather meal, fish meal).
3. **Lipids** — vegetable oil refining residues, tallow, used cooking oil, oilseed cakes.
4. **Lignin** — aromatic polymer; agricultural residues, paper mill waste (black liquor).
5. **Waxes, Resins, Tannins** — forest-based byproducts.

### 2.1.2 Inorganic Constituents
1. **Macro-elements** — N, P, K, Ca, Mg, S, Na.
2. **Micro-elements / Trace metals** — Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, B, Mo, Cl.
3. **Heavy metals (potentially toxic)** — Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr, As, Ni — must be monitored.
4. **Mineral oxides** — SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃, CaO, MgO, TiO₂ — in fly ash, slag, red mud.

### 2.1.3 Energy-Rich Fractions
- **Fixed Carbon** and **Volatile Matter** (in solid fuels like biomass, MSW, refuse-derived fuel).
- **Hydrocarbons** (in petrochemical sludges, plastics).

### 2.1.4 Water & Moisture
- Critical for biological processes (digestion, fermentation, composting).

---

## 2.2 Types of Compositional Analysis

### (a) Proximate Analysis
Determines four basic parameters:
- **Moisture content**
- **Volatile matter (VM)**
- **Fixed carbon (FC)**
- **Ash content**

### (b) Ultimate (Elemental) Analysis
Determines: **C, H, O, N, S** (sometimes also Cl).

### (c) Biochemical Composition Analysis
Determines: Cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, extractives, pectin, protein, lipid, etc.

### (d) Structural Carbohydrate Analysis (Van Soest / Detergent Fiber Method)
- **Neutral Detergent Fiber (NDF)** = Cellulose + Hemicellulose + Lignin.
- **Acid Detergent Fiber (ADF)** = Cellulose + Lignin.
- **Acid Detergent Lignin (ADL)** = Lignin.
- By difference: Hemicellulose = NDF − ADF; Cellulose = ADF − ADL.

### (e) Calorific Value Determination
- **HHV (Higher Heating Value)** via Bomb Calorimeter.
- **LHV (Lower Heating Value)** = HHV − 2.442 × (9H + M), where H = hydrogen fraction, M = moisture %.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must know for solving numerical problems on this topic in KTU ESE/Internal exams.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Standard / Reference |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | Moisture Content (w.b.) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | ISO 18134-1 |
| 2 | Moisture Content (d.b.) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | ISO 18134-1 |
| 3 | Ash Content | $Ash = \dfrac{W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100$ | % d.b. | ASTM E1755 |
| 4 | Volatile Matter | $VM = \dfrac{W_d - W_{ash} - W_{FC}}{W_d} \times 100$ | % d.b. | ASTM E872 |
| 5 | Fixed Carbon | $FC = 100 - VM - Ash$ | % d.b. | By difference |
| 6 | HHV (Dulong's formula) | $HHV = 33.7C + 144.2(H - O/8) + 9.4S$ | MJ/kg | Dulong-Berthelot |
| 7 | LHV | $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left( \dfrac{9H}{100} + \dfrac{MC}{100} \right) \times 1000$ | MJ/kg | Energy basis |
| 8 | C/N Ratio | $C/N = \dfrac{\%C_{organic}}{\%N_{total}}$ | Dimensionless | Composting standard |
| 9 | VS (Volatile Solids) | $VS = 100 - Ash_{db}$ | % d.b. | APHA 2540 G |
| 10 | Cellulose (Van Soest) | $Cell = ADF - ADL$ | % d.b. | Van Soest method |
| 11 | Hemicellulose (Van Soest) | $Hemi = NDF - ADF$ | % d.b. | Van Soest method |
| 12 | Theoretical Biogas Yield (Buswell) | $C_nH_aO_bN_c + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \dfrac{n}{2}CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8}\right)CO_2 + cNH_3$ | mol | Buswell equation |
| 13 | Theoretical CH₄ Yield | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{0.4 \times VS \times 1000}{COD_{VS}}$ (approx) | m³ CH₄/kg VS | Empirical |

> **Note**: In all the above expressions, $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight, $W_{ash}$ = weight of ash residue, $W_{FC}$ = weight of fixed carbon, $C/H/O/S$ = mass fractions of elements (decimal or %).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Compositional Knowledge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Engineers Need This:**
> 1. **Process selection** — High cellulose + low lignin → enzymatic hydrolysis / bioethanol. High lignin + low N → direct combustion / pyrolysis.
> 2. **Feasibility analysis** — High moisture waste (food waste, whey) → anaerobic digestion. Low moisture, high CV → incineration/pelletization.
> 3. **Material recovery yield estimation** — % pectin in orange peel dictates commercial extraction economics.
> 4. **Environmental compliance** — C/N ratio dictates compost stability; heavy metal content dictates land application limits.
> 5. **Economic valuation** — Calorific value × mass = energy recovery potential (kWh or MJ).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Proximate & Ultimate Analysis of Bagasse

**Problem Statement:**
A sample of sugarcane bagasse has the following laboratory data:
- Wet mass of sample = **120 g**
- Mass after oven drying at 105 °C for 24 h = **48 g**
- Mass after muffle furnace combustion at 550 °C = **6.5 g**
- Elemental (ultimate) analysis (dry basis): C = 47.5%, H = 6.0%, O = 42.0%, N = 0.4%, S = 0.1%, Ash = 4.0% (cross-check)
- Bomb calorimeter HHV = **18.6 MJ/kg** (dry basis)

**Required:** Calculate (a) Moisture content (w.b.), (b) Moisture content (d.b.), (c) Ash content (d.b.), (d) Volatile matter (d.b.) assuming the volatile matter drives off between 105–550 °C, and (e) Verify Dulong's HHV.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Moisture content (wet basis):**

Using the formula from the cheat sheet:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

Substituting the values:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{120 - 48}{120} \times 100$$

Performing the subtraction in the numerator first:
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{72}{120} \times 100$$

Dividing and multiplying:
$$MC_{wb} = 0.60 \times 100 = 60.0\,\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Bagasse as collected contains 60% water by wet mass — a high-moisture lignocellulosic waste.

**Step (b) — Moisture content (dry basis):**

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{120 - 48}{48} \times 100$$

$$MC_{db} = \frac{72}{48} \times 100 = 1.5 \times 100 = 150.0\,\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Every 1 kg of dry bagasse carries 1.5 kg of water when fresh.

**Step (c) — Ash content (dry basis):**

$$Ash = \frac{W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{6.5}{48} \times 100$$

$$Ash = 0.1354 \times 100 \approx 13.54\,\%$$

> **Note:** This is the *measured* ash on the wet/dry sample basis used here; for a rigorous lab result it would be reported on dry basis directly. We are demonstrating the calculation.

**Step (d) — Volatile Matter (d.b.):**

We have:
- After drying at 105 °C → $W_d$ = 48 g (loss = moisture)
- After combustion at 550 °C → $W_{ash}$ = 6.5 g (loss = volatile matter + fixed carbon)
- Therefore the loss between dry mass and ash = VM + FC.

For typical biomass (lignocellulosic) where fixed carbon is small, we approximate:
$$VM \approx \frac{W_d - W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{48 - 6.5}{48} \times 100$$

$$VM = \frac{41.5}{48} \times 100 = 0.8646 \times 100 \approx 86.46\,\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Bagasse is highly volatile (organic-rich) — ideal for pyrolysis, gasification, or combustion.

**Step (e) — Verification of Dulong's HHV:**

Dulong's formula (where C, H, O, S are in **mass % on dry basis**):
$$HHV = 0.337\,C + 1.442\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.094\,S \quad [\text{MJ/kg}]$$

Substituting C = 47.5, H = 6.0, O = 42.0, S = 0.1:

$$HHV = 0.337 \times 47.5 + 1.442 \times \left(6.0 - \frac{42.0}{8}\right) + 0.094 \times 0.1$$

Compute the term inside the bracket:
$$6.0 - \frac{42.0}{8} = 6.0 - 5.25 = 0.75$$

Now evaluate each term:
$$HHV = 16.01 + 1.442 \times 0.75 + 0.0094$$

$$HHV = 16.01 + 1.0815 + 0.0094$$

$$HHV \approx 17.10 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

This is **close to** the bomb-calorimeter value of 18.6 MJ/kg (within ~8% — acceptable for empirical Dulong prediction; deviation is expected because Dulong ignores moisture and ash contributions).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Biochemical Composition (Van Soest Method)

**Problem Statement:**
A sample of rice straw analyzed by Van Soest detergent fiber method gave:
- NDF = 72.0% (d.b.)
- ADF = 51.0% (d.b.)
- ADL = 8.5% (d.b.)

**Required:** Determine (a) Hemicellulose, (b) Cellulose, (c) Lignin content (d.b.).

### Solution

**Step (a) — Hemicellulose:**
$$Hemi = NDF - ADF = 72.0 - 51.0 = 21.0\,\%$$

**Step (b) — Cellulose:**
$$Cell = ADF - ADL = 51.0 - 8.5 = 42.5\,\%$$

**Step (c) — Lignin:**
$$Lignin = ADL = 8.5\,\%$$

**Verification — total structural carbohydrate + lignin:**
$$Hemi + Cell + Lignin = 21.0 + 42.5 + 8.5 = 72.0\,\%$$

This matches NDF exactly, confirming the calculation.

> **Engineering implication:** Cellulose (42.5%) and hemicellulose (21.0%) together form 63.5% of rice straw — making it an excellent feedstock for bioethanol or biogas via biological conversion.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table (typical % dry basis unless stated)** — This is a favorite KTU exam table to reproduce/interpret.

| Waste / Byproduct | Industry | Cellulose | Hemicellulose | Lignin | Protein | Lipid | Ash | C/N | HHV (MJ/kg) |
|-------------------|----------|-----------|---------------|--------|---------|-------|-----|-----|-------------|
| Sugarcane bagasse | Sugar | 40–45 | 25–30 | 20–25 | 1–2 | 1–2 | 1–4 | 80–120 | 17–19 |
| Rice straw | Agriculture | 32–38 | 20–25 | 8–12 | 3–5 | 1–2 | 12–18 | 50–80 | 14–16 |
| Wheat straw | Agriculture | 33–40 | 20–25 | 15–20 | 3–5 | 1–2 | 6–9 | 60–100 | 16–18 |
| Corn stover | Agriculture | 35–40 | 20–25 | 15–20 | 4–6 | 1–2 | 5–7 | 50–70 | 16–18 |
| Sawdust | Forestry | 40–50 | 20–30 | 25–35 | <1 | <1 | <1 | 400–700 | 18–20 |
| Fruit pomace | Food | 15–25 | 8–12 | 10–20 | 5–10 | 5–15 | 3–6 | 30–50 | 18–20 |
| Citrus peel | Food | 12–20 | 6–10 | <5 | 6–8 | 2–5 | 3–5 | 25–35 | 16–18 |
| Whey (dry) | Dairy | 0 | 0 | 0 | 11–14 | 1–2 | 8–10 | — | 15–17 |
| Distillery spent wash | Distillery | — | — | — | 8–12 | 2–4 | 15–25 | 8–12 | 10–13 |
| MSW (mixed) | Municipal | 30–50 | 10–20 | 10–20 | 8–15 | 5–10 | 15–30 | 30–60 | 8–14 |
| Fly ash | Coal/Power | 0 (inorganic) | — | — | — | — | 90–99 | — | — |
| Red mud | Alumina | 0 (inorganic) | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| Sewage sludge | Municipal | 8–15 | 5–10 | 8–12 | 20–30 | 5–15 | 25–40 | 6–12 | 10–15 |
| Poultry litter | Animal | — | — | — | 25–35 | 2–4 | 15–25 | 8–15 | 10–13 |
| Fish meal byproduct | Marine | — | — | — | 60–75 | 8–15 | 10–20 | 4–6 | 18–22 |

> **Reading the matrix:** The composition directly dictates the utilization pathway. For example, a high C/N (>40) waste like sawdust needs nitrogen supplementation for composting; a low C/N (<15) waste like fish meal can cause ammonia toxicity in compost and is best used as feed or for oil recovery.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Composition-to-Utilization Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn[Input: Waste Sample with Known Composition] --> q1{High Moisture above 60 percent?}
    q1 -- yes --> pathA[Liquid or Sludge Treatment]
    q1 -- no --> q2{High Cellulose plus Hemicellulose above 50 percent?}

    pathA --> pathA1[Anaerobic Digestion for Biogas]
    pathA --> pathA2[Fermentation to Value-added Product]

    q2 -- yes --> q3{Lignin Content}
    q2 -- no --> pathB[Non-cellulosic Pathway]

    q3 -- low lignin below 15 percent --> q3a[Enzymatic Hydrolysis plus Bioethanol]
    q3 -- high lignin above 20 percent --> q3b[Thermochemical Conversion: Pyrolysis or Gasification]

    pathB --> q4{Dominant Fraction}
    q4 -- Protein above 20 percent --> pathB1[Animal Feed or Protein Extraction]
    q4 -- Lipid above 10 percent --> pathB2[Biodiesel via Transesterification]

    q3a --> outA[Bioethanol Plant]

    pathA1 --> outC[Biogas Plant]
    pathB1 --> outD[Feed Industry]
    pathB2 --> outE[Biodiesel Refinery]
    pathB3 --> outF[Cement Plant]
    pathB4 --> outG[Pectin Industry]
```

> **Why this diagram is essential for KTU exams:** Examiners often give a "composition snippet" and ask students to **identify the most suitable utilization route**. The above decision tree is the systematic way to answer such 7-mark or 14-mark questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "valuable constituents" in the context of waste and byproduct utilization. List any four categories of valuable constituents with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Valuable constituents are the recoverable, commercially exploitable chemical and biochemical components present in waste streams that make them suitable for utilization rather than disposal.

| Category | Example |
|----------|---------|
| Cellulose | Sugarcane bagasse |
| Protein | Dairy whey |
| Lipid | Used cooking oil |
| Mineral oxide (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) | Fly ash |

*(Any 4 categories with correct example → 3 marks; 2 categories → 2 marks; 1 category → 1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between proximate analysis and ultimate analysis of a solid waste.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis |
|--------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Purpose | Determines combustible & non-combustible fractions | Determines elemental composition |
| Parameters | Moisture, VM, Ash, FC | C, H, O, N, S |
| Standards | ASTM E1755, E872 | ASTM D5373, CHNS analyzer |
| Engineering use | Energy classification, process selection | Combustion stoichiometry, emissions |

*(Correct differentiation with both parameters & use → 3 marks; partial → 1.5–2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **biochemical composition of lignocellulosic wastes** with reference to cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. Discuss the **Van Soest detergent fiber method** for their quantitative determination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The proximate analysis of a municipal solid waste (MSW) sample gave the following data (on dry basis): Moisture = 45% (w.b.), Volatile Matter = 52%, Ash = 28%, Fixed Carbon = 20%. The ultimate analysis (d.b.) is: C = 38%, H = 5.5%, O = 22%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.3%. Calculate the **HHV using Dulong's formula** and the **LHV** assuming a 5% remaining moisture (d.b.). Comment on the suitability of this MSW for **incineration with energy recovery**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

**1. Biochemical composition of lignocellulosic wastes (4 marks):**

Lignocellulosic wastes (agricultural residues, forestry waste, MSW organic fraction) are predominantly composed of three structural biopolymers along with minor extractives and ash:

- **Cellulose** (40–50% in most agricultural residues): A linear polymer of D-glucose units linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds. Forms crystalline microfibrils providing structural rigidity. Hydrolyzable to glucose by cellulase enzymes or acid.
- **Hemicellulose** (20–30%): A branched heteropolymer of pentoses (xylose, arabinose) and hexoses (mannose, galactose, glucose). Amorphous and easier to hydrolyze than cellulose.
- **Lignin** (15–30%): A three-dimensional aromatic polymer of phenylpropane units (coniferyl, sinapyl, p-coumaryl alcohols). Provides waterproofing and structural support; highly resistant to microbial/enzymatic degradation.
- **Extractives & Ash** (5–15%): Waxes, resins, tannins, and inorganic minerals.

**2. Van Soest detergent fiber method (3 marks):**

| Step | Reagent | Dissolves | Residue |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Neutral Detergent (NDF solution) | Soluble sugars, pectin, protein, lipids | **NDF** = Cellulose + Hemicellulose + Lignin |
| 2 | Acid Detergent (ADF solution) | Hemicellulose | **ADF** = Cellulose + Lignin |
| 3 | 72% H₂SO₄ (ADL step) | Cellulose | **ADL** = Lignin |

**Calculations:**
- Hemicellulose = NDF − ADF
- Cellulose = ADF − ADL
- Lignin = ADL

This sequential extraction enables quantitative determination of each structural component.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**Given (dry basis):** C = 38%, H = 5.5%, O = 22%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.3%, remaining moisture M = 5% (d.b.).

**Step 1 — HHV by Dulong's formula:**
$$HHV = 0.337\,C + 1.442\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.094\,S \quad [\text{MJ/kg}]$$

$$HHV = 0.337 \times 38 + 1.442 \times \left(5.5 - \frac{22}{8}\right) + 0.094 \times 0.3$$

Compute the bracket:
$$5.5 - \frac{22}{8} = 5.5 - 2.75 = 2.75$$

Substitute:
$$HHV = 12.806 + 1.442 \times 2.75 + 0.0282$$

$$HHV = 12.806 + 3.9655 + 0.0282 = 16.80 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — LHV calculation:**

The LHV accounts for the heat lost in vaporizing the moisture (and water from hydrogen combustion). The simplified formula:
$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left(\frac{9H}{100} + \frac{M}{100}\right) \times 1000 / 1000$$

Using MJ/kg basis (with H as mass fraction = 0.055, M as mass fraction = 0.05):
$$LHV = 16.80 - 2.442 \times (9 \times 0.055 + 0.05)$$

$$LHV = 16.80 - 2.442 \times (0.495 + 0.05) = 16.80 - 2.442 \times 0.545$$

$$LHV = 16.80 - 1.331 = 15.47 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on suitability for incineration with energy recovery (2 marks):**

- HHV of 16.80 MJ/kg (d.b.) is in the typical range for MSW (8–14 MJ/kg as-received, up to 18–20 MJ/kg d.b.).
- After moisture correction (45% w.b.), the as-received CV is approximately $16.80 \times (1 - 0.45) \approx 9.24$ MJ/kg.
- This is **at the lower threshold for autogenous combustion** (typically requires ≥7–10 MJ/kg as-received).
- High ash (28%) reduces the combustible fraction.
- **Verdict:** Marginal/conditional suitability. Pre-drying or co-combustion with high-CV fuel (e.g., coal, biomass pellets) is recommended for efficient energy recovery.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **composition of food industry byproducts** with specific reference to **(i) dairy whey**, **(ii) fruit/vegetable pomace**, and **(iii) oilseed cakes**. For each, identify the dominant valuable constituent and the most appropriate utilization pathway. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of **rice husk** yields the following data:
- Wet weight = 100 g
- Weight after drying at 105 °C = 88 g
- Weight after combustion at 550 °C = 17.5 g
- Bomb calorimeter HHV = 15.4 MJ/kg (d.b.)
- Elemental analysis (d.b.): C = 38.5%, H = 5.0%, O = 36.0%, N = 0.4%, S = 0.1%

Calculate the **moisture content (w.b.)**, **ash content (d.b.)**, **volatile matter (d.b.)**, **fixed carbon (d.b.)**, and **verify the HHV using Dulong's formula**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**(i) Dairy whey (2 marks):**
- Composition: Water (~93%), Lactose (4.5–5%), Whey proteins (0.6–0.8%), Minerals (0.5–0.7%), Lipids (trace).
- Dominant valuable constituent: **Lactose** and **whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin)**.
- Utilization: Direct spray-drying into whey powder for food/feed; ultrafiltration for protein isolates; fermentation to produce **bioplastics (PHB)** or **lactic acid**; prebiotic galacto-oligosaccharide production.

**(ii) Fruit/vegetable pomace (2.5 marks):**
- Composition (e.g., apple pomace): Pectin (5–15%), Cellulose (15–25%), Hemicellulose (8–12%), Sugars (10–25%), Polyphenols (1–3%), Crude fiber.
- Dominant valuable constituent: **Pectin** (in citrus/apple pomace) or **dietary fiber** (in grape/olive pomace).
- Utilization: Pectin extraction (acid hydrolysis → precipitation); polyphenol/antioxidant recovery (solvent extraction); substrate for solid-state fermentation (enzyme, mushroom, or single-cell protein production); animal feed.

**(iii) Oilseed cakes (2.5 marks):**
- Composition (e.g., soybean meal, groundnut cake): Crude protein (35–50%), Crude fiber (8–15%), Residual oil (1–8%), Minerals (4–7%), Anti-nutritional factors (gossypol in cottonseed, glucosinolates in mustard).
- Dominant valuable constituent: **Protein** (high biological value for soybean; medium for others).
- Utilization: Animal feed (after detoxification); protein concentrate/isolate for human food; substrate for **enzymatic hydrolysis** to produce bioactive peptides; fermentation for **single-cell protein**; biodiesel (from residual oil fraction).

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- $W_w$ = 100 g
- $W_d$ (after 105 °C) = 88 g
- $W_{ash}$ (after 550 °C) = 17.5 g

**Step 1 — Moisture content (w.b.):**
$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{100 - 88}{100} \times 100 = \frac{12}{100} \times 100 = 12.0\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Ash content (d.b.):**
$$Ash_{db} = \frac{W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{17.5}{88} \times 100 = 0.1989 \times 100 \approx 19.89\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Volatile matter (d.b.):**

For biomass, the mass loss between dry state and ash is dominated by volatile matter (with a small FC residue considered within the difference if not separately measured):
$$VM_{db} = \frac{W_d - W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{88 - 17.5}{88} \times 100 = \frac{70.5}{88} \times 100 \approx 80.11\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Fixed carbon (d.b.) by difference:**
$$FC = 100 - VM - Ash = 100 - 80.11 - 19.89 = 0.00\,\%$$

> **Note:** This near-zero FC is typical of rice husk — its high ash (~20%, mostly silica) leaves very little fixed carbon, making it a low-grade solid fuel by itself but excellent for **gasification** and **silica extraction**.

**Step 5 — Verification of HHV using Dulong's formula:**

$$HHV = 0.337\,C + 1.442\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.094\,S$$

$$HHV = 0.337 \times 38.5 + 1.442 \times \left(5.0 - \frac{36.0}{8}\right) + 0.094 \times 0.1$$

Compute the bracket:
$$5.0 - \frac{36.0}{8} = 5.0 - 4.5 = 0.5$$

Substitute:
$$HHV = 12.9745 + 1.442 \times 0.5 + 0.0094$$

$$HHV = 12.9745 + 0.721 + 0.0094 = 13.70 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Comparison with bomb calorimeter (15.4 MJ/kg):**
- Deviation = (15.4 − 13.70)/15.4 × 100 = ~11%
- This deviation arises because Dulong's formula does not account for the high silica/ash contribution to the energy balance, and assumes complete combustion of organic matter.
- **Conclusion:** The measured HHV (15.4 MJ/kg) is more reliable; Dulong gives a lower-bound estimate for high-ash biomass like rice husk.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing % wet basis vs. % dry basis** — Examiners explicitly test this. Always state the basis in your final answer. *Loss: up to 1 mark per question.*
> 2. **Wrong formula for HHV** — Students often write Dulong's formula with incorrect unit conversions (using MJ/kg but plugging % values, or vice versa). The standard form **0.337C + 1.442(H − O/8) + 0.094S in MJ/kg** requires **C, H, O, S in mass %**. *Loss: up to 2 marks.*
> 3. **Skipping "remaining moisture" in LHV** — LHV is always lower than HHV; missing the moisture-correction term loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the C/N ratio interpretation** — Many students compute C/N without commenting on its implication (composting requires 25–35; >40 needs N supplementation). *Loss: 1–2 marks in long answers.*
> 5. **Van Soest step confusion** — Writing "NDF = Cellulose + Lignin" (this is ADF, not NDF). Memorize the hierarchy clearly. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **Failing to comment on ash effect on CV** — High ash → low CV. Examiners expect at least one sentence linking the composition to the utilization decision. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "What to memorize for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic":**

- ✅ **Definition of proximate analysis** — Moisture, VM, Ash, FC (sum = 100% on d.b.).
- ✅ **Definition of ultimate analysis** — C, H, O, N, S (sum ≈ 100% on d.b., ash excluded).
- ✅ **Dulong's formula** — $HHV = 0.337C + 1.442(H - O/8) + 0.094S$ [MJ/kg] with C/H/O/S in mass %.
- ✅ **LHV correction** — Subtract 2.442 × (9H/100 + M/100) for moisture and water of combustion.
- ✅ **Van Soest hierarchy** — NDF (Cell + Hemi + Lignin) → ADF (Cell + Lignin) → ADL (Lignin).
- ✅ **Hemicellulose = NDF − ADF**; **Cellulose = ADF − ADL**.
- ✅ **Moisture formulas** — Know both w.b. and d.b. forms; remember the relationship $MC_{db} = MC_{wb}/(1 - MC_{wb}/100)$.
- ✅ **C/N ratio benchmarks** — Composting ideal 25–35; <15 ammonia toxicity; >40 nitrogen-deficient.
- ✅ **HHV ranges** — Remember: Wood biomass 17–20, MSW 8–14, Sewage sludge 10–15, Coal 24–30 MJ/kg (d.b.).
- ✅ **Composition-to-pathway logic** — High cellulose+hemicellulose + low lignin → biochemical route; high lignin → thermochemical; high lipid → biodiesel; high protein → feed; high SiO₂/Al₂O₃ → construction.
- ✅ **Analytical standards** — ASTM E1755 (ash), ASTM E872 (VM), ISO 18134-1 (moisture), ASTM D5865 (HHV), APHA 2540 (VS).
- ✅ **Standard temperatures** — Drying at **105 °C**; Ash at **550 °C**; VM at **950 °C** in covered crucible.
- ✅ **Buswell equation** for theoretical methane yield from a generic organic formula $C_nH_aO_bN_c$ — recall coefficients in the cheat sheet.
- ✅ **Typical compositions** of at least 5 common wastes: bagasse, rice straw, whey, MSW, fly ash (numbers in the analytical matrix above).

> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a numerical composition question appears, **always end with a 1–2 line engineering interpretation** (e.g., "suitable for anaerobic digestion", "requires co-combustion", "good feedstock for bioethanol"). This single sentence often earns 1–2 **bonus** marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utilization of rice husk: Production of silica and cement from rice husk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Utilization of rice husk: Production of silica and cement from rice husk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rice Husk (RH)** is an agricultural lignocellulosic byproduct generated during the milling of paddy (rice), constituting approximately **20–25%** of the total paddy weight by mass. Chemically, rice husk is composed of cellulose (35%), hemicellulose (25%), lignin (20%), and **silica (SiO₂)** in the range of **15–28%** on a dry weight basis, making it one of the most abundant silica-rich biomass residues in the world.

**Rice Husk Ash (RHA)** is the inorganic residue obtained after the complete combustion of rice husk under controlled thermal conditions. RHA is primarily composed of **amorphous silica (80–97%)** along with minor quantities of metallic oxides (K₂O, CaO, MgO, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃) and unburnt carbon (Loss on Ignition, LOI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silica from Rice Husk** refers to the high-purity silicon dioxide (SiO₂) extracted from RHA through chemical (acid/alkali leaching) or thermal routes, with applications spanning construction, electronics, pharmaceuticals, and catalysis.

> [!NOTE]
