# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Process Architecture

The industrial production of ethanol is canonically decomposed into **five sequential unit operations**, each of which is essential to convert raw biomass into fuel-grade bioethanol.

### **Stage 1 — Feedstock Preparation & Pretreatment**
The objective of this stage is to **unlock the structural sugars** trapped within the biomass. Different feedstocks require different pretreatments:
- **Sugarcane molasses / sugar juices** → dilution with water to **15–25 % w/v total sugars** (no chemical pretreatment required).
- **Starch-rich materials** (corn, cassava) → **gelatinization** (heating to **80–90 °C** in water) followed by **enzymatic liquefaction** (α-amylase at **pH 6.0–6.5, 85 °C**) and **saccharification** (glucoamylase at **pH 4.5, 60 °C**) to yield glucose.
- **Lignocellulosic biomass** (bagasse, rice straw, wood chips) → **mechanical size reduction** → **acid/alkaline/steam-explosion pretreatment** to break **lignin seal** and **hemicellulose sheath** → **enzymatic hydrolysis** (cellulase cocktail) to release glucose and xylose.

### **Stage 2 — Hydrolysis (Saccharification)**
Complex polysaccharides (starch, cellulose, hemicellulose) are enzymatically cleaved into **monomeric fermentable sugars** (glucose, xylose, arabinose). This stage is **not required** for free-sugar feedstocks like molasses.

### **Stage 3 — Fermentation (The Heart of the Process)**
**Saccharomyces cerevisiae** yeast (or *Zymomonas mobilis* bacteria) is the workhorse. Under **anaerobic conditions**, the yeast metabolizes hexose sugars via the **Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) pathway** to produce ethanol and CO₂.

The master reaction (Gay-Lussac equation):

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{heat}$$

Operating parameters:
- **Temperature:** 30–35 °C (mesophilic)
- **pH:** 4.0–5.0 (acidic, prevents contamination)
- **Fermentation time:** 48–72 hours (batch); continuous ~ 24 h residence time
- **Final broth ethanol concentration:** 6–12 % v/v (limited by **product inhibition** at > 12 %)

### **Stage 4 — Distillation**
The fermented "beer" (containing only 6–12 % ethanol) is fed to **distillation columns** where it is concentrated toward the **95.6 % v/v azeotrope**. Multi-column systems include:
- **Beer column (stripper):** removes dissolved CO₂, strips ethanol from solids (stillage).
- **Rectification column:** concentrates ethanol to ~ 95 % azeotrope.
- **Side-stream purifiers:** remove **fusel oils** (higher alcohols — amyl, isoamyl, propyl) and **aldehydes** (acetaldehyde).

### **Stage 5 — Dehydration (Anhydrous Ethanol Production)**
The 95.6 % azeotrope cannot be broken by simple distillation. Industrial methods include:
| Method | Principle | Energy Use |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| **Azeotropic Distillation** | Add **benzene** or **cyclohexane** as entrainer | High |
| **Extractive Distillation** | Use **ethylene glycol** as solvent | Moderate |
| **Vacuum Distillation** | Lower pressure shifts azeotrope | Energy-intensive |
| **Molecular Sieve Adsorption (PSA)** | **3 Å zeolite** selectively adsorbs water | **Lowest** (preferred) |
| **Membrane Pervaporation** | Hydrophilic membranes (NaA zeolite) | Low |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Gay-Lussac Stoichiometry** | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$ | — | Theoretical yield basis |
| 2 | **Theoretical Ethanol Yield** | $Y_{th} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.511$ | g ethanol / g glucose | Maximum possible |
| 3 | **Practical Yield (typical)** | $Y_p = 0.45$ to $0.48$ | g/g | ~ 90 % of theoretical |
| 4 | **Stoichiometric CO₂ Release** | $0.489$ | g CO₂ / g glucose | Byproduct credit |
| 5 | **Distillate Mass Balance** | $F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$ | kg & mass fraction | Material balance on column |
| 6 | **Reflux Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | dimensionless | Column design |
| 7 | **Azeotropic Composition (101.325 kPa)** | $95.6$ | % v/v | Distillation ceiling |
| 8 | **Specific Energy Demand (modern)** | $1.5$ to $2.5$ | MJ / L ethanol | Energy benchmarking |
| 9 | **Fermentation Dilution Sugar** | $15$ to $25$ | % w/v | Optimum substrate |
| 10 | **Glucose to Ethanol (mass)** | $\dfrac{2(46)}{180} = 0.511$ | dimensionless | — |
| 11 | **Glucose to Ethanol (mol)** | $2$ mol EtOH / mol glucose | mol/mol | — |
| 12 | **Distillate–Feed Fenske minimum plates** | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ (x_D / x_W)(1-x_W)/(1-x_D) \right]}{\ln \alpha}$ | plates | Column design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the bedrock for designing **bioreactors, distillation columns, and energy integration systems** in bio-refineries. KTU exam problems often require you to compute **ethanol output** from a given quantity of molasses or biomass using the **Gay-Lussac theoretical yield** corrected by a **practical efficiency factor** (typically **85–90 %**).

## 2.3 Why This Process Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Significance:**
> - **Energy Security:** Ethanol is blended with petrol (E10 = 10 % ethanol, E85 = 85 % ethanol) to reduce dependence on crude oil.
> - **Waste Valorization:** Molasses (a sugar-industry byproduct) and bagasse (a sugar-mill lignocellulosic waste) are converted into a high-value product, embodying the **circular bioeconomy** principle.
> - **Greenhouse Gas Mitigation:** Ethanol from biomass has a **CO₂-neutral** lifecycle (the CO₂ released during combustion is reabsorbed by the next crop).
> - **Industrial Solvent & Chemical Feedstock:** Used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, beverages, and as a precursor to **ethylene, acetaldehyde, and ethyl acetate**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Ethanol Yield from Glucose

We will derive, step by step, the **theoretical maximum mass of ethanol** obtainable from a given mass of glucose.

### Given
- Molecular weight of glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$): **180 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$): **46 g/mol**
- Molecular weight of CO₂: **44 g/mol**
- Balanced reaction: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$

### Step 1 — Confirm the Mass Balance
We verify that the reaction is **atom-balanced**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carbon (Left):}\ & 6 \\
\text{Carbon (Right):}\ & 2(2) + 2(1) = 6 \ \checkmark \\
\text{Hydrogen (Left):}\ & 12 \\
\text{Hydrogen (Right):}\ & 2(6) + 2(0) = 12 \ \checkmark \\
\text{Oxygen (Left):}\ & 6 \\
\text{Oxygen (Right):}\ & 2(1) + 2(2) = 6 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The reaction is balanced.

### Step 2 — Compute Moles of Ethanol from 1 Mole of Glucose
From the stoichiometry, **1 mole of glucose** produces **2 moles of ethanol**:

$$n_{EtOH} = 2 \times n_{glucose}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Moles to Mass
The mass of ethanol produced is:

$$m_{EtOH} = n_{EtOH} \times M_{EtOH} = 2 \times n_{glucose} \times 46 \text{ g/mol}$$

The mass of glucose consumed is:

$$m_{glucose} = n_{glucose} \times 180 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Theoretical Yield (g/g)
Dividing the mass of ethanol by the mass of glucose:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{th} &= \dfrac{m_{EtOH}}{m_{glucose}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} \\
&= \dfrac{92}{180} \\
&= 0.5111\ \text{g ethanol per g glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Practical Yield Correction
Real fermentations never reach theoretical yield due to:
- **Cell mass synthesis** (~ 5 % of substrate diverted to yeast growth)
- **Byproduct formation** (glycerol, acetic acid)
- **Cell maintenance energy** (ATP consumption)

Assuming an industrial fermentation efficiency of **90 %**:

$$Y_p = 0.5111 \times 0.90 = 0.460 \ \text{g ethanol / g glucose}$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A sugar mill generates **1,000 kg of molasses** containing **50 % w/w total fermentable sugars (sucrose + glucose + fructose)**. Calculate the maximum ethanol that can be produced, assuming 90 % fermentation efficiency and 95 % distillation recovery.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sugar available} &= 1000 \times 0.50 = 500\ \text{kg} \\
\text{Theoretical ethanol} &= 500 \times 0.5111 = 255.55\ \text{kg} \\
\text{After fermentation (90\%)} &= 255.55 \times 0.90 = 230.00\ \text{kg} \\
\text{After distillation (95\%)} &= 230.00 \times 0.95 = 218.50\ \text{kg ethanol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Volume equivalent** (using ethanol density = 0.789 kg/L):

$$V = \dfrac{218.50}{0.789} = 276.9\ \text{L of pure ethanol}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Material Balance on the Distillation Column

### Given
- Feed ($F$) = fermented beer at 8 % v/v ethanol
- Distillate ($D$) = 95 % v/v azeotrope
- Bottoms ($W$) = stillage (spent wash)

### Step 1 — Set Up Mass Balance (overall)
$$F = D + W$$

### Step 2 — Ethanol Component Balance
$$F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Recovery Ratio
$$R_{EtOH} = \dfrac{D \cdot x_D}{F \cdot x_F} = \dfrac{\text{Ethanol in distillate}}{\text{Ethanol in feed}}$$

For $x_F = 0.08$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_W \approx 0.001$:

$$R_{EtOH} = \dfrac{0.95}{0.08} \approx 11.875\ \text{(i.e., feed / distillate ratio)}$$

This means roughly **12 kg of beer** is required to produce **1 kg of 95 % distillate** — illustrating why **distillation is the most energy-intensive** step.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Feedstocks

| Feature | Sugarcane Molasses | Corn Starch | Lignocellulose (Bagasse) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Pretreatment Needed** | Dilution only | Gelatinization + Enzymatic | Acid / Steam Explosion + Enzymatic |
| **Sugar Form** | Sucrose, glucose, fructose | Starch (polysaccharide) | Cellulose + Hemicellulose |
| **Hydrolysis Step** | **Not required** | Required (α-amylase) | Required (cellulase) |
| **Process Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **CO₂ Footprint** | Low | Medium | Lowest (waste-based) |
| **Industrial Maturity** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ (emerging) |
| **KTU Exam Relevance** | **Very High** | High | Very High (Module 3 focus) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define bioethanol and list any four feedstocks used in its production.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Bioethanol** is ethyl alcohol (C₂H₅OH) produced from **biological feedstocks** through **fermentation of carbohydrates** followed by **distillation and dehydration**. It is a **renewable liquid biofuel** that can be blended with gasoline.
>
> **Four common feedstocks:**
> 1. **Sugarcane molasses** (byproduct of sugar industry)
> 2. **Corn / maize starch** (food grain)
> 3. **Cassava / tapioca** (tuber)
> 4. **Lignocellulosic biomass** — bagasse, rice straw, corn stover, sawdust
> 5. *(bonus)* Municipal solid waste, paper pulp, whey

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for chemical formula + 1 for feedstocks)*

---

### **Question 2: What is the significance of the 95.6 % v/v azeotrope in ethanol–water distillation?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **ethanol–water azeotrope** is a constant-boiling mixture at **95.6 % v/v ethanol and 4.4 % v/v water** (boiling point **78.2 °C**, slightly below pure ethanol). 
>
> **Significance:**
> - Simple distillation **cannot exceed** this composition, regardless of the number of trays.
> - To produce **anhydrous / fuel-grade ethanol (> 99.5 %)**, special methods are required — **azeotropic distillation** (with benzene), **molecular sieve adsorption (3 Å zeolite)**, or **membrane pervaporation**.
> - The azeotrope represents the **thermodynamic barrier** that defines the boundary between *hydrated* and *anhydrous* ethanol specifications.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for limitation + 1 for solution methods)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by writing the **Gay-Lussac equation** without verifying its **atom balance** — always check C, H, O atoms!
> - In numerical yield problems, forgetting to apply the **practical efficiency factor (85–90 %)** is a **classic 1-mark deduction**.
> - Confusing **hydrous azeotrope (95.6 %)** with **anhydrous fuel ethanol (> 99.5 %)** and missing the **molecular sieve / azeotropic distillation step** costs 1–2 marks.
> - Forgetting to convert **mass of ethanol → volume** (using **ρ = 0.789 kg/L**) when the question asks for litres — **frequently penalized**.

---

### **Question A: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Module 3]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **production of ethanol from sugarcane molasses**. Discuss the role of **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** and the various **byproducts** obtained at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sugar industry produces **2,500 kg/day of molasses** containing **48 % w/w total fermentable sugars**. Calculate the **theoretical and practical (90 % efficiency) ethanol yield**, and the **volume of anhydrous ethanol** recovered (assume distillation efficiency = 95 %, ethanol density = 0.789 kg/L). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**1. Process Description (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation:** Molasses is diluted with warm water to bring the sugar concentration to **15–25 % w/v**. The pH is adjusted to **4.0–5.0** using sulphuric acid. Nutrients (nitrogen source — urea; phosphorus — diammonium phosphate) are added.

**Step 2 — Fermentation:** Diluted molasses is charged into a **fermentation tank** and inoculated with **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** yeast at **5 % v/v** of the working volume. The tank is maintained at **30–35 °C** under **anaerobic conditions** for **48–72 hours**.

**Role of S. cerevisiae:**
- Metabolizes **hexose sugars** (glucose, fructose) via the **Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway**.
- Enzyme **pyruvate decarboxylase** converts pyruvate to acetaldehyde; **alcohol dehydrogenase** then reduces acetaldehyde to ethanol.
- Releases **2 mol ethanol + 2 mol CO₂** per mole of glucose.
- Tolerates up to **12–15 % v/v ethanol** (above which it self-inhibits).

**Step 3 — Distillation:** The fermented "beer" (6–10 % v/v ethanol) is fed to a **beer column (stripper)** to release dissolved CO₂ and separate ethanol from solids → then to a **rectification column** to concentrate to **95.6 % azeotrope**.

**Step 4 — Dehydration:** The azeotrope is passed through a **3 Å molecular sieve** that selectively adsorbs water, yielding **anhydrous ethanol (99.5 % v/v)**.

**2. Byproducts (2 marks):**

| Stage | Byproduct | Use / Disposal |
|-------|-----------|----------------|
| Fermentation | **CO₂** (0.49 g/g glucose) | Beverage carbonation, dry ice, greenhouse |
| Centrifugation | **Yeast sludge** | Sold as single-cell protein / fertilizer |
| Distillation bottoms | **Spent wash (stillage)** | Anaerobic digestion → biogas; or fertilizer |
| Side draws | **Fusel oils** (amyl alcohols) | Solvents, plasticizers |
| Side draws | **Aldehydes** (acetaldehyde) | Chemical intermediate |

**3. Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid flow diagram.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- Molasses feed = 2,500 kg/day
- Sugar content = 48 % w/w
- Fermentation efficiency = 90 %
- Distillation recovery = 95 %
- Ethanol density (ρ) = 0.789 kg/L
- Theoretical yield = 0.511 g ethanol / g glucose

**Step 1 — Calculate sugar mass available:**

$$M_{sugar} = 2500 \times 0.48 = 1200\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical ethanol yield:**

$$M_{EtOH,\,th} = 1200 \times 0.511 = 613.2\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Practical yield after fermentation (90 %):**

$$M_{EtOH,\,ferm} = 613.2 \times 0.90 = 551.88\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — After distillation (95 % recovery):**

$$M_{EtOH,\,final} = 551.88 \times 0.95 = 524.29\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Convert mass to volume:**

$$V_{EtOH} = \dfrac{524.29}{0.789} = 664.5\ \text{L/day}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Theoretical ethanol** = 613.2 kg/day
- **Practical ethanol (recovered)** = 524.3 kg/day
- **Anhydrous ethanol volume** = **664.5 L/day**

*(1 mark each for steps 1–4; 3 marks for final mass + volume + unit handling)*

---

### **Question B: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Module 3]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **lignocellulosic route** for ethanol production, with emphasis on **pretreatment**, **enzymatic hydrolysis**, and **fermentation**. List the **technical challenges** of this route. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **molecular sieve dehydration** with **azeotropic distillation** for producing anhydrous ethanol. A fermentation plant produces **800 kg/day of 8 % v/v ethanol broth**. If the density of the broth is **0.99 kg/L**, compute the **daily volume of 99.5 % anhydrous ethanol** produced (assume fermentation efficiency 90 %, distillation 96 %, density of pure ethanol 0.789 kg/L). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Lignocellulosic Ethanol Production (7 marks):**

Lignocellulosic biomass (bagasse, rice straw, wood) is composed of three polymeric fractions:
- **Cellulose** (35–50 %) — β-1,4-linked glucose polymer
- **Hemicellulose** (20–30 %) — xylan, mannan, arabinan
- **Lignin** (15–25 %) — aromatic polymer, recalcitrant

**Step 1 — Pretreatment (2 marks):** Required to disrupt the **lignin–carbohydrate complex (LCC)** and improve enzyme accessibility. Common methods:
- **Dilute acid hydrolysis** (H₂SO₄, 0.5–1 %, 120–200 °C) — solubilizes hemicellulose
- **Steam explosion** (160–240 °C, 1–5 min, sudden decompression) — disrupts fibre structure
- **Alkaline pretreatment** (NaOH, lime) — removes lignin
- **Mechanical comminution** (milling) — increases surface area

**Step 2 — Enzymatic Hydrolysis (2 marks):** Pretreated biomass is treated with a **cellulase enzyme cocktail** (Endoglucanase + Exoglucanase + β-Glucosidase) at **45–55 °C, pH 4.8, 48–72 h**. Yields **glucose (from cellulose)** and **xylose + arabinose (from hemicellulose)**.

**Step 3 — Fermentation (2 marks):** Both **glucose and pentose sugars** must be fermented. Conventional *S. cerevisiae* ferments only glucose; therefore:
- **Co-fermentation** with genetically modified yeast (*S. cerevisiae* 424A) or
- **Recombinant Zymomonas mobilis** that ferments both hexoses and pentoses is used.
- **SSF (Simultaneous Saccharification & Fermentation):** combines hydrolysis + fermentation in one vessel — reduces inhibition and capital cost.

**Step 4 — Technical Challenges (1 mark):**
- **High enzyme cost** (cellulases are expensive)
- **Lignin recalcitrance** — inhibits enzymes
- **Pentose sugar fermentation** — requires genetic engineering
- **Inhibitor formation** (furfural, HMF, acetic acid from pretreatment)
- **High capital and operating cost**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Azeotropic Distillation | Molecular Sieve Dehydration |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Principle** | Third component (benzene) alters relative volatility | Selective **3 Å zeolite** adsorption of water |
| **Energy Use** | High (reboiler + condenser heat) | Low (~ 1.5 MJ/L) |
| **Purity Achievable** | 99.5–99.9 % | 99.5–99.95 % |
| **Operating Cost** | High (benzene inventory & losses) | Low (regeneration by vacuum) |
| **Environmental Concern** | Benzene is carcinogenic | Environmentally benign |
| **KTU Preference** | Older plants | **Modern / preferred** |

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- Broth mass = 800 kg/day
- Broth ethanol concentration = 8 % v/v
- Broth density = 0.99 kg/L
- Fermentation efficiency = 90 % (already factored into 8 % — assume 8 % is the actual achieved value)

**Step 1 — Volume of broth:**

$$V_{broth} = \dfrac{800}{0.99} = 808.08\ \text{L/day}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of pure ethanol in broth (8 % v/v):**

$$V_{EtOH,\,broth} = 808.08 \times 0.08 = 64.65\ \text{L/day}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of ethanol in broth (using ρ = 0.789 kg/L):**

$$M_{EtOH,\,broth} = 64.65 \times 0.789 = 51.01\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Apply distillation recovery (96 %):**

$$M_{EtOH,\,final} = 51.01 \times 0.96 = 48.97\ \text{kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volume of 99.5 % anhydrous ethanol (using 0.789 kg/L for pure EtOH, since 99.5 % v/v ≈ pure):**

$$V_{final} = \dfrac{48.97}{0.789} = 62.06\ \text{L/day}$$

**Final Answer:** **≈ 62.06 L/day of 99.5 % anhydrous ethanol**

*(1 mark: broth volume; 1 mark: ethanol mass balance; 1 mark: distillation correction; 1 mark: final unit conversion)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Production of Ethanol**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) is produced from **biomass/sugar fermentation** + **distillation-dehydration**.
- ⭐ **Master Reaction (Gay-Lussac):** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$
- ⭐ **Theoretical yield** = **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose**; **Practical yield** = **~ 90 % of theoretical** (≈ 0.46 g/g).
- ⭐ **Molecular weights to memorize:** Glucose = **180 g/mol**; Ethanol = **46 g/mol**; CO₂ = **44 g/mol**.
- ⭐ **Fermentation organism:** **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** at **30–35 °C, pH 4–5, 48–72 h**.
- ⭐ **Azeotrope at 95.6 % v/v** — the **distillation ceiling**; overcome by **molecular sieve (3 Å zeolite)** or **azeotropic distillation (benzene)**.
- ⭐ **Ethanol density = 0.789 kg/L** (essential for mass ↔ volume conversions in problems).
- ⭐ **Feedstocks:** Molasses (no hydrolysis), Starch (gelatinization + amylase), Lignocellulose (pretreatment + cellulase).
- ⭐ **Byproducts:** **CO₂** (0.49 g/g sugar), **spent wash** (biogas), **fusel oils** (solvents), **yeast** (SCP).
- ⭐ **Process order:** **Dilution → Nutrient addition → Fermentation → Distillation → Dehydration → Storage**.
- ⭐ **Energy benchmarking:** Modern bioethanol plants use **1.5–2.5 MJ/L** (molecular sieve preferred for low energy).
- ⭐ **KTU exam hot-points:** Stoichiometric yield calculations, feedstock comparison, dehydration method comparison, role of *S. cerevisiae*, byproducts identification.
- ⭐ **Exam trap:** Don't forget to apply **fermentation efficiency AND distillation recovery** in multi-step numericals.
- ⭐ **Sustainability angle:** Bioethanol is **CO₂-neutral** when produced from biomass → supports **circular bioeconomy** and **waste-to-wealth** themes central to PGAET753.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wastes of tapioca starch industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wastes of tapioca starch industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tapioca Starch Industry Wastes** are the residual solid, liquid, and semi-solid by-products generated during the wet milling and extraction of starch from the tuberous roots of *Manihot esculenta* (cassava/tapioca). The principal waste streams are **(a) Tapioca Pulp/Press Cake (solid fibrous residue)**, **(b) Cassava Peel Waste**, and **(c) Starch Processing Effluent (liquid wastewater)**, each of which possesses a distinct physico-chemical character and demands a tailored valorization strategy.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, these wastes are categorized under **Agro-Industrial Solid Wastes (AISW)** and **High-Strength Industrial Effluents**, since tapioca starch is one of the most prominent agro-processing industries in Kerala and Tamil Nadu, contributing significantly to the regional biomass residue pool.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeezed Orange" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Starch-Extraction Kitchen Sink:**
> Imagine you are making fresh orange juice. You squeeze the orange and obtain (i) juice (the product), (ii) pulp and seeds (solid waste), and (iii) a sugary, cloudy washing-water (liquid waste). Tapioca starch extraction works exactly the same way: the **starch-rich milk** is the product, the **fibrous pulp** is the solid waste, and the **protein- and sugar-laden wash water** is the liquid effluent. The peels themselves (the "orange skin") form a third waste stream. Just as you would compost the pulp or feed peels to cattle, tapioca industries can convert every waste stream into value.

### 1.3 Key Process Context

The standard **wet-milling process** in a tapioca starch factory follows the sequence:

1. **Receiving & Weighing** of cassava roots.
2. **Washing** to remove soil and grit.
3. **Peeling** (manual or mechanical) → generates **Peel Waste** (~10–15 % of root weight).
4. **Rasping/Grating** to rupture the starch-bearing cells.
5. **Screening/Extraction** with water through rotary/vibratory screens → separates **Starch Milk** from **Pulp**.
6. **Dewatering & Drying of Pulp** → produces **Tapioca Pulp / Press Cake** (a major solid waste).
7. **Purification, Centrifugation & Drying of Starch** → final product.
8. **Process Water Discharge** → **Starch Processing Effluent** (the largest volume waste).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics (per 1 tonne of fresh cassava roots processed):**
> - Starch yield: **≈ 250–300 kg**
> - Pulp (wet basis): **≈ 400–500 kg**
> - Peels: **≈ 100–150 kg**
> - Liquid effluent: **≈ 5,000–8,000 litres**
> - **BOD of effluent:** **4,000 – 10,000 mg/L**
> - **COD of effluent:** **8,000 – 25,000 mg/L**

These numbers are the **key benchmark values** frequently asked in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Tapioca Wastes

The wastes are broadly classified into three streams, each with distinct characteristics and valorization potential:

#### A. Solid Waste — Tapioca Pulp / Press Cake

- **Physical form:** Moist, fibrous, brownish solid cake.
- **Generation rate:** **400–500 kg per tonne of fresh roots** (≈ 40–50 % w/w).
- **Composition (dry basis):**
  - Cellulose: **≈ 35–45 %**
  - Hemicellulose: **≈ 10–15 %**
  - Lignin: **≈ 5–10 %**
  - Residual starch: **≈ 40–55 %** (key — this is un-extracted value)
  - Crude protein: **≈ 2–4 %**
  - Moisture: **≈ 70–80 %** (as discharged)
- **Why it matters:** The residual starch content makes it a potent **fermentation substrate** and the fibrous fraction makes it a useful **ruminant feed** and **composting feedstock**.

#### B. Solid Waste — Cassava Peel

- **Generation rate:** **100–150 kg per tonne of fresh roots**.
- **Composition (dry basis):**
  - Crude fiber: **≈ 20–25 %**
  - Starch: **≈ 30–40 %**
  - Crude protein: **≈ 4–6 %**
  - Lipids: **≈ 1–2 %**
  - Ash: **≈ 3–5 %**
  - **Linamarin (cyanogenic glucoside):** **≈ 50–500 mg/kg** — a critical anti-nutritional and toxic factor.
- **Significance:** Peels are highly biodegradable; their high starch + linamarin content makes them excellent **anaerobic digestion** feedstock, but the **HCN potential** must be controlled before any feed application.

#### C. Liquid Waste — Starch Processing Effluent

- **Volume:** **5–8 m³ per tonne of roots**.
- **Characteristics:**
  - pH: **3.5 – 5.5** (acidic due to organic acid fermentation).
  - Total Suspended Solids (TSS): **2,000 – 6,000 mg/L**.
  - BOD₅: **4,000 – 10,000 mg/L**.
  - COD: **8,000 – 25,000 mg/L**.
  - Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN): **200 – 600 mg/L**.
  - Total Phosphorus: **50 – 150 mg/L**.
  - Dissolved starch & sugars: **2,000 – 6,000 mg/L**.
  - **Cyanide (HCN):** **20 – 100 mg/L**.
- **Why it matters:** The **BOD/COD ratio of ≈ 0.4 – 0.5** indicates the effluent is **highly biodegradable** and therefore an excellent candidate for **anaerobic digestion** with substantial **biogas recovery**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Application / Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starch extraction efficiency (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{M_{starch,\,extracted}}{M_{starch,\,in\,root}} \times 100$ | % | Typical plant efficiency = **80–88 %** |
| Solid waste generation factor (SWF) | $SWF = \dfrac{M_{solid\,waste}}{M_{root\,processed}}$ | kg/kg (or tonne/tonne) | Pulp SWF ≈ **0.40 – 0.50** |
| Peel-to-root ratio (PR) | $PR = \dfrac{M_{peels}}{M_{roots}}$ | kg/kg | PR ≈ **0.10 – 0.15** |
| BOD/COD ratio | $R = \dfrac{BOD_5}{COD}$ | dimensionless | Biodegradability index; R > 0.4 → **highly biodegradable** |
| Specific biogas yield | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{V_{biogas}}{M_{VS\,added}}$ | m³/kg VS | For tapioca pulp: **0.30 – 0.45 m³/kg VS** |
| Theoretical methane yield (Buswell equation) | $C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CO_2$ | mol | Used for theoretical CH₄ potential of starch/carbohydrate substrates |
| Cyanide detoxification (removal efficiency) | $E_{CN} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Achieved by **sun-drying, ensiling, or soaking** |
| Mass balance on starch | $M_{root} \times S_{root} = M_{starch} + M_{pulp} \times S_{pulp} + M_{peel} \times S_{peel} + M_{effluent} \times S_{eff}$ | kg | Used in process engineering audit |
| Effluent load per tonne root | $L_{BOD} = Q \times C_{BOD}$ | kg BOD / tonne | Q in m³/tonne, C in mg/L; typical = **20–80 kg BOD/tonne** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Point:** The **BOD/COD ratio** is the single most asked parameter because it directly dictates the choice of treatment — **anaerobic** for R > 0.4, **aerobic** for R < 0.3, and **anaerobic + aerobic (two-stage)** for high-strength food-industry effluents like this.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Anaerobic Digesters (UASB / CSTR):** The most deployed solution in Kerala sago factories. Effluent + pulp co-digestion produces **biogas (60–70 % CH₄)** and **treated water** for land irrigation.
- **Composting:** Pulp + peel mixture, optionally with cow dung and biochar, yields a nutrient-rich organic compost in **45–60 days**.
- **Single Cell Protein (SCP):** Pulp and effluent are fermented with *Aspergillus niger*, *Candida utilis*, or *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* to produce protein-rich biomass for animal feed.
- **Mushroom Cultivation:** Pleurotus spp. (oyster mushroom) can be grown directly on the pulp substrate after simple pasteurization.
- **Bioethanol:** Residual starch in pulp/peel is hydrolyzed (acid/enzyme) and fermented to ethanol — a second-generation biofuel route.
- **Pectin / Biopolymer Extraction:** Peel is a low-grade source of pectin and can be used for biopolymer production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Starch Mass Balance

> A tapioca starch unit processes **10 tonnes/day** of fresh cassava roots. The starch content in roots is **28 % (w/w, fresh basis)**. The process extracts starch with an **extraction efficiency of 85 %**. The moisture content of the discharged pulp is **75 % (wet basis)**. The residual starch in the pulp is **42 % (dry basis)**. Calculate: (a) the starch output per day, (b) the dry pulp produced per day, and (c) the percentage of total input starch that ends up un-extracted (in pulp + effluent + peel losses).

**Given:**
- $M_{root} = 10{,}000$ kg/day
- $S_{root} = 0.28$ (kg starch / kg root)
- $\eta = 0.85$
- Pulp moisture $W_{pulp} = 0.75$ (wet basis)
- Residual starch in pulp = $0.42$ (dry basis)

**Step 1 — Total starch in the feed:**

$$M_{starch,\,in} = M_{root} \times S_{root} = 10{,}000 \times 0.28 = 2{,}800 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Starch extracted (product):**

$$M_{starch,\,out} = M_{starch,\,in} \times \eta = 2{,}800 \times 0.85 = 2{,}380 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Un-extracted starch (lost to pulp + effluent + peel):**

$$M_{starch,\,lost} = 2{,}800 - 2{,}380 = 420 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Assume all un-extracted starch reports to the pulp (simplified).** Then starch in pulp = **420 kg/day**.

If starch in pulp = **42 % of dry pulp mass**, then:

$$M_{pulp,\,dry} = \dfrac{420}{0.42} = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Convert dry pulp to wet pulp using 75 % moisture:**

$$M_{pulp,\,wet} = \dfrac{M_{pulp,\,dry}}{1 - W_{pulp}} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{1 - 0.75} = \dfrac{1{,}000}{0.25} = 4{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage of input starch un-extracted:**

$$\% \, \text{loss} = \dfrac{420}{2{,}800} \times 100 = 15.00 \,\%$$

**Result Summary Table:**

| **Quantity** | **Value (per day)** |
|---|---|
| Fresh root input | 10,000 kg |
| Total starch in feed | 2,800 kg |
| Starch extracted (product) | 2,380 kg |
| Starch lost in pulp | 420 kg |
| Dry pulp generated | 1,000 kg |
| Wet pulp generated | 4,000 kg |
| Un-extracted fraction | 15.00 % |

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Biogas Potential from Pulp

> The same unit produces **4,000 kg/day** of wet pulp at **75 % moisture**. The Volatile Solids (VS) content of the dry pulp is **92 %**. The specific methane yield is **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS added**. Methane fraction in biogas = **65 %**. Calculate (a) VS added per day, (b) methane volume per day, and (c) total biogas volume per day.

**Step 1 — Dry pulp per day** (computed earlier): $M_{pulp,\,dry} = 1{,}000$ kg/day.

**Step 2 — VS per day:**

$$M_{VS} = M_{pulp,\,dry} \times VS_{fraction} = 1{,}000 \times 0.92 = 920 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

**Step 3 — Methane volume per day:**

$$V_{CH_4} = M_{VS} \times Y_{CH_4} = 920 \times 0.35 = 322 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Total biogas volume (CH₄ + CO₂, etc.):**

$$V_{biogas} = \dfrac{V_{CH_4}}{f_{CH_4}} = \dfrac{322}{0.65} = 495.4 \text{ m}^3 \text{ biogas / day}$$

**Step 5 — Energy equivalent** (1 m³ CH₄ ≈ 35.8 MJ):

$$E_{energy} = 322 \times 35.8 = 11{,}527.6 \text{ MJ/day} \approx 3{,}202 \text{ kWh/day}$$

This energy can offset a significant fraction of the **drying-furnace fuel demand** in a typical sago factory.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Effluent BOD Load

> Effluent flow = **6 m³ per tonne of root**. BOD₅ concentration = **7,500 mg/L**. Root processed = **10 tonnes/day**. Calculate (a) effluent volume per day, (b) total BOD load per day, and (c) equivalent population load (per capita BOD ≈ 60 g/day).

**Step 1 — Daily effluent volume:**

$$Q = 6 \text{ m}^3/\text{tonne} \times 10 \text{ tonnes} = 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Total BOD load:**

$$L_{BOD} = Q \times C_{BOD} = 60 \text{ m}^3 \times 7{,}500 \text{ g/m}^3 = 450{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 450 \text{ kg BOD/day}$$

**Step 3 — Population equivalent:**

$$PE = \dfrac{L_{BOD}}{\text{per capita BOD}} = \dfrac{450{,}000 \text{ g/day}}{60 \text{ g/person/day}} = 7{,}500 \text{ persons equivalent}$$

This is the **key exam takeaway** — a small sago unit can have a population-equivalent pollution load of several thousand people, which is why **effluent treatment is a regulatory and engineering priority**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tapioca Starch Process — Block-Level Flow with Waste Streams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["A: Fresh Cassava Roots"] --> B["B: Washing Station"]
    B --> C["C: Peeling Unit"]
    C --> D["D: Rasping Grater"]
    D --> E["E: Rotary Extraction Screen"]
    C -->|Peel Stream| P1["P1: Peels Solid Waste"]
    E -->|Starch Milk| F["F: Centrifugal Separator"]
    E -->|Fibrous Pulp| P2["P2: Tapioca Pulp Press Cake"]
    F --> G["G: Starch Drier"]
    G --> H["H: Starch Product"]
    F -->|Process Water| P3["P3: Liquid Effluent Stream"]
    P1 --> U1["U1: Biogas / Animal Feed / Compost"]
    P2 --> U2["U2: Biogas / Animal Feed / SCP / Mushroom"]
    P3 --> U3["U3: Anaerobic Digestion / Biogas / Treated Water"]
```

### 4.2 Waste Utilization Decision Matrix (Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["R: Three Waste Streams from Sago Industry"] --> S1["S1: Solid Pulp"]
    R --> S2["S2: Solid Peels"]
    R --> S3["S3: Liquid Effluent"]
    S1 --> V1["V1: Composting"]
    S1 --> V2["V2: Anaerobic Digestion"]
    S1 --> V3["V3: Single Cell Protein"]
    S1 --> V4["V4: Mushroom Substrate"]
    S2 --> W1["W1: Sun Dried Animal Feed"]
    S2 --> W2["W2: Biogas via AD"]
    S2 --> W3["W3: Pectin Extraction"]
    S3 --> X1["X1: UASB Reactor Biogas"]
    S3 --> X2["X2: Constructed Wetland"]
    S3 --> X3["X3: Land Irrigation After AD"]
    V2 --> Y["Y: Combined Biogas to Boiler Dryer"]
    W2 --> Y
    X1 --> Y
    Y --> Z["Z: Energy Self Sufficient Sago Factory"]
```

### 4.3 Treatment Train — High-Strength Effluent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["IN: Raw Starch Effluent"] --> EQ["EQ: Equalization Tank"]
    EQ --> AS["AS: Acidogenic Reactor"]
    AS --> UASB["UASB: Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket"]
    UASB -->|Biogas| BG["BG: Gas Holder"]
    UASB --> AF["AF: Aerobic Polishing Activated Sludge"]
    AF --> CW["CW: Constructed Wetland Polishing"]
    CW --> OUT["OUT: Compliant Discharge / Reuse"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*List the major solid and liquid waste streams generated by a tapioca starch industry. Mention one valorization route for each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Solid – Tapioca Pulp / Press Cake (40–50 % w/w):** High in residual starch and fiber; valorized via **anaerobic digestion** to produce biogas.
- **Solid – Cassava Peel (10–15 % w/w):** Contains linamarin and starch; valorized via **sun-drying followed by use as ruminant feed** (after HCN reduction).
- **Liquid – Starch Processing Effluent (5–8 m³/tonne):** High BOD/COD (BOD = 4,000–10,000 mg/L, COD = 8,000–25,000 mg/L), acidic (pH 3.5–5.5); valorized via **UASB-based anaerobic digestion** for biogas generation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*State the typical BOD and COD values of tapioca starch industry effluent. Compute the BOD/COD ratio and comment on its biodegradability.*

**Model Answer:**
Typical BOD₅ = **4,000–10,000 mg/L**; COD = **8,000–25,000 mg/L**. Taking mid-values: BOD = 7,000 mg/L; COD = 16,500 mg/L.

$$R = \dfrac{BOD}{COD} = \dfrac{7{,}000}{16{,}500} \approx 0.42$$

Since R > **0.40**, the effluent is **highly biodegradable** and is therefore ideally suited for **anaerobic biological treatment** (UASB, CSTR, or anaerobic filter) rather than costly aerobic treatment.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, describe the major waste streams generated in a tapioca starch industry. Discuss the characteristics of each waste. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Process Flow (in words — diagram to be drawn by student):** Roots → Washing → Peeling → Rasping → Screening → Starch milk → Centrifugation → Drying → Starch product. Three waste streams are discharged: **(i) Peels, (ii) Pulp, (iii) Effluent**.

2. **Tapioca Pulp (Press Cake):**
   - Quantity: **400–500 kg per tonne root** (40–50 % w/w, wet).
   - Moisture: **70–80 %**.
   - Composition: residual starch 40–55 % (dry), cellulose 35–45 %, hemicellulose 10–15 %, lignin 5–10 %, crude protein 2–4 %.
   - Character: fibrous, moist, biodegradable, mildly acidic.

3. **Cassava Peel:**
   - Quantity: **100–150 kg per tonne root**.
   - Composition: starch 30–40 %, crude fiber 20–25 %, protein 4–6 %, **linamarin 50–500 mg/kg**.
   - Character: cyanogenic; needs detoxification before feed use.

4. **Starch Effluent:**
   - Quantity: **5,000–8,000 L per tonne root**.
   - pH: **3.5–5.5** (acidic).
   - BOD: **4,000–10,000 mg/L**; COD: **8,000–25,000 mg/L**.
   - TSS: **2,000–6,000 mg/L**; dissolved starch & sugars: 2,000–6,000 mg/L; cyanide: 20–100 mg/L.
   - Character: high-strength, biodegradable (BOD/COD ≈ 0.4–0.5).

**(b)** A sago factory processes **20 tonnes/day** of cassava roots containing **30 % starch**. The extraction efficiency is **88 %**. The pulp generated contains **45 % residual starch on dry basis** and has **78 % moisture**. The volatile solids in dry pulp are **90 %**. The specific methane yield is **0.38 m³ CH₄/kg VS** and methane forms **65 %** of the biogas. Calculate: (i) starch produced per day, (ii) wet pulp produced per day, and (iii) total biogas volume produced per day. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Starch in feed:**

$$M_{starch,\,in} = 20{,}000 \times 0.30 = 6{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Starch extracted:**

$$M_{starch,\,out} = 6{,}000 \times 0.88 = 5{,}280 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Un-extracted starch (lost):**

$$M_{starch,\,lost} = 6{,}000 - 5{,}280 = 720 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Dry pulp** (assume all un-extracted starch reports to pulp at 45 % of dry mass):

$$M_{pulp,\,dry} = \dfrac{720}{0.45} = 1{,}600 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Wet pulp:**

$$M_{pulp,\,wet} = \dfrac{1{,}600}{1 - 0.78} = \dfrac{1{,}600}{0.22} = 7{,}272.7 \text{ kg/day} \approx 7{,}273 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 6 — Volatile solids added per day:**

$$M_{VS} = 1{,}600 \times 0.90 = 1{,}440 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

**Step 7 — Methane produced:**

$$V_{CH_4} = 1{,}440 \times 0.38 = 547.2 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{day}$$

**Step 8 — Total biogas volume:**

$$V_{biogas} = \dfrac{547.2}{0.65} = 841.8 \text{ m}^3 / \text{day}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Starch produced = **5,280 kg/day**
- Wet pulp produced = **7,273 kg/day**
- Total biogas = **841.8 m³/day**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]

**(a)** Discuss the various biological and thermal valorization options for the solid wastes (pulp and peel) of tapioca starch industries. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Biological Options:**

1. **Anaerobic Digestion (AD):** Most effective route. Co-digestion of pulp + effluent in **UASB/CSTR** reactors at mesophilic conditions (35 °C) with HRT of 15–25 days. Produces **0.30–0.45 m³ CH₄/kg VS**, yielding 300–500 m³ biogas per tonne of fresh root processed. Biogas offsets furnace fuel for starch drying.

2. **Composting:** Pulp + peel blended with cow dung / biochar in **windrow or aerated static pile**. C/N adjusted to 25–30:1. Mature compost in **45–60 days**. Output: 250–350 kg organic compost per tonne root; usable as soil conditioner.

3. **Single Cell Protein (SCP):** Solid-state fermentation of pulp with *Aspergillus niger* or submerged fermentation of hydrolysate with *Candida utilis* gives protein-enriched (30–45 % crude protein) feed supplement.

4. **Mushroom Cultivation:** *Pleurotus ostreatus* and *P. sajor-caju* grow directly on pasteurized pulp substrate; biological efficiency of **60–90 %**; spawn run 18–22 days.

**Thermal Options:**

5. **Direct Combustion / Drying Fuel:** Dried pulp calorific value ≈ **15–17 MJ/kg** (due to residual starch). Can be co-fired in boiler furnaces.

6. **Pyrolysis / Torrefaction:** Conversion to biochar at 300–500 °C (torrefaction) or 500–700 °C (slow pyrolysis); yield 30–40 % biochar with calorific value 25–30 MJ/kg — a solid biofuel.

7. **Bioethanol:** Acid (dilute H₂SO₄ at 121 °C) or enzymatic hydrolysis of starch in pulp, followed by fermentation with *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* gives **60–80 L ethanol per tonne of fresh root** from waste streams alone.

8. **Pellet / Briquette Manufacture:** Mechanically densified pellets from dried pulp/peel for use as a renewable industrial fuel.

**(b)** The effluent from a sago factory has a flow of **8 m³ per tonne of root**. The BOD₅ and COD of the effluent are **8,500 mg/L** and **20,000 mg/L** respectively. The factory processes **15 tonnes/day** of roots. Compute: (i) daily effluent volume, (ii) total BOD and COD load per day in kg, (iii) BOD/COD ratio and biodegradability comment, and (iv) population equivalent at 60 g BOD/capita/day. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily effluent volume:**

$$Q = 8 \text{ m}^3/\text{tonne} \times 15 \text{ tonnes} = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — BOD load:**

$$L_{BOD} = 120 \text{ m}^3 \times 8{,}500 \text{ g/m}^3 = 1{,}020{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 1{,}020 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — COD load:**

$$L_{COD} = 120 \text{ m}^3 \times 20{,}000 \text{ g/m}^3 = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 2{,}400 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — BOD/COD ratio:**

$$R = \dfrac{8{,}500}{20{,}000} = 0.425$$

**Comment:** R = 0.425 > 0.40, so the effluent is **highly biodegradable** and is well-suited for **anaerobic treatment** (UASB/CSTR) with biogas recovery.

**Step 5 — Population equivalent:**

$$PE = \dfrac{1{,}020{,}000 \text{ g/day}}{60 \text{ g/person/day}} = 17{,}000 \text{ persons equivalent}$$

**Interpretation:** A 15 TPD sago unit has the pollution load of a town of **17,000 inhabitants** — a striking indicator of why treatment is mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply moisture correction in pulp mass balance** — when pulp is given as "wet basis at 75 % moisture", students often write the dry mass as the wet mass and lose 4–5 marks. **Always convert:** $M_{wet} = M_{dry} / (1 - W)$.
> 2. **Confusing BOD and COD units** — examiners want **kg/day**, so multiply $Q$ (m³/day) by **C (g/m³)** and divide by 1,000 for kg, OR by 1,000,000 to directly use mg/L.
> 3. **Neglecting the cyanogenic (linamarin/HCN) issue** in cassava peel valorization — KTU explicitly tests the detoxification step (sun-drying, ensiling, or boiling). Marks deducted if absent.
> 4. **Reporting BOD/COD ratio without the biodegradability comment** — examiners expect the **interpretive sentence** ("highly biodegradable → anaerobic treatment recommended").
> 5. **Forgetting to specify mesophilic vs. thermophilic conditions** when discussing AD — KTU 2024 rubrics explicitly want the temperature band (35 °C mesophilic / 55 °C thermophilic) and HRT (15–25 days) for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Three waste streams:** Pulp (solid), Peel (solid), Effluent (liquid).
- **Solid waste generation:** Pulp ≈ **40–50 % w/w**; Peels ≈ **10–15 % w/w** of fresh root.
- **Liquid effluent generation:** **5–8 m³/tonne root**.
- **BOD₅ of effluent:** **4,000–10,000 mg/L**.
- **COD of effluent:** **8,000–25,000 mg/L**.
- **BOD/COD ratio:** **≈ 0.4 – 0.5** → highly biodegradable → **anaerobic treatment preferred**.
- **pH of effluent:** **3.5 – 5.5** (acidic, needs neutralization before aerobic step).
- **Pulp composition (dry basis):** Starch 40–55 %, Cellulose 35–45 %, Hemicellulose 10–15 %, Lignin 5–10 %.
- **Peel hazard:** **Linamarin** (cyanogenic glucoside) — must be detoxified (sun-dry 2–3 days, ensile, or boil 30 min).
- **Pulp valorization routes:** **AD → biogas, Composting, SCP, Mushroom (Pleurotus spp.), Bioethanol, Pellets.**
- **Effluent valorization routes:** **UASB/CSTR anaerobic digestion, Constructed wetland polishing, Treated water for irrigation.**
- **Biogas yield:** **0.30 – 0.45 m³ CH₄ per kg VS** from tapioca pulp; CH₄ fraction **60–70 %**.
- **Anaerobic digestion conditions:** Mesophilic **35 °C**, HRT **15–25 days**, pH **6.8 – 7.4**.
- **Energy potential:** Waste-derived biogas from a 10 TPD unit can yield **~ 3,000 kWh/day**, offsetting drying fuel.
- **Population equivalent of a typical small sago unit:** **7,000 – 20,000 persons** (key alarming statistic).
- **Buswell equation:** Foundation for theoretical methane yield calculation of carbohydrate substrates.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme focus:** Process flow + numerical mass balance + BOD/COD interpretation + detoxification of cyanogens.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tapioca thippi-utilization as fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tapioca thippi-utilization as fuel # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tapioca Thippi (Cassava Residue / Cassava Bagasse):** *Tapioca thippi* is the fibrous, solid residual biomass generated as a primary byproduct during the wet milling and starch extraction process of cassava (*Manihot esculenta* Crantz) tubers. It constitutes the pulp, peel, and cellular debris left after the separation of starch granules from the slurry. In the context of waste-to-energy engineering, **tapioca thippi** is classified as an **agricultural lignocellulosic biomass residue** possessing significant fuel value due to its residual carbohydrates, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin content. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PGAET753 - Waste and Byproducts Utilization*, this topic falls under the broader category of **"Conversion of agro-residues to bioenergy"**, where the emphasis is on characterizing waste streams from Kerala's staple crops and engineering their thermochemical and biochemical conversion into useful fuel vectors. ## 1.2 Generation Pathway (Process Flow Context) Tapioca thippi is generated in the following industrial chain: 1. **Harvesting** → Cassava tubers (Kerala: *Moozhikothala*, *Sree Vijaya*, *H-226* cultivars). 2. **Peeling & Washing** → Removal of outer brown skin. 3. **Crushing/Grating** → Pulping into a slurry. 4. **Starch Extraction** → Washing through sieves; starch settles as milk. 5. **Thippi Discharge** → The fibrous residue (wet, ~70–80% moisture) is squeezed out and discharged. > [!NOTE] > **Typical Generation Ratio:** Approximately **1 kg of dry thippi is produced per 2.5 – 4 kg of fresh cassava tubers processed**, depending on extraction efficiency. A medium-scale Kerala starch factory processing **20 tonnes/day** of cassava generates roughly **5–8 tonnes/day of wet thippi**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like this:** Imagine you grate a potato, soak it in water, and drain out all the starch to make a thick gravy base. What is left behind in the sieve — that wet, fibrous, sponge-like pulp — is essentially **tapioca thippi**. Now, instead of throwing it away (which is what many small-scale Kerala industries still do, leading to foul-smelling dumpsites), we treat this pulp like a dried tea bag or sawdust: dry it, compress it, and burn it. Because plants originally trapped solar energy into their cell walls (cellulose/lignin), burning this "spent pulp" simply releases that stored solar energy as heat. So thippi is essentially **"spent, low-grade solar battery"** — and our job as engineers is to recharge it into useful fuel form (briquettes, biogas, or charcoal). ## 1.4 Significance in the Kerala Context | Parameter | Relevance | |---|---| | **Kerala Production** | ~2.5–3.0 million tonnes of cassava/year | | **Starch Industries** | ~200+ small/medium units (Palakkad, Kollam, Malappuram) | | **Disposal Problem** | Wet thippi rapidly ferments → H₂S, organic acids → water pollution | | **Energy Opportunity** | Combustion energy ≈ **14–17 MJ/kg (dry basis)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Metric (Bold):** The **calorific value of sun-dried tapioca thippi is approximately 14–17 MJ/kg** on a dry basis, comparable to rice husk (13–15 MJ/kg) and sawdust (15–17 MJ/kg). --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physicochemical Characterization of Tapioca Thippi The fuel potential of any lignocellulosic residue is governed by three analytical pillars — **proximate analysis, ultimate analysis, and calorific value determination**. ### 2.1.1 Proximate Analysis (Engineering Composition) The proximate analysis reports the mass fractions of four constituents that govern combustion behavior: - **Moisture content (M):** Water mass lost at 105 °C. - **Volatile Matter (VM):** Combustible gases released at 950 °C (in absence of air). - **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Residual solid carbon after volatile expulsion. - **Ash Content (A):** Non-combustible mineral residue after complete combustion at 815 °C. ### 2.1.2 Ultimate Analysis (Elemental Composition) Reports the mass fractions of **Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), Sulfur (S)**, plus trace chlorine. This determines stoichiometric air requirement and emission profile. ### 2.1.3 High Heating Value (HHV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV) The HHV can be predicted from the ultimate analysis using the **Dulong-Berthelot equation** (most frequently tested in KTU): $$ \text{HHV} \; (\text{MJ/kg}) \;=\; 0.3383\,C \;+\; 1.422\,\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 0.0954\,S $$ where C, H, O, S are mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and sulfur respectively. ## 2.2 Typical Fuel Properties of Tapioca Thippi | Property | Sun-Dried Thippi | Oven-Dried Thippi | Reference Comparator (Rice Husk) | |---|---|---|---| | Moisture (%) | 10–15 | 4–8 | 10–12 | | Volatile Matter (%) | 70–78 | 72–80 | 65–72 | | Fixed Carbon (%) | 12–18 | 14–20 | 12–16 | | Ash Content (%) | 3–6 | 3–6 | 15–20 | | Carbon (C) % | 38–42 | 40–44 | 35–38 | | Hydrogen (H) % | 5.5–6.2 | 5.8–6.3 | 5.0–5.5 | | Oxygen (O) % | 38–44 | 38–42 | 35–40 | | Nitrogen (N) % | 0.8–1.5 | 0.8–1.5 | 0.5–1.0 | | Sulfur (S) % | < 0.2 | < 0.2 | < 0.2 | | **HHV (MJ/kg)** | **14–17** | **16–18** | **13–15** | | Bulk Density (kg/m³) | 150–250 | 150–250 | 100–150 | > [!NOTE] > **Why is thippi a *good* fuel despite being a waste?** Because its **ash content (3–6%) is significantly lower than rice husk (15–20%)**, meaning less slagging and fouling in boilers. Combined with its **high volatile matter (>70%)**, it ignites readily — an excellent characteristic for gasification and pyrolysis applications. ## 2.3 Conversion Routes — Utilization as Fuel Tapioca thippi can be converted into four major fuel forms, each at a different complexity and energy density tier: ### Route 1 — Direct Combustion (Loose Form) **Why & How:** - **Why:** Simplest pathway; no preprocessing except sun-drying to <15% moisture. - **How:** Burned in a fixed-grate or fluidized-bed boiler. The high volatile matter ensures quick ignition, while moderate fixed carbon sustains the combustion front. - **Engineering reality:** Direct combustion of loose thippi is inefficient due to low bulk density (150–250 kg/m³) — feeder handling is difficult, and combustion is incomplete. Suitable only for cottage-scale boilers (e.g., sago factories using their own waste for process steam). ### Route 2 — Densification (Briquettes / Pellets) ⭐ KTU HIGH-YIELD **Why & How:** - **Why:** Densification increases bulk density from ~200 kg/m³ to **600–1200 kg/m³**, transforming a fluffy waste into a log-like fuel comparable to wood charcoal or lignite. - **How:** Dried thippi is ground (<6 mm), optionally blended with a binder (cassava starch slurry — a brilliant zero-cost synergy, since the same factory produces starch!), and compacted in: - **Screw extruder (ram press):** → Hexagonal or cylindrical briquettes ("white coal") - **Pellet mill (ring-die):** → 6–12 mm diameter pellets - **Process conditions:** Compaction pressure **15–25 MPa**, die temperature **80–120 °C** (lignin softens, acts as a natural binder). ### Route 3 — Thermochemical Conversion (Gasification / Pyrolysis) **Why & How:** - **Why:** Converts the solid biomass into a combustible gas (producer gas) or bio-char, which are more versatile fuels. - **How:** - **Gasification (800–900 °C, sub-stoichiometric air):** Yields producer gas (CO + H₂ + CH₄ + N₂) with calorific value **4–6 MJ/Nm³**. - **Pyrolysis (400–600 °C, anaerobic):** Yields bio-oil, bio-char, and pyrolysis gas in roughly 30:30:40 mass ratio. ### Route 4 — Biochemical Conversion (Anaerobic Digestion → Biogas) **Why & How:** - **Why:** Wet thippi (with 70–80% moisture) is unsuitable for direct combustion but ideal for anaerobic digestion because moisture is actually *required* for microbial activity. - **How:** Wet thippi is fed (sometimes co-digested with cattle dung) into a sealed digester. Methanogenic archaea convert organic matter into **biogas (60–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂)** with calorific value **20–25 MJ/m³**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Dulong-Berthelot (HHV prediction) | $\text{HHV} = 0.3383\,C + 1.422\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.0954\,S$ | MJ/kg, from ultimate analysis | | 2 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2.442 \!\left(\dfrac{9H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100}\right)$ | MJ/kg; subtracts latent heat of water vapor | | 3 | Stoichiometric air | $\text{Air}_{\text{st}} = \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{23.2}$ | kg air / kg fuel | | 4 | Excess air ratio | $e = \dfrac{\text{Actual air}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}}$ | Dimensionless (typical: 1.2–1.5 for biomass) | | 5 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $\text{AFR} = e \times \text{Air}_{\text{st}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | | 6 | Energy densification ratio | $\text{E}_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{briquette}}}{\rho_{\text{loose}}}$ | Dimensionless; typical 4–6× | | 7 | Biogas yield (empirical) | $Y_b \approx 0.4\,V_{\text{org}} - 0.05\,V_{\text{org}}^2$ | m³ CH₄ per kg VS added | | 8 | Combustion efficiency | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_g - T_a}{T_g}$ (simplified) | Fraction; T in absolute (K) | | 9 | Mass balance (drying) | $M_{\text{dry}} = M_{\text{wet}}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{w_1 - w_2}{1 - w_2}\right)$ | Mass shrinkage on drying | | 10 | Carbon conversion (gasifier) | $X_C = \dfrac{C_{\text{in fuel}} - C_{\text{in char}}}{C_{\text{in fuel}}}$ | Fraction (0.85–0.95 typical) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition for the Dulong Equation:** Valid for fuels with **oxygen content below 50%** (mass) and sulfur content **< 6%** — tapioca thippi comfortably satisfies both, making this the go-to equation in KTU problems. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Calorific Value of Tapioca Thippi Using Dulong-Berthelot > **Problem Statement:** A sample of oven-dried tapioca thippi has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis): **C = 42.5%, H = 6.0%, O = 41.2%, N = 0.8%, S = 0.1%, Ash = 5.0%**. Moisture in the as-fired condition is **10%**. Calculate: > (a) Higher Heating Value (HHV) on dry basis. > (b) Lower Heating Value (LHV) on as-fired basis. > (c) Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel (dry basis). > (d) Actual air required at 30% excess air. --- ### Step 1 — Verify Mass Closure $$ \begin{aligned} \sum \text{mass fractions} &= C + H + O + N + S + \text{Ash} \\ &= 42.5 + 6.0 + 41.2 + 0.8 + 0.1 + 5.0 \\ &= 95.6\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** The difference (100 − 95.6 = 4.4%) may include trace moisture still bound within the dry sample or unaccounted oxygen. Since ash is already accounted for, the combustible portion is **C + H + O + N + S = 95.6%**. We proceed with these values. --- ### Step 2 — Compute HHV Using Dulong-Berthelot $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HHV} &= 0.3383\,C \;+\; 1.422\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 0.0954\,S \\[6pt] &= 0.3383 \times 42.5 \;+\; 1.422 \times \left(6.0 - \dfrac{41.2}{8}\right) \;+\; 0.0954 \times 0.1 \\[6pt] &= 14.378 \;+\; 1.422 \times \left(6.0 - 5.15\right) \;+\; 0.00954 \\[6pt] &= 14.378 \;+\; 1.422 \times 0.85 \;+\; 0.00954 \\[6pt] &= 14.378 \;+\; 1.209 \;+\; 0.00954 \\[6pt] \text{HHV} &= 15.596 \;\text{MJ/kg (dry basis)} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** The term $\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)$ accounts for the fact that some hydrogen is already bound to oxygen as hydroxyl groups and cannot contribute to combustion heat. The factor $1.422$ is the energy released per kg of "net" (combustible) hydrogen oxidized to liquid water. --- ### Step 3 — Compute LHV on As-Fired Basis The LHV formula accounts for latent heat of vaporization of water formed from hydrogen combustion and the original moisture in the fuel: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LHV}_{\text{dry}} &= \text{HHV} - 2.442 \times \left(\dfrac{9H}{100}\right) \\ &= 15.596 - 2.442 \times \left(\dfrac{9 \times 6.0}{100}\right) \\ &= 15.596 - 2.442 \times 0.54 \\ &= 15.596 - 1.319 \\ &= 14.277 \;\text{MJ/kg (dry)} \end{aligned} $$ **Now adjust for 10% as-fired moisture:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{LHV}_{\text{as-fired}} &= \text{LHV}_{\text{dry}} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{w}{100}\right) - 2.442 \times \dfrac{w}{100} \\[6pt] &= 14.277 \times 0.90 - 2.442 \times 0.10 \\[6pt] &= 12.849 - 0.2442 \\[6pt] \text{LHV}_{\text{as-fired}} &= 12.605 \;\text{MJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** First, dilution by moisture reduces combustible fraction by $(1-w)$. Second, this moisture evaporates during combustion, absorbing latent heat ($2.442$ MJ per kg of water). Both effects are subtracted. --- ### Step 4 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement (Dry Basis) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Air}_{\text{st}} &= \dfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S}{23.2} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{11.6 \times 42.5 + 34.8 \times 0.85 + 4.35 \times 0.1}{23.2} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{493.0 + 29.58 + 0.435}{23.2} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{523.015}{23.2} \\[6pt] \text{Air}_{\text{st}} &= 22.54 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** The numerator represents the oxygen demand of the fuel (kg O₂ per 100 kg fuel, divided conceptually). The denominator (23.2 = 100/23.3 × …) converts kg O₂ to kg air, since air contains 23.2% oxygen by mass. --- ### Step 5 — Actual Air with 30% Excess $$ \begin{aligned} e &= 1 + 0.30 = 1.30 \\[6pt] \text{Air}_{\text{actual}} &= e \times \text{Air}_{\text{st}} = 1.30 \times 22.54 = 29.30 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Briquetting Mass & Energy Balance > **Problem:** A sago factory in Palakkad generates **6 tonnes/day of wet thippi** (75% moisture, wet basis). It intends to sun-dry the thippi to **12% moisture** and then briquette it. The briquetting press operates **8 hours/day**, requires **drying energy of 3.2 MJ per kg of water evaporated**, and briquetting energy of **0.4 MJ/kg of dry briquette produced**. Find: > (a) Mass of dry briquettes produced per day. > (b) Total process energy input (MJ/day). > (c) Net energy content of briquettes if HHV = 16 MJ/kg (dry). ### Step 1 — Initial Mass of Wet Thippi $$ M_{\text{wet}} = 6000 \;\text{kg/day}, \quad w_1 = 0.75 $$ Dry solid mass (water-free basis): $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{dry solid}} &= M_{\text{wet}} \times (1 - w_1) \\ &= 6000 \times 0.25 \\ &= 1500 \;\text{kg dry solid/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Mass After Drying to 12% Moisture Let $M_{\text{dry}}$ be mass after drying at moisture $w_2 = 0.12$: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{dry solid}} &= M_{\text{dry}} \times (1 - w_2) \\ 1500 &= M_{\text{dry}} \times 0.88 \\ M_{\text{dry}} &= \dfrac{1500}{0.88} = 1704.5 \;\text{kg/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Water Evaporated During Drying $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{evap}} &= M_{\text{wet}} - M_{\text{dry}} \\ &= 6000 - 1704.5 \\ &= 4295.5 \;\text{kg water/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Drying Energy Required $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{dry}} &= W_{\text{evap}} \times 3.2 \;\text{MJ/kg} \\ &= 4295.5 \times 3.2 \\ &= 13{,}745.6 \;\text{MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Briquetting Energy $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{briq}} &= M_{\text{dry}} \times 0.4 \;\text{MJ/kg} \\ &= 1704.5 \times 0.4 \\ &= 681.8 \;\text{MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Total Process Energy & Net Energy Output $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{total in}} &= E_{\text{dry}} + E_{\text{briq}} = 13{,}745.6 + 681.8 = 14{,}427.4 \;\text{MJ/day} \\[6pt] E_{\text{output}} &= M_{\text{dry}} \times \text{HHV} = 1704.5 \times 16 = 27{,}272 \;\text{MJ/day} \\[6pt] \text{Net Energy Gain} &= 27{,}272 - 14{,}427.4 = 12{,}844.6 \;\text{MJ/day} \\[6pt] \text{Energy ROI} &= \dfrac{27{,}272}{14{,}427.4} = 1.89 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{1.89\;\text{MJ output per MJ input}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Drying dominates energy consumption (~95% of total input). In real practice, Kerala's solar insolation (≈ 4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day) can provide ~50–70% of this drying energy through open sun drying — making the energy ROI effectively **3–5×** when solar drying is used. ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Thippi vs Other Biomass Fuels | Property | Tapioca Thippi | Rice Husk | Coconut Shell | Bagasse | Wood Sawdust | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | HHV (MJ/kg, dry) | **15–17** | 13–15 | 18–21 | 17–19 | 16–18 | | Ash (%) | **3–6** ⭐ | 15–20 | 2–4 | 1–4 | 0.5–2 | | Volatile Matter (%) | **70–78** | 65–72 | 65–75 | 75–85 | 75–82 | | Fixed Carbon (%) | 12–18 | 12–16 | 18–22 | 10–15 | 15–20 | | Bulk Density (kg/m³) | 150–250 | 100–150 | 500–650 | 80–150 | 180–250 | | Briquetting Ease | High | Medium | Low (hard) | Medium | High | | Keralan Availability | **High** ⭐ | High | High | Medium | Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Verdict:** Thippi combines **low ash** (unlike rice husk — minimizes slagging), **high volatiles** (good for gasification), and **Kerala-specific abundance** — making it the most strategically valuable agro-residue fuel in the state's bioenergy roadmap. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Architecture — Tapioca Thippi-to-Fuel Conversion Pathways ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cassava Tubers Harvested] --> B[Peeling and Washing] B --> C[Crushing and Rasping] C --> D[Starch Slurry] D --> E[Starch Milk Extraction] E --> F[Wet Tapioca Thippi
70 to 80 percent Moisture] E --> G[Starch Product
Market Sale] F --> H{Conversion Route Selection} H --> R1[Route 1: Direct Combustion] H --> R2[Route 2: Densification Briquettes] H --> R3[Route 3: Thermochemical Conversion] H --> R4[Route 4: Biochemical Digestion] R1 --> R1A[Sun Drying to 15 percent MC] R1A --> R1B[Loose Combustion in Fixed Grate Boiler] R1B --> R1C[Process Steam for Sago Factory] R2 --> R2A[Mechanical Dewatering Screw Press] R2A --> R2B[Sun Drying to 10 to 12 percent MC] R2B --> R2C[Grinding below 6 mm] R2C --> R2D[Optional Binder Cassava Starch Slurry] R2D --> R2E[Screw Press Briquetting 15 to 25 MPa] R2E --> R2F[White Coal Briquettes
HHV 16 to 18 MJ per kg] R2F --> R2G[Industrial Boiler or Household Fuel] R3 --> R3A[Drying to less than 10 percent MC] R3A --> R3B{Gasifier or Pyrolyser} R3B --> R3C[Producer Gas 4 to 6 MJ per Nm3] R3B --> R3D[Bio Oil and Bio Char] R3C --> R3E[Engine or Turbine] R3D --> R3F[Soil Amendment and Liquid Fuel] R4 --> R4A[Direct Wet Feed to Anaerobic Digester] R4A --> R4B[Methanogenic Bacteria 30 to 40 day HRT] R4B --> R4C[Biogas 60 to 70 percent CH4] R4C --> R4D[Cooking or Engine CHP] R4B --> R4E[Digestate as Organic Fertilizer] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Densification Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT STREAM"] WT[Wet Thippi
75 percent MC] end subgraph PRETREAT["PRE TREATMENT MODULE"] D1[Mechanical Screw Press
Reduces MC to 60 percent] D2[Rotary Drum Solar Dryer
Reduces MC to 12 percent] D1 --> D2 end subgraph SIZE_RED["SIZE REDUCTION"] G1[Hammer Mill
Particle Size less than 6 mm] end subgraph MIXING["BINDER MIXING UNIT"] M1[Cassava Starch Binder
5 to 10 percent by weight] end subgraph COMPACTION["COMPACTION MODULE"] C1[Variable Speed Screw Extruder
Pressure 15 to 25 MPa] C2[Heating Element 80 to 120 degree C] C1 --- C2 end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT FUEL PRODUCT"] BR[Briquette Diameter 50 to 90 mm
Density 700 to 1100 kg per m3
HHV 16 to 18 MJ per kg] end WT --> D1 D2 --> G1 G1 --> M1 M1 --> C1 C1 --> BR ``` ## 4.3 Mass-Energy Flow Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[1000 kg Wet Thippi at 75 percent MC] -->|Drying| P2[294 kg Dried Thippi at 12 percent MC] P2 -->|Briquetting| P3[290 kg Briquettes at 8 percent MC] P1 -->|Energy Out during Drying| E1[706 kg Water Vaporized
Heat of Vaporization 1766 MJ] P2 -->|Energy In Compression| E2[Briquetting Energy 117 MJ] P3 -->|Combustion Energy Released| E3[HHV Output approx 4640 MJ] E1 --> BAL{Energy Balance Check} E2 --> BAL E3 --> BAL BAL --> RESULT[Net Energy Released approx 2757 MJ
Process Energy Recovery Ratio approx 1.89] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The block diagrams above are designed to be KTU-exam-friendly mental models. In a 14-mark question, drawing such a flowchart with labeled boxes for *input → pretreatment → size reduction → compaction → output* carries **3–4 marks** by itself, and combined with mass/energy calculations carries full marks. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **Define tapioca thippi. List three reasons why it is considered a promising fuel in Kerala.** **Model Answer:** Tapioca thippi is the fibrous residual biomass discharged during starch extraction from cassava tubers. It is a promising fuel in Kerala because: (i) **high availability** — Kerala produces over 2.5 million tonnes of cassava annually, generating large thippi volumes; (ii) **low ash content** (~3–6%) compared to rice husk (~15–20%), minimizing boiler slagging; (iii) **good calorific value** (HHV ≈ 14–17 MJ/kg on dry basis) suitable for direct combustion and briquette production. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **Differentiate between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a biomass fuel such as tapioca thippi.** **Model Answer:** **HHV (Gross Calorific Value):** Total heat released when fuel is completely combusted and the water vapor in the combustion products is condensed back to liquid, recovering its latent heat of vaporization. **LHV (Net Calorific Value):** Heat released when products of combustion leave as water *vapor* — the latent heat is **not** recovered. For tapioca thippi with H ≈ 6%, the difference HHV − LHV ≈ 2.442 × (9H/100) ≈ **1.3 MJ/kg**. LHV is always lower than HHV and is more representative of real boiler performance since flue gases leave hot. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common mistake #1:** Students often forget to subtract ash percentage before applying the Dulong-Berthelot equation, or use the as-fired values without converting to dry basis. **Always state the basis explicitly** (dry basis vs as-fired basis) at the top of your solution. > - **Common mistake #2:** In briquetting problems, students use the *wet* mass instead of the *dry solid* mass for the moisture balance. The dry solid mass is invariant during drying — it is the moisture that changes. > - **Common mistake #3:** When asked for "energy output," students give energy *input* (process energy) instead of combustion energy released. Read the question wording carefully. > - **Common mistake #4:** Units — losing 1–2 marks by mixing MJ with kJ, or kg with tonnes. Always write units in every line of the solution. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style] **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe, with a neat flowchart, the various routes by which tapioca thippi can be utilized as a fuel. Compare briquetting and biogas production pathways in terms of energy density, capital cost, and suitability for Kerala's small-scale industries. **Model Solution Outline:** 1. **Generation:** Brief description of thippi generation in sago/starch industry. 2. **Conversion Routes (Flowchart):** - Direct Combustion → Boiler Steam - Briquette / Pellet Production → "White Coal" - Gasification → Producer Gas - Pyrolysis → Bio-oil + Bio-char - Anaerobic Digestion → Biogas + Digestate 3. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Briquetting | Biogas Production | |---|---|---| | Energy Density (MJ/kg or MJ/m³) | 16–18 MJ/kg | 20–25 MJ/m³ | | Moisture Tolerance | Requires drying (<15%) | Works with wet feed (70–80%) | | Capital Cost | Medium (₹10–25 lakh) | Higher (₹25–60 lakh) | | Payback Period | 1.5–3 years | 3–5 years | | Best Suited For | Small factories with own steam demand | Farms with cattle dung co-digestion | | Kerala Industry Suitability | ⭐ High | Medium | 4. **Conclusion:** For Kerala's starch factories, briquetting is the most techno-economically viable route due to its moderate cost and direct synergy with the factory's own steam demand. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A tapioca thippi sample has the following ultimate analysis on dry basis: C = 41.0%, H = 5.8%, O = 42.0%, N = 1.0%, S = 0.2%, Ash = 5.5%. If the fuel is burned with 35% excess air, calculate: (i) HHV of the fuel (MJ/kg, dry basis). (ii) Stoichiometric air requirement (kg air/kg fuel). (iii) Actual air supplied (kg air/kg fuel). **Model Solution:** **(i) HHV by Dulong-Berthelot:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{HHV} &= 0.3383 \times 41.0 + 1.422 \times \left(5.8 - \dfrac{42.0}{8}\right) + 0.0954 \times 0.2 \\ &= 13.870 + 1.422 \times (5.8 - 5.25) + 0.0191 \\ &= 13.870 + 1.422 \times 0.55 + 0.0191 \\ &= 13.870 + 0.782 + 0.019 \\ \boxed{\text{HHV} &= 14.671 \;\text{MJ/kg}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Stoichiometric Air:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Air}_{\text{st}} &= \dfrac{11.6 \times 41.0 + 34.8 \times 0.55 + 4.35 \times 0.2}{23.2} \\ &= \dfrac{475.6 + 19.14 + 0.87}{23.2} \\ &= \dfrac{495.61}{23.2} \\ \boxed{\text{Air}_{\text{st}} &= 21.36 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Actual Air with 35% Excess:** $$ \begin{aligned} e &= 1.35 \\ \boxed{\text{Air}_{\text{actual}} &= 1.35 \times 21.36 = 28.84 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the process of briquette manufacturing from tapioca thippi. Discuss the role of binders, the significance of compaction pressure, and the engineering properties that make thippi briquettes a marketable fuel. **Model Solution Outline:** 1. **Raw Material Preparation:** - Dewatering by screw press (MC 75% → 60%) - Sun/solar drying (MC → 12%) - Hammer mill grinding (<6 mm particle size) 2. **Binder Addition:** - Cassava starch slurry (5–10% by weight) — *unique synergistic advantage* since the same factory produces starch. - Alternative: molasses, lime, clay. - Binder ensures briquette integrity during handling and transport. 3. **Compaction:** - Screw extruder / piston press / roller press - Pressure: **15–25 MPa** - Die temperature: **80–120 °C** — lignin softens (Tg ≈ 90–100 °C) and acts as a natural binder. 4. **Engineering Properties of Thippi Briquettes:** - Bulk density: **700–1100 kg/m³** (vs 150–250 kg/m³ for loose thippi) - HHV: **16–18 MJ/kg** (dry) - Moisture: < 10% - Durability Index: > 90% - Combustion time: 60–90 min per kg (vs 15–20 min for loose form) 5. **Marketability:** Comparable to firewood/charcoal; can be sold as "white coal" to brick kilns, tea estates, and household segments. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A sago processing unit in Palakkad generates 8 tonnes/day of wet tapioca thippi with 78% moisture (wet basis). The thippi is sun-dried to 14% moisture and then briquetted. The briquetting press operates 10 hours/day. Calculate: (i) The mass of dried thippi obtained per day. (ii) The mass of water removed during drying. (iii) If each briquette weighs 250 g, how many briquettes are produced per day? **Model Solution:** **(i) Mass of dried thippi:** Dry solid mass (constant through drying): $$ M_{\text{dry solid}} = 8000 \times (1 - 0.78) = 8000 \times 0.22 = 1760 \;\text{kg/day} $$ At 14% moisture: $$ M_{\text{dried}} = \dfrac{1760}{1 - 0.14} = \dfrac{1760}{0.86} = \boxed{2046.5 \;\text{kg/day}} $$ **(ii) Mass of water removed:** $$ W_{\text{removed}} = 8000 - 2046.5 = \boxed{5953.5 \;\text{kg water/day}} $$ **(iii) Number of briquettes:** $$ N = \dfrac{2046.5 \times 1000}{250} = \boxed{8186 \;\text{briquettes/day}} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tapioca Thippi as Fuel:** - **Definition:** Fibrous residue from cassava starch extraction; lignocellulosic agro-biomass. - **Generation:** ~0.25–0.40 kg dry thippi per kg fresh tuber; Kerala produces millions of tonnes. - **Moisture (wet):** 70–80%; (dried): 10–15%. - **HHV:** **14–17 MJ/kg (dry)**; **LHV:** **~13–15 MJ/kg (dry)**. - **Low Ash** (3–6%) is a key advantage over rice husk. - **Dulong Equation:** $\text{HHV} = 0.3383\,C + 1.422(H - O/8) + 0.0954\,S$ - **Conversion Routes (4):** Direct Combustion | Briquettes/Pellets | Gasification/Pyrolysis | Biogas (Anaerobic Digestion). - **Briquette conditions:** Compaction pressure 15–25 MPa; die temp 80–120 °C; binder 5–10% cassava starch. - **Briquette density:** 700–1100 kg/m³ (4–6× increase over loose thippi). - **Biogas yield:** ~0.2–0.4 m³ CH₄ per kg VS (volatile solids) added. - **Synergy with Kerala:** Starch slurry acts as a *zero-cost binder* for briquettes — an elegant industrial symbiosis. - **Environmental benefit:** Reduces open dumping (which causes H₂S and methane emissions from anaerobic decay), offsets fossil fuel use, supports circular economy. - **Key exam formulas:** Dulong-Berthelot, LHV correction, stoichiometric air, drying mass balance, energy ROI. > **One-line memory anchor:** *"Thippi is Kerala's quiet white coal — dry it, press it, burn it."* ---

topics.title: Extraction of starch by hydrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extraction of starch by hydrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Starch Extraction by Hydrolysis** is a biochemical process engineering operation in which polymeric starch granules—recovered from agro-industrial waste streams and byproducts (cassava peels, potato pulp, corn steep liquor solids, rice bran, sago waste, mango kernel, etc.)—are depolymerized into shorter-chain saccharides (dextrins, maltose, and ultimately glucose) using **catalytic cleavage of glycosidic bonds** by either mineral acids, hydrolytic enzymes, or a sequential combination of both.

In the strict KTU 2024 framework, the term "extraction by hydrolysis" refers to the *integrated upstream–downstream unit operations* in which:
- **Upstream**: Starch-bearing lignocellulosic or tuberaceous biomass is pre-treated, milled, and slurried.
- **Core Reaction**: The slurry undergoes controlled hydrolytic depolymerization.
- **Downstream**: The hydrolysate is neutralized, filtered, decolorized, and concentrated to yield either a purified starch isolate or a sugar-rich syrup.

The general stoichiometric reaction is given as:

$$
(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + n\,H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Acid / Enzyme}} n\,C_6H_{12}O_6
$$

Where:
- $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n$ represents the repeating anhydroglucose unit of starch.
- $n$ denotes the **degree of polymerization (DP)**, ranging from **300 to 3000** for native starches.
- $C_6H_{12}O_6$ is the monomeric **D-glucose** end-product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PGAET753, Module 3):**
> The extraction of starch by hydrolysis is treated as a *value-addition pathway* for solid food-processing wastes. The hydrolysis step is classified into **Acid Hydrolysis (AH)**, **Enzymatic Hydrolysis (EH)**, and **Dual/Combined Hydrolysis**, each with distinct kinetics, equipment, and product profiles.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a long **beaded necklace** (the starch polymer) where each bead is a glucose sugar. This necklace is tightly coiled into a microscopic granule inside plant cells. To "extract" useful sugars from waste biomass, we need to:

1. **Unravel the necklace** (milling & slurrying).
2. **Cut the string between every bead** (hydrolysis) so individual glucose beads fall out.
3. **Collect and clean the beads** (downstream purification).

The "scissors" used to cut the string can be either:
