## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** List the major byproducts of the sugarcane industry and mention the most common animal feed application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major byproducts of the sugarcane industry and their primary feed applications are:

1. **Sugarcane tops** – Used as **green fodder or silage** for cattle, especially during summer scarcity.
2. **Bagasse** – Used as a **fibrous roughage** in complete feed blocks after **chemical/biological delignification**.
3. **Molasses** – Used as a **high-energy liquid supplement** and as a **binder** in **UMMB** (Urea-Molasses Mineral Blocks).
4. **Press-mud (filter cake)** – Used as a **mineral-protein supplement** after **de-waxing and drying**, or as organic fertilizer via composting.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, name the byproduct AND its specific feed application — do not leave either blank.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is **raw bagasse not recommended for direct feeding** to ruminants?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Raw bagasse is unsuitable for direct feeding because:

1. **Low digestibility (25–30%)** due to high **lignin (20–25%)** that physically shields cellulose from rumen microbial cellulase.
2. **Low crude protein (2–3%)** and **low palatability** leading to poor intake.
3. **High fibre content (NDF 80–85%)** that exceeds the threshold for optimal rumen fermentation, often causing impaction in cattle.

Hence, **pre-treatment (NaOH, urea ammoniation, fungal delignification, or steam explosion)** is essential to break the lignin-carbohydrate complex before feeding.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Bagasse & Molasses Focus*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **chemical treatment methods** used to improve the nutritional value of **sugarcane bagasse** as animal feed. Mention the chemistry of each treatment.

**(b) [7 marks)** A UMMB block contains **50% molasses, 10% urea, 25% rice bran, 8% mineral mix, 4% salt, and 3% lime**. Calculate the **% sugar, % equivalent crude protein (from urea), and total protein contribution**, assuming molasses contains 50% sugar and urea is 45% N. What is the **max safe daily intake** for a 400 kg cow, assuming the block should supply <30% of the animal's daily N requirement of 80 g?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chemical treatment of bagasse (7 marks):**

Three major chemical treatments are used:

**1. Alkali treatment (NaOH / KOH / Ca(OH)₂):**
- Dose: 3–5% NaOH on DM basis; 48 h soaking at room temperature.
- **Chemistry:** Alkali **saponifies** the ester bonds between lignin and hemicellulose, dissolves some hemicellulose, and **swells** the cellulose fibres by disrupting hydrogen bonds.
- **Result:** Cellulose exposed to rumen microbes; digestibility rises from 28% → 50–55%.

**2. Urea (Ammoniation) treatment:**
- Dose: 4% urea + 50% moisture; sealed storage 3 weeks.
- **Chemistry:** Urea hydrolyses to **NH₃ + CO₂** under urease action:
  $$\text{CO(NH}_2\text{)}_2 + H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{urease}} 2\,NH_3 + CO_2$$
- NH₃ swells fibre and also provides **non-protein nitrogen (NPN)** to rumen microbes, which use it to build microbial protein.
- **Result:** Digestibility ↑ 8–12 percentage points; CP content rises from 3% → 10–12%.

**3. Oxidative delignification (H₂O₂ / Ozone):**
- 1–2% H₂O₂ at pH 11.5, 25 °C, 24 h.
- **Chemistry:** H₂O₂ generates **hydroxyl radicals (·OH)** that **oxidise lignin aromatic rings**, breaking the polymer into soluble fragments.
- **Result:** Selective lignin removal (up to 50%); mild cellulose loss.
- Safer and more environmentally friendly than chlorine-based bleaching.

---

**(b) UMMB Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — % Sugar in the block**

$$Sugar\,(\%) = 50 \times 0.50 = 25.00\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent CP from urea**

Urea is 45% N, hence CP equivalent:

$$CP_{urea}\,(\%) = 10 \times 0.45 \times 6.25 = 28.125\,\%$$

(The factor 6.25 converts N → CP.)

**Step 3 — Total CP contribution (urea + inherent CP of other ingredients, assumed ~2% for rice bran, 3% for molasses)**

$$CP_{molasses} = 50 \times 0.03 = 1.5\,\%$$

$$CP_{rice\,bran} = 25 \times 0.02 = 0.5\,\%$$

$$CP_{total} = 28.125 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 30.125\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Maximum safe daily intake**

The cow's daily N requirement = 80 g. The block should supply **at most 30%** of this = 24 g N/day.

N contributed by 1 kg of block = $1000\,g \times 0.10\,(urea) \times 0.45 = 45\,g\,N$ (only urea N considered as the relevant supplementary N).

Wait — we need N from the *block* as a whole, considering urea = 10% of the block.

N per kg block (from urea only) = $1000 \times 0.10 \times 0.45 = 45\,g$.

But this exceeds the *safe cap*. We must apply the 30% cap:

$$N_{allowed} = 80 \times 0.30 = 24\,g\,N/day$$

Since 1 kg of block gives 45 g N (urea only):

$$Block\,intake_{max} = \frac{24}{45} \times 1000 = 533\,g/day$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The cow should consume **no more than ~530 g of UMMB per day** to avoid urea toxicity (which can cause fatal rumen ammonia poisoning above 50 g N per 100 kg body weight).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the 30% cap and quote 1 kg/day — this is dangerous. Always tie the calculation to the *specific physiological limit* mentioned in the question.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Sugarcane Tops & Press-Mud Focus*

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **nutritional composition, limitations, and processing methods** of **sugarcane tops** for use as cattle feed.

**(b) [7 marks]** What is **press-mud**? Explain its **composition, anti-nutritional factors, processing steps to make it feed-grade, and inclusion level** in ruminant concentrate mixtures.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sugarcane Tops (SCT) – 7 marks:**

**Composition (per 100 g DM):**
- Crude protein: 4–6 g
- Crude fibre: 30–35 g
- Ether extract: 1.5–2.0 g
- Total ash: 6–8 g
- NFE (soluble carbohydrates): 50–55 g
- Oxalic acid: 1.5–2.0 g
- In vivo DM digestibility: 50–55%

**Limitations:**
1. **Seasonal availability** (only Nov–Mar); wilts rapidly.
2. **High oxalate** binds dietary Ca → risk of **hypocalcaemia** and **osteodystrophy** in long-term feeding.
3. **High moisture (65–75%)** makes transportation uneconomical and storage prone to mould.
4. **Lower palatability** than maize fodder or Napier grass.

**Processing methods:**

1. **Chopping** (2–3 cm) – increases intake 15–20%.
2. **Ensiling:**
   - Chop + wilt to 30–35% DM.
   - Add 5% molasses as fermentable sugar.
   - Pack in trench/pit silo; ferment 6–8 weeks.
   - pH drops to 3.8–4.2; lactic acid = 4–6% DM.
   - Oxalate reduced by 30–50% due to microbial degradation.
3. **Urea treatment (4% urea, 50% moisture, 3 weeks):** CP increases from 5% to ~12% via NH₃ fixation.
4. **Sun-drying + Pelleting:** DM ↑ 90%; long shelf life; transportable.
5. **Supplementation:** Combine with legume fodder (e.g., *Stylosanthes*) at 1:1 ratio to balance protein.

---

**(b) Press-mud (Filter Cake) – 7 marks:**

**Definition:** Press-mud is the **semi-solid residue** collected from the **rotary vacuum filter** during clarification of sugarcane juice, where lime and phosphoric acid precipitate impurities.

**Composition (on DM basis):**
- Waxes (crude): 5–14%
- Crude protein: 12–16%
- Sugars (residual): 5–8%
- Crude fibre: 25–30%
- Calcium: 3–5%
- Phosphorus: 1.5–2.5%
- Potassium: 1.0–1.5%
- Moisture (as collected): 70–80%

**Anti-nutritional / Limiting factors:**
1. **High moisture** – spoils in 48–72 h.
2. **High wax content** – reduces digestibility, unpalatable.
3. **High ash and variable mineral content** – risk of mineral imbalance.
4. **Possible pesticide/heavy metal residues** depending on cane source.
5. **Pathogens** (E. coli, fungal spores) from raw filter cake.

**Processing steps to feed-grade:**
1. **Sun-drying or rotary drum drying** → DM ↑ 85–90% (shelf life ↑ 6 months).
2. **Solvent extraction (hexane at 60–80 °C)** → removes 90% wax → **sugarcane wax** is recovered as valuable industrial byproduct.
3. **De-oiled press-mud** is then ground and sieved.
4. Optional: **Urea + molasses enrichment (2% + 5%)** → raises CP and palatability.
5. **Quality check:** aflatoxin <20 ppb, total ash <20%, CP >10%.

**Inclusion level:** **5–10%** of concentrate mixture for adult cattle; up to 15% in sheep/goat rations.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (each costs 2–3 marks):**
> 1. **Writing "bagasse is used as feed" without mentioning delignification** — examiner expects processing discussion.
> 2. **Confusing molasses with jaggery or syrup** — molasses is the *final* mother liquor (C-molasses), not the first syrup (A-molasses).
> 3. **Omitting the chemistry of NaOH treatment** — students often say "it improves digestibility" without writing the lignin-carbohydrate ester saponification.
> 4. **Quoting TDN without showing calculation steps** — show every arithmetic.
> 5. **Forgetting the 6.25 factor in CP calculation** — using 5.7 or any other factor is a direct 1-mark cut.
> 6. **Confusing UMMB with UMB (urea mineral block)** — UMMB has molasses; UMB has no molasses.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & High-Yield Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print and Pin on Wall:**

- [ ] **Sugarcane tops** = green leafy top, 4–6% CP, 25–35% DM, oxalate 1.5–2% is the key anti-nutrient; ensiling with 5% molasses is the gold-standard preservation.
- [ ] **Bagasse** = fibrous, 2–3% CP, lignin 20–25%, raw digestibility 25–30%, after NaOH (4%, 48 h) → 50–55%.
- [ ] **Three delignification routes:** Chemical (NaOH/urea/H₂O₂), Physical (steam/grinding), Biological (white-rot fungi, 21 d).
- [ ] **Molasses** = 45–55% sugar, TDN ~75%, used as energy + UMMB binder; max 10% of ration; laxative beyond 15%.
- [ ] **UMMB formula:** 50% molasses + 10% urea + 25% bran + 8% mineral + 4% salt + 3% lime.
- [ ] **Urea → NH₃ reaction:** must be remembered for the 7-mark "explain with chemistry" question.
- [ ] **Press-mud** = wax 5–14%, CP 12–16%, Ca 3–5%, P 1.5–2.5%; de-wax with hexane; inclusion 5–10%.
- [ ] **CP calculation** uses factor **6.25**; N from Kjeldahl × 6.25.
- [ ] **TDN formula:** DCP + DCF + 2.25·DEE + DNFE; fat energy multiplier = 2.25.
- [ ] **Circular economy angle:** 4 byproducts → 4 different feed products, zero waste, dual income (wax + feed).
- [ ] **Kerala relevance:** VIVA (Vibrant Village Animal Husbandry) projects use UMMB widely — can be cited in viva for extra credit.
- [ ] **Numericals to master:** Kjeldahl N% → CP%, TDN from Weende analysis, % improvement in digestibility, UMMB safe intake limit.

---

**End of Notes — KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Output**

topics.title: By-products of fruits and vegetables-based agro-industries: Mango seed kernel and pineapple waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET753 - WASTE AND BYPRODUCTS UTILIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** By-products of fruits and vegetables-based agro-industries: Mango seed kernel and pineapple waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fruit and Vegetable Agro-Industry By-Products — Context

Agro-industries processing fruits and vegetables generate enormous quantities of solid and liquid by-products — peels, seeds, kernels, cores, pomace, leaves, and crown portions. These streams, if discarded, cause severe environmental pollution (high BOD, COD, and methane emissions from landfill dumps), yet they are **rich reservoirs of bioactive phytochemicals, edible oils, dietary fibre, enzymes, polyphenols, and fermentable sugars**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> By-products of fruit and vegetable agro-industries refer to the residual biomass streams (peels, seeds, kernels, cores, leaves, pulp residues) generated during primary processing of horticultural crops — such as mango pulp/canning, pineapple canning, juice extraction, and dehydration industries — which can be valorized into value-added products (oils, starches, enzymes, dietary fibre, antioxidants, biogas, and platform chemicals) through appropriate physical, chemical, biochemical, or thermochemical conversion technologies.

## 1.2 Mango Seed Kernel — Intuitive Overview

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the mango seed kernel as the *hidden treasure chest* inside the fruit. When you enjoy a mango, you throw away a fibrous pit. Inside that pit lies the **kernel (cotyledon)** — a small almond-shaped structure that looks and behaves very much like a cocoa bean or a nut. Just as cocoa beans yield cocoa butter used in chocolates and cosmetics, the mango kernel yields **mango butter**, a creamy, pale fat with remarkable emollient and skin-healing properties. The same kernel also contains high-quality **starch (60–70 %)** comparable to corn or tapioca starch, plus antioxidant polyphenols (mangiferin, gallic acid, ellagic acid) — making it a true *multi-product biorefinery feedstock* rather than waste.

> [!NOTE]
> **Procurement Reality:** India is the world's largest mango producer (~**20 million tonnes/year**). The seed accounts for **15–20 %** of the whole fruit weight, and the kernel represents **45–75 %** of the seed. Annually, India generates an estimated **1.0–1.5 million tonnes** of mango seed kernel as a by-product of the pulp, pickle, and canning industries.

## 1.3 Pineapple Waste — Intuitive Overview

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a pineapple as a small *biochemical factory on a leaf*. When the canner slices the fruit, it discards the rough skin (peel), the fibrous central core, the spiky crown leaves, and the stem. Collectively this is **40–55 %** of the whole fruit's weight — and yet it is one of the richest natural sources of the proteolytic enzyme **bromelain**, comparable to papaya's papain. The same waste holds fermentable sugars, dietary fibre, pectin, and even organic acids. With a small investment in extraction, the *entire pineapple* can be monetized — fruit for fresh/canned consumption, and waste for enzymes, fibre, vinegar, biogas, and nutraceuticals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Procurement Reality:** Global pineapple production is ~**29 million tonnes/year**, dominated by the Philippines, Costa Rica, Brazil, Thailand, and India. Canning and juice industries generate **40–55 %** solid residue. The peel alone contributes ~**30–35 %** of fruit weight and is exceptionally rich in **bromelain (200–500 mg/kg fresh peel)** and fermentable sugars.

## 1.4 Quantitative Significance (Standard Metrics)

| Metric | Mango Seed Kernel | Pineapple Waste |
|---|---|---|
| Generation share (% of whole fruit) | **15–20 %** (seed); **45–75 %** of seed = kernel | **40–55 %** (peel + core + crown + stem) |
| Moisture content (fresh basis) | **45–55 %** | **80–85 %** (peel), **75–80 %** (core) |
| Key value component | Fat (**8–15 %**), Starch (**55–70 %**), Protein (**6–10 %**) | Bromelain, Sugars (**10–15 %**), Fibre (**15–25 %**) |
| Primary valorized product | Mango butter, kernel starch, polyphenols | Bromelain enzyme, dietary fibre, biogas |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mango Seed Kernel — Composition & Valorization Pathways

### 2.1.1 Anatomical Origin
The mango fruit (*Mangifera indica*) develops a single, large, fibrous, ovoid **stone (endocarp)** that encloses the **kernel (cotyledon)** — a soft, cream-coloured, oil-rich tissue. Industrially, the kernel is recovered from mango pulp / canning units by:
1. Stone removal from pulp line.
2. Decortication (manual/mechanical) to separate fibrous husk from kernel.
3. Drying (sun/solar/mechanical at 50–60 °C to **moisture < 8 %**) for shelf-life.
4. Storage in cool, dry, insect-proof containers.

### 2.1.2 Proximate & Phytochemical Composition

The dry mango seed kernel typically contains:

- **Carbohydrates (mainly starch): 55–70 %**
- **Crude fat (mango butter): 8–15 %**
- **Crude protein: 6–10 %**
- **Crude fibre: 2–4 %**
- **Polyphenols (mangiferin, gallic acid, ellagic acid, quercetin): 1–3 %**
- **Ash (minerals — K, Mg, Ca, P): 2–4 %**
- **Moisture: 5–8 %** (after drying)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mangiferin** is a xanthone glucoside unique to mangoes with proven antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, and anti-viral activity. Its concentration in the kernel ranges from **0.2 to 0.9 % (dry weight basis)**.

### 2.1.3 Mango Butter (Kernel Fat) — Properties

Mango butter is extracted by:
- **Solvent extraction** (hexane, petroleum ether at 60 °C) — industrial preferred
- **Mechanical expression** (cold/hot screw press) — for organic certification
- **Supercritical CO₂ extraction** — premium grade, no solvent residue

The refined fat has the following characteristics:

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Melting point (slip point) | **32–38 °C** (close to body temperature) |
| Saponification value | **180–195 mg KOH/g** |
| Iodine value | **35–55 g I₂/100 g** (semi-solid) |
| Free fatty acids (as oleic) | **0.5–5.0 %** |
| Major fatty acids | Oleic (**40–50 %**), Stearic (**35–45 %**), Palmitic (**5–8 %**), Linoleic (**1–5 %**), Arachidic (**1–3 %**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mango butter has a **fatty-acid profile and melting behaviour very similar to cocoa butter** (with slightly higher stearic and oleic content), making it a high-value **cocoa-butter equivalent (CBE)** in chocolate formulations.

### 2.1.4 Mango Kernel Starch
The kernel is rich in starch granules of size **5–25 µm** (comparable to rice/maize). Properties:
- Amylose content: **20–35 %**
- Gelatinization temperature: **65–78 °C**
- Pasting viscosity: Moderate, suitable for noodles, sauces, biodegradable films
- Extraction yield: **40–55 %** of kernel dry weight

### 2.1.5 Polyphenol & Antioxidant Recovery
Mangiferin and gallic acid are recovered by **solvent extraction** (ethanol 50–80 %, 40–60 °C) followed by **resin purification / membrane filtration / crystallization**.

## 2.2 Pineapple Waste — Composition & Valorization Pathways

### 2.2.1 Waste Stream Anatomy
A pineapple (*Ananas comosus*) processing unit generates:

- **Peel (skin + eyes + crown base):** ~**30–35 %** of fruit weight
- **Core (central fibrous cylinder):** ~**5–10 %**
- **Crown leaves: ~5–8 %**
- **Stem/leaves (field residue): ~15–25 %** (per hectare)
- **Juice clarification sludge (after centrifugation): ~2–5 %**

### 2.2.2 Proximate & Phytochemical Composition

| Component | Peel | Core | Leaves |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture | **80–85 %** | **75–80 %** | **70–75 %** |
| Total sugars (sucrose, glucose, fructose) | **10–15 %** | **8–12 %** | **5–8 %** |
| Crude fibre (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin) | **15–25 %** | **10–18 %** | **25–35 %** |
| Bromelain (proteolytic enzyme) | **200–500 mg/kg** | **100–250 mg/kg** | trace |
| Vitamin C | **20–50 mg/100 g** | **15–30 mg/100 g** | trace |
| Organic acids (citric, malic) | **0.5–1.5 %** | **0.4–0.8 %** | trace |
| Pectin | **1–3 %** | **0.5–1.5 %** | trace |
| Ash | **0.5–1.0 %** | **0.3–0.7 %** | **1.5–3.0 %** |

### 2.2.3 Bromelain (EC 3.4.22.32 / EC 3.4.22.33)
A cysteine protease with broad substrate specificity:
- **Optimal pH:** 6.0–8.0 (broad)
- **Optimal temperature:** 50–65 °C
- **Molecular weight:** ~**23–28 kDa**
- **Activity units:** One Bromelain Tyrosine Unit (BTU) = amount of enzyme liberating 1 µg tyrosine/min from casein at pH 7.0, 35 °C.

**Extraction process:**
1. Crushing/peeling of peel + core.
2. Juice extraction by pressing / centrifugation.
3. **Precipitation** by acetone, ethanol, or ammonium sulphate.
4. **Dialysis and lyophilization** (freeze drying).
5. Standardization to **1200 BTU/mg** or **2400 GDU/g** (gelatin digestion units).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bromelain is a **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** enzyme used as a meat tenderizer, anti-inflammatory agent (post-surgical, sinusitis, osteoarthritis), digestive aid, anti-edema drug, and in cosmetic skin-debriding formulations.

### 2.2.4 Pineapple Dietary Fibre (PDF)
Extracted peel/core residue after juice and bromelain recovery is:
- Washed, bleached (optional H₂O₂ 1–3 %),
- Dried, milled, sieved to **< 80 mesh**,
- Characterized by **WHC (water holding capacity): 4–8 g/g** and **OHC (oil holding capacity): 2–4 g/g**.
- Used as a **functional food ingredient** in bakery, extruded snacks, and low-calorie/high-fibre formulations.

### 2.2.5 Other Valorization Routes

- **Pectin extraction** — peel → acid hydrolysis (pH 1.5–2.0, 80–90 °C, 60–90 min) → alcohol precipitation → yields ~**2–4 %** of fresh peel.
- **Vinegar / wine** — fermentation of sugar-rich juice using *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (ethanol) followed by *Acetobacter aceti* (acetic acid).
- **Biogas (anaerobic digestion):** 1 tonne fresh pineapple waste → ~**40–60 m³ biogas** (≈ **65–70 % CH₄**).
- **Composting / vermicomposting** — using *Eisenia foetida*.
- **Bioethanol** — enzymatic hydrolysis (cellulase + pectinase) + fermentation; theoretical yield ~**50–80 L/tonne** fresh waste.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick-Reference Table

| # | Concept / Formula | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Yield of kernel oil (solvent) | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{W_{oil}}{W_{kernel,dry}} \times 100$ | % w/w; $W_{oil}$ = mass of oil, $W_{kernel,dry}$ = dry kernel mass |
| 2 | Acid value (AV) of kernel fat | $AV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{KOH} \times N}{W_{sample}}$ | mg KOH/g oil; $V_{KOH}$ = titrant vol (mL), $N$ = normality |
| 3 | Saponification value (SV) | $SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times (V_{blank}-V_{sample}) \times N}{W}$ | mg KOH/g |
| 4 | Iodine value (IV) | $IV = \dfrac{12.69 \times (V_{blank}-V_{sample}) \times N}{W}$ | g I₂/100 g |
| 5 | Bromelain activity (BTU) | $\text{BTU/mg} = \dfrac{(A_{sample} - A_{blank}) \times V_{dilution} \times 1000}{t \times m_{enzyme}}$ | U/mg; $A$ = absorbance at 280 nm (Folin-Ciocalteu tyrosine) |
| 6 | Theoretical biogas yield (Buswell equation) | $C_{n}H_{a}O_{b} + \bigl(n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2}\bigr) H_{2}O \rightarrow \bigl(\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4}\bigr) CH_{4} + \bigl(\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4}\bigr) CO_{2}$ | Stoichiometric, basis: carbohydrate $C_6 H_{10} O_5$ |
| 7 | Methane yield (Carbohydrate) | $C_6H_{10}O_5 + H_2O \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ | 100 g cellulose → 50 g CH₄ (~ 0.415 m³ at STP) |
| 8 | Theoretical CH₄ from pineapple peel | $V_{CH_4} = Y_{CH_4} \times m_{VS} \times 0.415$ | m³, $Y_{CH_4}$ = 0.40–0.50; $m_{VS}$ = volatile solids (kg) |
| 9 | Pectin yield (acid extraction) | $P_{yield} = \dfrac{W_{pectin,dry}}{W_{peel,fresh}} \times 100$ | % w/w |
| 10 | Degree of esterification (DE) of pectin | $DE = \dfrac{moles\, of\, esterified\, -COOH}{total\, -COOH} \times 100$ | High methoxyl pectin: DE > 50 %; Low methoxyl: DE < 50 % |
| 11 | Mango kernel starch gelatinization enthalpy | $\Delta H_{gel} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p \, dT$ | J/g; measured by DSC (typical 10–15 J/g) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always substitute absolute-value notation with **\vert … \vert** in exam scripts. Many KTU students lose 1–2 marks because the markdown table-vertical-pipe conflict forces ambiguous rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cosmetic industry:** Mango butter is a direct substitute for shea/cocoa butter in lip balms, body lotions, hair conditioners, and soap bases (L'Oréal, The Body Shop commercialize it).
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Bromelain tablets (Bromelain-POS, Traumanase, Phlogenzym) are prescribed for post-operative inflammation, sinusitis, and osteoarthritis.
- **Food industry:** Mango kernel starch is approved by FSSAI (India) and FDA (US, GRN No. 000383) as a food ingredient.
- **Bioenergy:** A 100 TPD pineapple cannery can offset ~**40 %** of its thermal energy demand by anaerobic digestion of its waste stream with biomethane capture.
- **Circular bioeconomy:** Integrated mango + pineapple biorefinery models convert **100 %** of the harvested biomass into edible oils, enzymes, fibres, bioactive extracts, and renewable energy — achieving **zero waste**.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Methane Yield from Pineapple Peel (Buswell's Equation)

### 3.1.1 Generic Buswell Equation

The generalized stoichiometric equation for anaerobic digestion of an organic substrate $C_n H_a O_b$ is:

$$
C_{n}H_{a}O_{b} + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right) H_{2}O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right) CH_{4} + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right) CO_{2}
$$

### 3.1.2 Application to Cellulose ($C_6 H_{10} O_5$)

**Step 1 —** Substitute $n=6$, $a=10$, $b=5$:

$$
\text{Stoichiometric water term} = 6 - \frac{10}{4} - \frac{5}{2} = 6 - 2.5 - 2.5 = 1.0
$$

**Step 2 —** Methane coefficient:

$$
CH_4 = \frac{6}{2} - \frac{10}{8} + \frac{5}{4} = 3 - 1.25 + 1.25 = 3.0
$$

**Step 3 —** CO₂ coefficient:

$$
CO_2 = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{10}{8} - \frac{5}{4} = 3 + 1.25 - 1.25 = 3.0
$$

**Step 4 —** Final balanced reaction:

$$
C_{6}H_{10}O_{5} + H_{2}O \rightarrow 3\, CH_{4} + 3\, CO_{2}
$$

### 3.1.3 Mass-to-Volume Conversion

Molecular weight of cellulose = $6(12) + 10(1) + 5(16) = 162$ g/mol.
Molecular weight of CH₄ = 16 g/mol.

$$
\text{Mass of CH}_{4} \text{ per 162 g cellulose} = 3 \times 16 = 48 \text{ g CH}_{4}
$$

$$
\text{Volume at STP} = \frac{48}{16} \times 22.4 = 3 \times 22.4 = 67.2 \text{ L CH}_{4}
$$

### 3.1.4 Practical Yield with Correction Factor

Real digesters achieve only **85–95 %** of theoretical (due to cell-wall recalcitrance and microbial losses). Apply correction $Y_{corr} = 0.85$:

$$
V_{CH_4,\,practical} = 0.85 \times 67.2 \approx 57.1 \text{ L CH}_{4} \text{ per 162 g cellulose}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow V_{CH_4} \approx 0.352 \text{ m}^{3} \text{ per kg cellulose} \text{ at STP}
$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Example

A pineapple cannery generates **10 tonnes/day** of peel with composition: moisture 82 %, cellulose 12 %, hemicellulose 6 %, lignin 4 %, ash 2 % (wet basis).

**Step 1 —** Dry weight: $10\,000 \times (1 - 0.82) = 1\,800$ kg dry matter.

**Step 2 —** Biodegradable fraction (cellulose + hemicellulose) = $1\,800 \times 0.85 = 1\,530$ kg (lignin largely recalcitrant in short retention times).

**Step 3 —** Equivalent cellulose mass (hemicellulose factor 0.90):

$$
m_{VS,\,eff} = (0.12 \times 1.8 \times 1000) + (0.06 \times 1.8 \times 1000 \times 0.90) \approx 216 + 97.2 = 313.2 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 —** Theoretical CH₄ volume (assuming all as cellulose equivalent):

$$
V_{CH_4} = 313.2 \times 0.352 \approx 110.3 \text{ m}^{3} \text{ CH}_{4} / \text{day}
$$

**Step 5 —** Energy equivalent (CH₄ calorific value 35.8 MJ/m³):

$$
E = 110.3 \times 35.8 \approx 3\,949 \text{ MJ/day} \approx 1\,097 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

This offsets a meaningful fraction of the cannery's thermal energy demand and motivates commercial-scale anaerobic digestion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bromelain Activity Calculation (BTU/mg)

The Folin-Ciocalteu (Lowry method) based BTU activity is computed as:

$$
\text{BTU/mg} = \frac{(A_{sample} - A_{blank}) \times V_{dilution} \times 1000}{t \times m_{enzyme} \times \varepsilon_{tyr}}
$$

where $\varepsilon_{tyr}$ = extinction coefficient of tyrosine at 660 nm (with Folin reagent) ≈ **0.0224 absorbance units per µg tyrosine**.

**Worked Example:**
A pineapple peel extract (2.5 mL) reacts with 5 mL casein substrate (1 %) at pH 7.0, 35 °C, for 10 min. Reaction stopped with TCA. Filtrate absorbance at 660 nm = 0.480. Blank = 0.020. Dilution factor = 10. Mass of enzyme in 2.5 mL = 0.50 mg.

**Step 1 —** Net absorbance:

$$
\Delta A = 0.480 - 0.020 = 0.460
$$

**Step 2 —** Tyrosine equivalent:

$$
\mu g\, Tyr = \frac{0.460}{0.0224} \approx 20.54\, \mu g
$$

**Step 3 —** Activity:

$$
\text{BTU/mg} = \frac{20.54 \times 10 \times 1000}{10 \times 0.50} \approx 4\,108\, \text{BTU/mg (unrealistic; demonstrates raw calculation; corrected using standard calibration)}
$$

In industrial practice, the result is reported in **GDU/g** (Gelatin Digestion Units) or **CDU/mg** (Casein Digestion Units) and benchmarked against commercial bromelain standards (**1200–2400 GDU/g**).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Mango Kernel vs Pineapple Waste

| Aspect | Mango Seed Kernel | Pineapple Waste |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary biorefinery product** | Mango butter (lipid) | Bromelain (enzyme) |
| **Secondary product** | Starch, polyphenols (mangiferin) | Dietary fibre, pectin |
| **Tertiary product** | Defatted meal (animal feed) | Biogas, compost, bioethanol |
| **Extraction technology** | Solvent / mechanical / supercritical CO₂ | Aqueous extraction + acetone precipitation |
| **Key processing temperature** | 50–80 °C (oil extraction); 60–80 °C (starch gelatinization) | 4 °C (cold extraction); 50–65 °C (activity optimum) |
| **End-use sectors** | Cosmetics, food, nutraceuticals | Pharmaceuticals, food, textiles, cosmetics |
| **Estimated market value** | USD 8–15/kg (butter); USD 2–5/kg (starch) | USD 100–500/kg (pure bromelain); USD 5–15/kg (food-grade) |
| **Environmental impact if discarded** | High BOD/COD, methane, pest attraction | High BOD/COD, landfill leachate |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mango Seed Kernel Biorefinery — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mango Pulp Canning Industry] --> B[Decortication Unit]
    B --> C[Fibrous Husk]
    B --> D[Mango Seed Kernel]
    C --> C1[Boiler Fuel / Compost]
    D --> E[Drying at 50-60 C, Moisture < 8 percent]
    E --> F[Size Reduction Hammer Mill]
    F --> G[Solvent Extraction n-Hexane 60 C]
    G --> G1[Crude Mango Butter]
    G --> G2[Defatted Kernel Meal]
    G1 --> H1[Refining Degumming Bleaching Deodorization]
    H1 --> I1[Refined Mango Butter Cosmetic Food Grade]
    I1 --> J1[Cocoa Butter Equivalent Chocolates]
    I1 --> J2[Lotions Lip Balms Soaps]
    G2 --> K1[Starch Extraction Wet Milling]
    K1 --> L1[Mango Kernel Starch]
    L1 --> M1[Biodegradable Films]
    L1 --> M2[Thickener in Sauces Baby Foods]
    G2 --> K2[Polyphenol Extraction Ethanol 70 percent]
    K2 --> L2[Mangiferin Concentrate]
    L2 --> M3[Nutraceuticals Antioxidant Capsules]
```

## 4.2 Pineapple Waste Biorefinery — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pineapple Cannery Fruit Reception] --> B[Peeling Coring Crown Trimming]
    B --> C[Edible Fruit Slices for Canning]
    B --> D[Waste Stream Peel Core Crown]
    D --> E[Crushing and Pressing]
    E --> F[Pineapple Juice Press Liquid]
    E --> G[Pressed Solid Residue]
    F --> H1[Centrifugation Clarification]
    H1 --> I1[Crude Bromelain Extract]
    I1 --> J1[Acetone Fractionation]
    J1 --> K1[Precipitated Bromelain Pellet]
    K1 --> L1[Lyophilization Freeze Drying]
    L1 --> M1[Standardized Bromelain Powder 1200 BTU/mg]
    M1 --> N1[Pharmaceutical Anti-inflammatory]
    M1 --> N2[Food Meat Tenderizer]
    M1 --> N3[Cosmetic Skin Debridement]
    G --> P1[Acid Hydrolysis pH 1.8 85 C 60 min]
    P1 --> Q1[Pectin Precipitation Ethanol 95 percent]
    Q1 --> R1[Dried Pectin Powder DE 60-70 percent]
    G --> P2[Anaerobic Digestion Mesophilic 35 C 25 d HRT]
    P2 --> Q2[Biogas 60 percent CH4]
    Q2 --> R2[Combined Heat and Power CHP Boiler]
    G --> P3[Detergent Washing Bleaching Drying Milling]
    P3 --> Q3[Pineapple Dietary Fibre Powder]
    Q3 --> R3[Bakery Extruded Snacks Function]
    F --> S1[Sugar Fermentation Saccharomyces cerevisiae]
    S1 --> S2[Wine Vinegar via Acetobacter aceti]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow: Pre-Valorization vs Post-Valorization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conventional Disposal Landfill Composting Loss of Embedded Value] --> B[Pollution BOD COD Methane Emission]
    C[Biorefinery Valorization Multi Product Recovery] --> D[Economic Revenue Streams]
    C --> E[Environmental Compliance GHG Reduction]
    C --> F[Zero Waste Circular Economy SDG Alignment]
    D --> G[Industry Sustainability Profitability]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
List the major by-products of the **pineapple canning industry** and state **two** high-value compounds that can be recovered from them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Major by-products: **peel, core, crown leaves, stem, juice clarification sludge** (1½ marks).
- Two high-value compounds: **(a) Bromelain** — proteolytic enzyme, 200–500 mg/kg in peel, used as anti-inflammatory drug and meat tenderizer; **(b) Pectin** — 1–3 % of fresh peel, used as gelling agent in jams and pharmaceutical matrix. (1½ marks)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Define **mango butter**. State its **two** most significant industrial applications and give the **melting point range**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Mango butter is the refined fat extracted from the dried kernel (*cotyledon*) of the mango seed (*Mangifera indica*), typically via solvent (hexane) or screw-press extraction. (1 mark)
- Two applications: **(i)** cocoa-butter equivalent (CBE) in chocolate and confectionery; **(ii)** emollient base in cosmetics (lip balms, body butters, hair conditioners, soaps). (1½ marks)
- Melting point: **32–38 °C** (close to body temperature). (½ mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Mango Seed Kernel Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **composition** of dried mango seed kernel and explain with a flow diagram the **extraction and refining of mango butter** for cosmetic-grade application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the recovery of **mangiferin and mango kernel starch** as secondary valorization products, with their respective applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Composition + Extraction:**
1. **Composition of dried mango kernel** (½ mark):
   - Starch: **55–70 %**
   - Fat (mango butter): **8–15 %**
   - Protein: **6–10 %**
   - Fibre: **2–4 %**
   - Polyphenols (mangiferin): **1–3 %**
   - Ash: **2–4 %**
2. **Pre-treatment** (½ mark): Kernel decorticated from stone, sun-dried to moisture < 8 %, hammer-milled to 30–60 mesh.
3. **Solvent extraction** (1 mark): Soxhlet / continuous extractor with food-grade n-hexane at 55–65 °C for 6–8 h. Solvent-to-meal ratio 4:1 to 6:1 (v/w). Recovery of miscella → distillation under vacuum.
4. **Crude oil refining steps** (2 marks):
   - Degumming with hot water (60 °C) or phosphoric acid (0.05–0.1 %).
   - Neutralization with NaOH (12–18°Bé) to remove free fatty acids.
   - Bleaching with activated earth (1–2 %) under vacuum at 90–110 °C.
   - Deodorization with steam at 220–240 °C under 2–6 mbar vacuum.
5. **Quality criteria for cosmetic grade** (1 mark): Acid value < 2 mg KOH/g; peroxide value < 5 meq/kg; colour: white to pale ivory; odour: bland; melting point 32–38 °C; iodine value 35–55 g I₂/100 g.
6. **Flow diagram** (2 marks): see Mermaid block in Section 4.1.

**(b) Mangiferin & Starch Recovery:**
1. **Starch extraction** (2 marks):
   - Defatted kernel meal → steeping in 0.1 % NaOH at 50 °C for 12 h → wet milling → filtration through 200-mesh sieve → centrifugation (3000 rpm, 15 min) → washing → drying at 40 °C → pulverization.
   - Starch yield: **40–55 %** of kernel dry weight.
   - Characterization: granule size 5–25 µm; amylose 20–35 %; gelatinization temp 65–78 °C.
   - **Applications**: biodegradable packaging films, baby food thickener, pharmaceutical tablet disintegrant, low-glycemic-index noodles.
2. **Mangiferin recovery** (2 marks):
   - Defatted meal (or whole kernel powder) → solvent extraction with **70 % ethanol** at 50 °C for 3 h, solid:liquid 1:10.
   - Filtration → rotary evaporation → aqueous concentrate.
   - Purification via **macroporous resin (e.g., AB-8)** column → ethanol gradient elution.
   - Crystallization / freeze-drying → yellow-brown powder, **HPLC purity > 90 %**.
3. **Applications of mangiferin** (2 marks):
   - Nutraceutical capsules: antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic.
   - Cosmeceutical serums: anti-aging, UV-protection.
   - Functional food additive: natural antioxidant (E-number equivalent, though not yet assigned).
   - Pharmaceutical research: candidate molecule for neuroprotection (Alzheimer's models).
4. **Defatted meal** (1 mark) by-product → protein concentrate / animal feed supplement (~20–25 % protein, balanced amino acid profile, rich in essential amino acids like lysine, leucine).

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Pineapple Waste Focus

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **recovery of bromelain** from pineapple peel with a process flow diagram. Discuss the **key parameters** that affect its activity and yield. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the production of **pineapple dietary fibre (PDF)** and **biogas** from the residual solid waste after bromelain extraction. Include reaction stoichiometry and energy estimate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bromelain Recovery + Process Parameters:**
1. **Feedstock**: Fresh pineapple peel (Ananas comosus, *Smooth Cayenne* or *Mauritius* cultivar) (½ mark).
2. **Process flow** (3 marks):
   - **Crushing**: peel + core passed through hammer mill or paddle pulper; particle size 3–5 mm.
   - **Juice extraction**: screw press or basket centrifuge; yield ~70–80 % juice.
   - **Clarification**: centrifugation at 5000–7000 rpm, 4 °C, 20 min.
   - **Precipitation**: cold acetone (–20 °C) added drop-wise to clarified juice at 2:1 (v/v); alternatively, ammonium sulphate (40–70 % saturation) for salting out.
   - **Standing**: 4 °C, 12 h.
   - **Recovery**: centrifugation at 8000 rpm, 10 min → pellet.
   - **Purification**: re-dissolution in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) + dialysis (10 kDa cut-off) to remove salts.
   - **Lyophilization**: –40 °C, 24–48 h → free-flowing powder.
   - **Standardization**: activity assay using casein substrate (Folin-Ciocalteu method); standardized to **1200 BTU/mg** or **2400 GDU/g**.
3. **Key activity/yield parameters** (2 marks):
   - **pH**: 6.0–8.0 optimum (acidic or highly alkaline inactivates cysteine thiol).
   - **Temperature**: 50–65 °C optimum; >70 °C denatures.
   - **Time**: < 30 min contact with substrate avoids autolysis.
   - **Activators**: cysteine, EDTA, ascorbate (1–5 mM).
   - **Inhibitors**: heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Ag⁺), iodoacetate, TLCK.
   - **Cultivar and maturity**: *Mauritius* > *Kew* > *Smooth Cayenne*; mid-ripe fruit gives highest yield.
4. **Industrial applications** (1½ marks):
   - Pharmaceutical: anti-inflammatory tablets (Bromelain-POS 500 mg), sinusitis treatment, post-surgical oedema.
   - Food: meat tenderizer (sprinkle on cuts 30 min before cooking).
   - Cosmetic: exfoliant face-mask enzyme, skin debridement.
   - Brewing: chill-proofing of beer.

**(b) Pineapple Dietary Fibre + Biogas Production:**
1. **Pineapple dietary fibre (PDF)** (2 marks):
   - Residual solid (after juice + bromelain extraction) → washing (2–3 cycles) → detergent washing (Na₄P₂O₇ 0.5 %, 60 °C, 30 min) → bleaching (H₂O₂ 2 %, pH 9, 70 °C, 60 min) → water rinse → drying (60 °C, 12 h) → milling → sieving through 80 mesh.
   - Yield: **15–25 %** of fresh peel.
   - **Characterization**: WHC 4–8 g/g; OHC 2–4 g/g; TDF (total dietary fibre) > 70 %.
   - **Applications**: fortification in bread, biscuits, extruded snacks; partial flour replacement (10–20 %) for calorie reduction.
2. **Biogas via anaerobic digestion** (3 marks):
   - **Feedstock**: residual lignocellulosic fraction or whole waste stream.
   - **Reactor**: Continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) or plug-flow; mesophilic (35 °C); HRT 20–30 days.
   - **Reaction stoichiometry** (Buswell):
     $$C_{6}H_{10}O_{5} + H_{2}O \rightarrow 3\,CH_{4} + 3\,CO_{2}$$
   - **Methane yield**: 0.40–0.50 m³ CH₄ / kg volatile solids added.
3. **Numerical estimate for 1 tonne fresh peel** (1½ marks):
   - Dry matter: $1000 \times 0.18 = 180$ kg.
   - Volatile solids: $180 \times 0.94 = 169.2$ kg.
   - Practical CH₄ yield: $169.2 \times 0.45 = 76.1$ m³ CH₄.
   - Energy content: $76.1 \times 35.8 \approx 2\,724$ MJ ≈ **757 kWh**.
4. **Process integration & circularity** (½ mark): bromelain → fibre → biogas cascade ensures **100 %** waste utilization, achieving zero-discharge and generating three revenue streams.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing *seed* with *kernel*.** The mango *stone* (endocarp) is the woody shell; the *kernel* (cotyledon) is the inner edible/extractable part. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these terms are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Reporting bromelain in wrong units.** Students often quote "U/mg" or "% activity" without specifying **BTU/mg** or **GDU/g**. Always state the substrate and reaction conditions for the unit.
> 3. **Omitting process flow diagrams.** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B answers MUST include at least one labelled flow diagram — a written answer alone is capped at 80 % of marks for "process" questions.
> 4. **Forgetting the Buswell stoichiometry.** When asked about biogas yield, merely stating "biogas is produced" earns ≤ 2 of the 7 marks. The balanced reaction $C_6 H_{10} O_5 + H_2 O \rightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$ is mandatory.
> 5. **Mixing up mango butter with mango pulp.** Mango butter is **kernel fat**, not the fruit pulp lipid fraction. Examiner explicitly checks this distinction.
> 6. **Neglecting the mg/kg vs % w/w distinction** when quoting bromelain content. Use consistent units throughout the answer.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🍃 **Mango kernel** = **15–20 %** of fruit weight, contains **55–70 % starch**, **8–15 % fat (mango butter)**, **6–10 % protein**, and bioactive **mangiferin (xanthone)**.
- 🍫 **Mango butter** melting point **32–38 °C**; rich in **oleic (~45 %)** and **stearic (~40 %)** acids → **cocoa-butter equivalent (CBE)**.
- 🌿 **Mangiferin** concentration **0.2–0.9 %** (DW); recovered by **70 % ethanol extraction + resin purification**; antioxidant + anti-inflammatory.
- 🍍 **Pineapple waste** = **40–55 %** of fruit (peel + core + crown + leaves); moisture **75–85 %**.
- 🧪 **Bromelain** is a **cysteine protease**, M.W. **23–28 kDa**, optimum **pH 6–8**, **T = 50–65 °C**, activity expressed as **BTU/mg or GDU/g**.
- 🌾 **Pineapple dietary fibre (PDF)** after juice/bromelain removal: WHC **4–8 g/g**, OHC **2–4 g/g**, used in bakery and extruded products.
- 💨 **Biogas via Buswell equation**: $C_6 H_{10} O_5 + H_2 O \rightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$; ~**0.40–0.50 m³ CH₄/kg VS**; energy ≈ **36 MJ/m³ CH₄**.
- 🧴 **End-uses**: Cosmetics (mango butter, bromelain), Pharmaceuticals (bromelain tablets, mangiferin), Food (kernel starch, PDF, vinegar), Energy (biogas CHP).
- ♻️ **Circular bioeconomy principle**: cascade valorization — primary product (oil/enzyme) → secondary (starch/fibre) → tertiary (biogas/compost) → **zero waste**.
- 📊 **KTU exam tips**: include labelled flow diagrams, state units, give balanced stoichiometry, distinguish seed vs kernel, mention cultivar effects.
